summary: The systematic approach scientists use to understand the world through asking questions, designing experiments, making observations, and drawing conclusions. This process involves identifying what can be changed or controlled in an experiment and what can be observed or measured to understand phenomena. Investigation works best when combining careful observation with creative thinking.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: Science begins with wonder and asking 'Why?' and 'How?' questions about the world. Scientific questions can range from everyday observations (like why one side of a puri is thinner) to bigger mysteries (like is the world getting warmer). In Grade 8, students learn to ask more focused questions that can be investigated through simple experiments, moving beyond simple curiosity to systematic inquiry.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The process of designing and conducting experiments to answer scientific questions. Investigation means more than just looking at something—it involves asking focused questions, designing ways to do simple experiments, and using observations to improve understanding. Scientists identify what can be changed or controlled and what can be observed or measured. This systematic approach applies to all scientific experiments, from simple to complicated.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

aliases: Changing One Thing at a Time
summary: The practice of changing only one factor at a time while keeping other conditions the same to understand its effect. For example, when testing the effect of oil temperature on puri puffing, use dough circles of the same thickness and drop them in the same way, changing only the temperature. This allows clear understanding of cause and effect. Variables that can be controlled include thickness, size, type of material, temperature, and method of testing.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The process of carefully watching and recording what happens during experiments. Some observations have yes/no answers (does the puri puff up?), while others involve measuring numbers (time taken to puff up in seconds). It is important to keep notes of everything seen and sensed during an experiment—including whether oil splatters, smells, or smokes. After one round of experiments, observations may lead to more questions for further investigation.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The hidden world of tiny organisms unseen by naked eye but deeply linked to humans. Microorganisms exist in a single drop of water. Some are invisible helpers that aid in digesting food or producing medicines, while others can be harmful and cause infections. Understanding these organisms helps explain health and disease.
diagram_description: Illustration showing plant with roots underground, magnifying glass examining parts, with scientific instruments like thermometer and spring balance in soil representing investigation
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: What the body needs to stay healthy and fight infections. Nutritious food, exercise, medicines, and vaccines help maintain health and combat disease-causing microorganisms. This represents how science plays a major role in improving our lives by understanding and supporting the body's natural defenses.
diagram_description: Image of healthcare worker administering vaccine to a child wearing mask
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The different ways electric current manifests and performs useful work in daily life. Electric current has multiple effects that are harnessed in various applications—heating effect keeps us warm while magnetic effect helps motors run and machines function. Understanding these effects shows how science improves our lives through practical applications.
diagram_description: Images of electric heater and electric iron showing heating applications
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The phenomenon where electric current produces heat when passing through conductors. This effect is used to keep us warm through devices like room heaters and electric irons. It is a practical application of electricity that makes daily life easier and more comfortable.
diagram_description: Electric heater showing heating coils
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The phenomenon where electric current produces magnetic fields that can cause mechanical motion. This effect is crucial for motors to run and machines to function. It enables the conversion of electrical energy into mechanical work, powering countless devices in modern life.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The fundamental physical interactions that cause objects to change motion or shape. Forces make objects speed up, slow down, or change direction—explaining why a thrown ball falls back to the ground or why a car stops when brakes are applied. Understanding forces is essential to explain motion and mechanical phenomena in daily life and nature.
diagram_description: Person throwing and catching a ball, illustrating force and motion
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How forces cause objects to change their state of motion. Forces make objects speed up, slow down, or change direction. Examples include a ball thrown upward falling back to the ground due to gravitational force, or a car stopping when brakes apply friction force. Understanding forces explains everyday motion phenomena.
diagram_description: Ball being thrown up and falling down, person catching ball
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How force is distributed over an object's surface. Pressure describes the concentration of force per unit area. The same force concepts that explain solid object interactions also explain how air moves—small pressure differences result in gentle breezes, while stronger pressure differences lead to strong winds and sometimes even cyclones. Pressure is key to understanding both mechanical effects and weather phenomena.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: Powerful weather events caused by pressure differences in the atmosphere. Cyclones and storms result from strong pressure differences that drive powerful air movements. These phenomena affect daily lives, agriculture, and safety. Understanding the physics of force and pressure helps explain these dangerous natural events.
diagram_description: Coastal scene showing storm with lightning, strong winds affecting palm trees and sea waves, people taking shelter
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subtopic_names: States of Matter and Particle Motion
summary: The fundamental concept that everything around us is made of tiny particles. To understand phenomena like air pressure or water boiling temperature, we must examine what kind of particles materials are made of and how they move. The behavior of these particles explains the properties of solids, liquids, and gases, as well as how materials can be classified and combined.
diagram_description: Zoomed-in views showing particle arrangement in fabric (solid), water (liquid), and balloon (gas)
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

concept_name: States of Matter and Particle Motion
summary: How particle arrangement and movement determine the state of matter. In solids, particles are tightly packed and cannot move much, giving solids fixed shape. In liquids, particles can move more freely but remain close, allowing liquids to flow while maintaining volume. In gases, particles move around freely with large spaces between them, allowing gases to fill any container. This particle behavior explains properties like air pressure and boiling temperature.
diagram_description: Magnified views showing particle arrangement: tightly packed in fabric (solid), loosely arranged in water (liquid), widely spaced in balloon (gas)
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: Categorizing materials based on their composition and how particles combine. Elements are pure substances made of one type of particle. Compounds are formed when two or more elements bond together chemically. Mixtures are combinations of substances that can be separated physically. This classification is fundamental to understanding chemistry and helps explain how substances interact and transform.
frequently_confused_with: States of Matter and Particle Motion
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How substances mix at the particle level to form homogeneous mixtures. Once we understand how particles combine or mix, we can understand solutions—for example, how sugar dissolves in tea to make it sweet. A solution forms when one substance (solute) disperses evenly throughout another substance (solvent) at the particle level, creating a uniform mixture.
diagram_description: Glass of tea with magnified view showing sugar and water particles mixed together forming a solution
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How light interacts with different surfaces and materials. Light rays can reflect off flat and curved mirrors, and bend when passing through lenses. Understanding these behaviors helps explain the working of many objects around us, from mirrors to corrective glasses. Both smooth and rough surfaces reflect light, though in different ways.
diagram_description: Images showing light reflection in spoon, light through lens, and various optical instruments
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How light bounces off surfaces to form images. Light reflects off flat and curved mirrors, creating images that can be magnified, reduced, or distorted. Reflection occurs from both polished surfaces like mirrors and rough surfaces. The Moon also reflects sunlight. Understanding reflection explains what happens when we see an image in a shiny metal spoon.
diagram_description: Shiny metal spoon showing distorted reflection, illustrating curved mirror reflection
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How light changes direction when passing from one material to another. Light bends when passing through lenses and transparent materials. The bending of light (refraction) explains how corrective glasses help many people see clearly. Lenses use refraction to focus or spread light, forming clear images. This principle is applied in eyeglasses, cameras, and microscopes.
diagram_description: Light rays bending through a lens, eyeglasses illustration
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The changing appearance of the Moon due to its position relative to Earth and Sun. The Moon reflects sunlight, and depending on the relative positions of Earth, Moon, and Sun, a slightly different part of the Moon is illuminated each night. This gives rise to the beautiful phases of the Moon—from new moon to full moon (purnima) and back. The phases form a periodic cycle that repeats approximately every month.
diagram_description: Series of moon phase images showing progression from new moon through crescents, half moon, gibbous to full moon and back
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How careful observation of celestial objects led to the development of time-keeping systems. Watching the periodic cycles of the Moon's phases allowed humans to create the first calendars. By combining careful observations of sunrises, sunsets, and lunar cycles, various calendar systems came into being. It is fascinating that calendars which determine our routines on Earth are linked to the motions of objects far beyond our planet. This shows the connection between astronomy and daily life.
diagram_description: Calendar page showing 'Chaitra' month (March 22 - April 20, 2025) with dates in both English and Hindi script
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The complex patterns of relationships between living organisms and their environments. Every living being—from the tiniest insect to the largest whale, from blades of grass to tall trees—depends on and responds to air, water, sunlight, and other organisms around them. These interactions form ecosystems that support life on our planet. Understanding these relationships is crucial to recognizing how different parts of nature are connected.
diagram_description: Two ecosystem scenes: aquatic ecosystem with pond plants and underwater life; terrestrial ecosystem with tree, birds, deer, and rabbits showing food web relationships
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: Understanding what makes Earth suitable for life and the challenges it faces. Earth lies at the perfect distance from the Sun where water remains liquid, and has an atmosphere providing oxygen while shielding from harmful ultraviolet rays. However, human activities can cause changes in Earth's temperature, disrupting climate patterns with dangerous consequences. Humans are both the cause of and potential solution to these problems, using scientific principles of observation, measurement, and experimentation to understand and protect the delicate balance on which life depends.
diagram_description: Two contrasting ecosystem scenes showing healthy ecosystems with diverse life
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: The special conditions that make Earth suitable for life. Earth lies at the perfect distance from the Sun where water can remain in liquid form—essential for life as we know it. Earth also has an atmosphere that provides the oxygen we breathe while shielding us from harmful ultraviolet rays from the Sun. These conditions together create an environment where life can thrive, making Earth unique among known planets.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

summary: How human actions affect Earth's climate systems and the urgent challenges facing our planet. Human activities can cause small changes in Earth's temperature, disrupting climate patterns with dangerous consequences. Humans are at the heart of both the problem and the solution—we influence Earth's climate, but we can also use science to understand these changes and guide our actions. Scientific principles of observing, measuring, and experimenting are key to protecting the delicate balance on which life depends.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subtopic_names: Variation in Shape and Structure of Cells
concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
summary: All living organisms are made up of cells, which are the basic structural and functional units of life. Cells were first observed using microscopes. A typical cell has three main parts: cell membrane (outer boundary), cytoplasm (jelly-like substance), and nucleus (control center). Some cells have an additional cell wall outside the membrane. The invention of the microscope opened the invisible world of tiny organisms.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

parent_concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
summary: In 1665, Robert Hooke published Micrographia showing detailed drawings of tiny things seen through a microscope. He observed a thin slice of cork and saw many small empty spaces resembling a honeycomb. He called each small space a 'cell' - the first time this word was used in science. Around the same time in the 1660s, Antonie van Leeuwenhoek made better lenses and was the first to clearly see and describe tiny living things like bacteria and blood cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.2 shows (a) Micrographia book cover, (b) Microscope of Robert Hooke, and (c) Cork cells as published in Micrographia showing honeycomb-like structure
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

parent_concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
summary: A typical cell has three basic parts: (1) Cell membrane - the outer porous layer that separates one cell from another, allows entry of essential materials and exit of waste; (2) Cytoplasm - the space between cell membrane and nucleus, contains other cell components and compounds like carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and mineral salts, where most life processes occur; (3) Nucleus - the round structure in the middle covered by a thin nuclear membrane, regulates all cell activities and growth.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.3(c) shows onion peel cells under microscope with labeled cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus. Fig. 2.4 shows human cheek cells with labeled cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus (no cell wall). Fig. 2.5 shows schematic drawings comparing (a) animal cell and (b) plant cell with labeled parts including nucleus, cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm, vacuole, chloroplast, and mitochondria
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

parent_concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
concept_name: Variation in Shape and Structure of Cells
summary: Cells vary greatly in shape, size, and structure depending on their function. Muscle cells are spindle-shaped, thin, and flexible to help in contraction and movement (e.g., in food pipe pushing food down, in stomach wall churning food). Nerve cells (neurons) are very long with branches to carry messages throughout the body and pass them quickly. Inner cheek cells are thin and flat to form protective lining.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.6 shows (a) spindle-shaped muscle cell and (b) long branched nerve cell in humans
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subtopic_names: Levels of Organisation in Living Organisms
summary: The body of a living organism is organised hierarchically from simple to complex. Multicellular organisms are those made up of many cells (like animals, plants, humans). Their life begins with a single cell - the egg - which divides repeatedly to form a complete organism. The levels of organisation help us understand how simple building blocks like cells come together to form complex living beings.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

concept_name: Levels of Organisation in Living Organisms
summary: The levels of organisation are: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ System → Organism. (1) Cell is the basic unit of life, like a brick is the basic unit of a wall; (2) Tissue is a group of similar cells performing similar functions; (3) Organ is formed when different tissues are organised together; (4) Organ system is formed when several organs work together to perform a major body function; (5) Organism is the complete living being made up of all organ systems working together.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.7 shows the levels: (a) Cell, (b) Tissue, (c) Organ (stomach), (d) Organ system (digestive system), (e) Organism (human body)
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Microorganisms are tiny living organisms that cannot be seen with the naked eye. They are made up of just one cell (unicellular) like bacteria and Amoeba, or very few cells (multicellular) like some fungi and algae. They are found everywhere - in water, soil, air, and even inside our bodies. They can be observed using a microscope.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Microorganisms found in pond water and soil suspension include: (1) Protozoa - single-celled moving organisms with irregular shape (e.g., Amoeba) or with specialized structures for movement (e.g., Paramecium); (2) Algae - single or multicellular, green due to chlorophyll, some move with specialized structures, spherical shape; (3) Fungi - branched filaments without chlorophyll, can be unicellular (yeast) or multicellular (mould), having sac-like or brush-like structures; (4) Bacteria -.
diagram_description: Tables 2.1 and 2.2 show diagrams and descriptions of microorganisms: Amoeba (protozoa), Paramecium (protozoa), Algae, Bread mould (fungi), Mould (fungi), and Bacteria with different shapes. Fig. 2.8 shows observation of soil suspension under microscope. Fig. 2.13 shows a bacterial cell with nucleoid region labeled
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Some microorganisms like fungi and bacteria act on plant and animal waste and slowly break it down into simpler, nutrient-rich substances through a process called decomposition. For example, fruit and vegetable peels mixed with soil and left for 2-3 weeks turn into dark-colored manure rich in nutrients that increases soil fertility. These microorganisms also decompose fallen leaves, decaying plants, and dead animal bodies. The nutrients go back to the soil and help plants grow better.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.10 shows recycling of nutrients by making manure in a container with layers
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Rhizobium bacteria live in swollen regions called root nodules on the roots of legumes such as beans, peas, and lentils. These bacteria trap nitrogen from the air and convert it into a form useful for plants. This helps plants grow better without chemical fertilizers and naturally increases nitrogen in the soil, keeping it healthy. That is why farmers grow legumes in rotation with other crops - to enrich soil nitrogen content for the next crop.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.12 shows root nodules of Cowpea plant which contain Rhizobium bacteria
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Microorganisms play important roles in food production: (1) Yeast - a unicellular fungus that grows well in warm conditions. When added to flour with sugar and warm water, yeast respires and breaks down food, releasing carbon dioxide gas that forms bubbles making dough soft and fluffy (used in breads, cakes). Yeast also produces a small amount of alcohol during this process. Lactobacillus bacteria help ferment batter for idli, dosa, and dough for bhatura.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.11 shows change in volume of dough: (a) Dough in bowl A with yeast (risen and fluffy), (b) Dough in bowl B without yeast (no change)
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Microalgae are microscopic plant-like organisms that live in water, soil, air, and even on trees. They make their own food using sunlight through photosynthesis and release oxygen - producing more than half of Earth's oxygen supply. They are rich in nutrients and serve as food for many aquatic animals. Some like Spirulina, Chlorella, and Diatoms are used by humans as health supplements and medicines.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

concept_name: Cell as the Basic Unit of Life
summary: The cell is considered the basic unit of life because the bodies of all living organisms are made up of cells. A single cell contains various components that help organisms perform various functions necessary for survival. Cells can work individually or cooperate with each other in multicellular organisms.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

parent_concept_name: Cell as the Basic Unit of Life
summary: Unicellular organisms are made up of just one cell (e.g., bacteria, protozoa like Amoeba, yeast fungus) and carry out all functions necessary for survival in that single cell. Multicellular organisms are made up of many cells (e.g., animals, plants, humans, mould fungus). Their life begins with a single cell called 'egg' which has the ability to divide repeatedly to form a complete organism. In multicellular organisms, cells carry out specialized functions individually but also cooperate with each other to increase the chance of survival.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: A lens is a curved piece of glass - thick in the middle and thin at the edge - shaped like a lentil seed, hence the name. It was discovered long ago that such curved glass could make small things look bigger. A magnifying glass uses a lens to enlarge the appearance of small objects. A round-bottom flask filled with water can act like a magnifying glass, making letters appear larger when viewed through it. Reading glasses also use lenses to help people see better.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.1 shows a round-bottom flask filled with water placed on an open book, demonstrating magnification effect
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: The microscope is a scientific tool that uses improved and more powerful lenses to help humans see what their eyes cannot. Its invention opened a fascinating hidden world filled with tiny living creatures. Simple microscopes magnify things 100 to 400 times, allowing observation of microorganisms. Robert Hooke's microscope made things look 200 to 300 times bigger.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.2(b) shows the Microscope of Robert Hooke used in 1665
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Viruses are microscopic and acellular (not made of cells). They multiply only when they enter a living cell. Viruses may infect plants, animals, or bacterial cells and may cause disease. They are different from other microorganisms because they reproduce only inside the host organism.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Ancient Indian texts, particularly the Vedas, have references to the word 'Krimi' which means different tiny entities including 'Drishya' (visible) and 'Adrishya' (invisible). Various Vedic texts mention their beneficial and harmful effects. The Atharvaveda also refers to 'Krimi'. This shows ancient awareness of microscopic organisms.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Many microorganisms like bacteria and fungi can live in an oxygen-free (anaerobic) environment. Some bacteria can decompose plant and animal waste or household wastewater. During this process, they release a mixture of gases called biogas, primarily composed of carbon dioxide and a high proportion of methane. This gas is used as a fuel source for cooking, heating, generating electricity, and even running vehicles.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

concept_name: Ananda Mohan Chakrabarty and Oil-eating Bacteria
summary: Ananda Mohan Chakrabarty (1938-2020) was a scientist who studied bacteria. In 1971, he developed a special bacterium that could break down oil spills, helping to clean the environment. His discovery received a patent in 1980. A patent is a copyright given to a person so that no one else can copy, use or sell his/her invention without permission.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: The yolk (yellow part) of an ostrich egg is a single cell - the largest known cell in the living world - measuring about 130 mm to 170 mm in diameter. The egg contains extra non-cellular material: a shell for protection and a white liquid that nourishes the cell during its continued development.
subject: biology
chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

summary: Health is more than just the absence of disease. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), health is defined as 'a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease.' A healthy person can perform various tasks efficiently, cope well in different situations, and adjust well with peer groups and society. Health depends on many factors including lifestyle (how we live) and environment (our surroundings). Mental health involves staying positive, managing stress, and having strong relationships.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.1 shows three overlapping circles labeled Mental, Physical, and Social representing the three aspects of health
subject: biology

summary: The World Health Organization (WHO) defines health as 'a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease.' This definition emphasizes that health is not just about not being sick, but includes feeling good physically, staying positive mentally, and having strong relationships socially.
subject: biology

summary: Health has three interconnected aspects: Physical well-being involves taking care of the body through proper nutrition, exercise, and rest. Mental well-being includes staying positive, managing stress and worry, and maintaining a calm mind. Social well-being involves spending time with friends and family, talking, laughing, having fun, and adjusting well with peer groups and society. All three aspects are equally important; even if we eat well and live in a clean place, we may not feel good if we are lonely or upset.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.1 shows three overlapping circles representing Physical, Mental, and Social aspects of health
subject: biology

summary: Health depends on many factors including lifestyle (how we live) and environment (our surroundings). Lifestyle factors include eating nutritious food, maintaining hygiene, exercising regularly, getting proper sleep, spending time with family and friends, and having a positive attitude. Environmental factors include clean air and water, living in a clean place, and the Air Quality Index (AQI) which helps us know how clean the air is. In cities, air pollution from vehicles and factories can cause problems like coughing or asthma.
subject: biology

summary: For staying healthy we must eat nutritious food, maintain hygiene, stay in a clean place, exercise regularly, get proper sleep, spend time with family and friends, and have a positive attitude. Healthy habits support both a healthy body and a healthy mind. Taking care of our body and mind is important through proper lifestyle choices and keeping our environment clean.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.2 shows various healthy practices: eating balanced diet, staying physically active, saying 'No' to smoking or alcohol, managing stress, and getting enough sleep
subject: biology

summary: To maintain a healthy lifestyle: (1) Eat a balanced diet with plenty of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. (2) Avoid processed, fatty, or sugary food and drinks. (3) Stay physically active by playing outdoors, walking, running, cycling, or exercising. (4) Limit screen time and spend more time in nature.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.2 illustrates healthy lifestyle practices including eating balanced diet, staying active, avoiding smoking/alcohol, managing stress, and getting adequate sleep
subject: biology

summary: In addition to inculcating good habits and adopting a healthy lifestyle, we must keep ourselves and our surroundings clean. Clean air and water are important for our health. In cities, air pollution from vehicles and factories can cause problems like coughing or asthma. The Air Quality Index (AQI) helps us know how clean the air is.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.3 shows two playgrounds: (a) a clean, well-maintained playground and (b) a polluted, dirty playground with flies and mosquitos
subject: biology

aliases: How We Know We Are Unwell
summary: Our body usually works in a certain way to keep us healthy. When we feel unwell, it means something inside us may not be working as it should. We may have symptoms and signs that indicate we are unwell. A symptom is what we feel (like pain, tiredness, or dizziness), while a sign is something that can be seen or measured (like high body temperature when we have fever, rash, high blood pressure, or swelling). These help doctors understand what might be making us unwell.
subject: biology

summary: A symptom is what we feel internally, such as pain, tiredness, or dizziness. A sign is something that can be seen, observed, or measured by others, such as high body temperature when we have fever, rash on the skin, high blood pressure, or swelling. The key difference is that symptoms are subjective experiences felt by the patient, while signs are objective indicators that can be detected or measured by doctors. Both symptoms and signs help doctors understand what might be making us unwell.
subject: biology

summary: A disease is a condition that affects the normal working of the body or mind. It can happen when one or more organs or organ systems stop functioning properly. Diseases can be grouped into two major types based on their causes and how they spread: (1) Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens (disease-causing organisms like bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms, or protozoa) and can spread from one person to another. Examples include typhoid, dengue, flu, chickenpox, and COVID-19.
subject: biology

summary: Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens (disease-causing organisms) and can spread from one person to another. Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms, and protozoa (single-celled organisms). These pathogens can enter our body through the air we breathe, by consuming contaminated food or water, through direct contact like shaking hands, or indirectly by sharing personal items of an infected person. Some pathogens are spread by insects like mosquitos and houseflies, which are called vectors.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.4 shows common methods of disease transmission: by air, direct contact, indirect contact, by food, through mosquitos/insects, and from rabid animals between infected person and healthy person
subject: biology

summary: Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from one person to another. They are usually linked to lifestyle, diet, and/or environment. Examples include cancer, diabetes, asthma, high blood pressure, and obesity. Deficiency diseases like scurvy, anaemia, and goitre (caused by lack of specific nutrients in the diet) are also non-communicable.
subject: biology

summary: Common communicable diseases in India can be grouped by their mode of transmission: diseases spread through the air, through contaminated water and food, by insects (vectors), and through parasitic worms. Each disease has specific causal agents (viruses, bacteria, protozoa, or worms), affects particular sites in the body, presents characteristic symptoms, and requires specific preventive measures. Understanding these details helps in recognizing and preventing the spread of these diseases.
subject: biology

summary: Airborne diseases spread through the air when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Common examples include: (1) Common cold and influenza: caused by viruses, affecting the respiratory tract with symptoms like nasal congestion and discharge, sore throat, fever, cough, and body ache. Prevention includes washing hands frequently, not sharing personal items, and covering mouth and nose using a mask. (2) Chickenpox: caused by a virus affecting the respiratory tract and skin, with mild fever, itchy skin, rashes, and blisters.
subject: biology

aliases: Diseases Spread Through Contaminated Water and Food
summary: Waterborne and foodborne diseases spread through contaminated drinking water or food. Common examples include: (1) Hepatitis A: caused by a virus affecting the liver, with symptoms like fatigue, fever, loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, jaundice, and pain in the upper right abdomen. Prevention includes drinking boiled water and vaccination. (2) Cholera: caused by bacteria affecting the intestine, with diarrhoea and dehydration.
subject: biology

summary: Vector-borne diseases are transmitted by insects called vectors, particularly mosquitos. Common examples include: (1) Malaria: caused by protozoa, transmitted through skin to blood, with symptoms like high fever, profuse sweating, and periodic chills. Prevention includes use of mosquito nets and repellents, wearing long-sleeved clothes, and control of mosquito breeding in and around your home. (2) Dengue fever (Break bone fever): caused by a virus transmitted through skin to blood, with fever, headache, muscle and joint pain, and nausea.
subject: biology

summary: Some infectious diseases are caused by worms that live inside our bodies, especially in the digestive system. They feed on nutrients and live as parasites—organisms that live in or on another living being. Example: Ascariasis caused by roundworms in the intestine, with symptoms including worms in stool, loss of appetite, poor growth, diarrhoea, weight loss, and anaemia. These worms usually spread through contaminated food, water, soil, or contact with infected people or animals.
subject: biology

summary: Prevention is better than cure. To protect ourselves from both communicable and non-communicable diseases, we should follow general preventive measures, build our immunity through healthy living and vaccination, and use antibiotics wisely when needed. Understanding how our immune system works and how to prevent antibiotic resistance are important for disease control.
subject: biology

summary: Simple but important precautions to prevent infectious diseases include: (1) Keeping ourselves and our surroundings clean. (2) Practising basic hygiene every day. (3) Washing hands with soap and water to remove pathogens. (4) Covering our mouth and nose while coughing or sneezing.
subject: biology

summary: The natural ability of our body to fight diseases is known as immunity. Some people get sick more frequently than others, although living in a similar environment, because of differences in their immunity. Our body has a special system called the immune system that helps fight against diseases by recognising and attacking harmful germs.
subject: biology

summary: Vaccines help prevent serious infections caused by viruses and bacteria. A vaccine helps our body fight certain diseases by training the immune system to recognise and attack harmful germs, providing what is known as acquired immunity—protection developed after exposure to a pathogen or a vaccine. Vaccines can be made in different ways: from weakened or dead pathogens (like viruses or bacteria), from inactive or harmless parts of the pathogen, or (in newer vaccines) by instructing our own body cells to make a harmless part of the germ, which our immune system then learns to fight.
subject: biology

summary: If our immune system fails to protect us against an infectious disease, we fall ill and need to visit a doctor. The doctor may give us medicines called antibiotics, which kill the bacteria that might have caused the disease. Antibiotics work only against bacterial infections because they target parts of bacterial cells that are different from human or other animal cells. They do not work against viruses or diseases caused by protozoa.
subject: biology

summary: Antibiotic resistance is a phenomenon where bacteria that were earlier killed by a given antibiotic are found to survive and multiply despite treatment with that antibiotic. This makes common infections harder to treat and increases the risk of complications, prolonged illness, and even death. Antibiotic resistance develops when: (1) Few bacteria naturally develop resistance to antibiotics. (2) When antibiotics kill bacteria causing illness, they also kill good bacteria protecting the body from infection.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.5(a) shows how antibiotic-resistant bacteria spread in the community through humans, animals, plants, food products, and hospitals. Fig. 3.5(b) illustrates how bacteria become resistant: few bacteria develop resistance, antibiotics kill both harmful and protective bacteria, resistant bacteria multiply, and resistance transfers to other bacteria; it also shows preventive measures like getting prescriptions and not giving antibiotics to cattle
subject: biology

summary: Ayurveda teaches us that true health is a balance of body, mind, and surroundings. Following dinacharya (daily routine) and ritucharya (seasonal routine) helps maintain this balance. Eating fresh, wholesome food suited to one's prakriti (body constitution) is essential. Regular exercise, cleanliness, restful sleep, and a calm mind support overall well-being. This can also be achieved through practices like yoga, meditation, and mindfulness.
subject: biology

summary: Traditional medicine systems like Ayurveda, Siddha, and Unani have been used in India for many years to manage common health problems. They use natural substances like herbs, oils, and minerals for managing illnesses and promoting recovery, and focus on a healthy lifestyle and balanced diet. While these systems can help with some conditions and are useful for everyday well-being, they may not be effective for all diseases and at all stages.
subject: biology

concept_name: Edward Jenner and the Smallpox Vaccine
summary: Smallpox was a deadly disease that caused blisters and killed millions. A milder disease called cowpox, seen in cows, could also infect humans. In the late 1700s, English doctor Edward Jenner observed that milkmaids who had cowpox did not catch smallpox, likely because the two viruses are related. He hypothesized that content in the pus of cowpox blisters protected people from smallpox.
diagram_description: A graph shows the number of countries reporting smallpox cases from 1950 to 1980, with a sharp decline after the 1965 vaccine campaign and eradication in 1979
subject: biology

summary: India is one of the world's largest vaccine producers. It manufactures vaccines on a massive scale and supplies them to many countries (over 100 countries under Vaccine Maitri). Indian vaccine companies played a key role during the COVID-19 pandemic and continue to support global health efforts.
subject: biology

concept_name: Alexander Fleming and Discovery of Penicillin
summary: Penicillin was discovered in 1928 by Alexander Fleming, a bacteriologist from London. While studying harmful bacteria, he noticed that a mould on a discarded petri dish stopped the bacteria from growing. He realized the mould released a substance that killed the bacteria. This chance discovery led to the discovery of penicillin, the first antibiotic used to treat bacterial infections.
subject: biology

summary: Kamal Ranadive (1917–2001) was a pioneering biomedical researcher. She studied how hormones and certain viruses are linked to cancer, helping improve its treatment and prevention. Her work also showed how tobacco, diet, and pollution can raise the risk of cancer, highlighting the importance of a healthy lifestyle.
subject: biology

summary: Maharaj Kishan Bhan was a well-known Indian doctor and scientist. As Secretary of the Department of Biotechnology, he helped promote science and innovation in India. He played a key role in developing the Rotavirus vaccine, which protects children from diarrhoea. He believed in using research to create affordable healthcare and made a big difference in India's health and biotechnology sectors.
subject: biology

summary: The environment consists of living (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) components. A habitat is a place where an organism lives, providing food, water, shelter, and space. Biotic components include plants, animals, and microorganisms. Abiotic components include air, water, soil, sunlight, and temperature. Different habitats have different biotic and abiotic components suited to the organisms living there.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.1 shows two types of habitats: (a) Pond habitat with water, aquatic plants, fish, and (b) Forest habitat with trees, grass, birds, deer, and soil
subject: biology

summary: A habitat is the place where an organism lives. It could be as large as a forest or as small as the bark of a tree. A habitat provides food, oxygen, shelter, and space to grow—conditions essential for survival. Different organisms need specific conditions to survive, which is why some live on land while others live in water. Examples include pond (aquatic habitat) and forest (terrestrial habitat).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.1 shows examples of pond and forest habitats with their characteristic organisms and physical features
subject: biology

summary: Biotic components are living beings in a habitat, such as plants, animals, and microorganisms. Abiotic components are non-living things, such as air, water, soil, sunlight, and temperature. Both types interact with each other. For example, fish (biotic) obtain oxygen from water (abiotic), and plants (biotic) need sunlight (abiotic) for photosynthesis. Different habitats have different combinations of biotic and abiotic components.
diagram_description: Table 12.1 lists examples of biotic components (fish, grass, trees, birds) and abiotic components (water, soil) in pond and forest habitats
subject: biology

summary: Life in nature is organized at different levels. An individual organism belongs to a population (group of same species), which is part of a community (different populations together), which exists in an ecosystem. This hierarchy shows how organisms are grouped and interact in nature.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.7 shows concentric circles representing the hierarchy from individual to population to community to ecosystem
subject: biology

summary: A population is a group of the same type of organisms living together in a habitat at a given time. For example, all fish of the same species in a pond, or 20 plants of one type in a 1m × 1m area. Population size is the number of individual organisms counted at a specific place and time. If all organisms in a habitat were the same, they would compete for the same resources (food, water, space) leading to scarcity.
diagram_description: Table 12.2 shows how to count and record population numbers of different organisms (Plant 1: 20, Plant 2: 5, Animals) in a marked area
subject: biology

summary: A community comprises different populations sharing the same habitat. The biotic components of a habitat—plants, animals, and microorganisms—together form the community. These organisms interact and depend on one another for survival. For example, in a pond community, there are populations of fish, frogs, turtles, snakes, dragonflies, mosquitos, snails, ducks, and various aquatic plants like algae, diatoms, duckweeds, and lotus.
subject: biology

summary: An ecosystem is formed when biotic components (plants, animals, microorganisms) and abiotic components (air, water, soil, sunlight, temperature) in a habitat interact with each other. Organisms in an ecosystem interact with abiotic components for food, shelter, and protection. Different communities of living organisms interact with abiotic components. Ecosystems can be large (forests, rivers) or small (a single tree).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.5 shows biotic and abiotic interactions in a pond ecosystem with sun, birds, fish, aquatic plants, water, and other organisms. Fig. 12.6 shows overlap of terrestrial (mountains, forests, grassland, farmland) and aquatic (river) ecosystems
subject: biology

summary: There are two main types of ecosystems in nature. Aquatic ecosystems include ponds, rivers, and lakes. Terrestrial ecosystems include forests, farms, grasslands, or even large trees like banyan, mango, or pilkhan. Farmland is a human-made ecosystem. Ecosystems can be large or small and often overlap—for example, a river (aquatic) flowing through mountains and forests (terrestrial).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.6 shows overlapping terrestrial (mountains, forests, grassland, farmland) and aquatic (river) ecosystems in one landscape
subject: biology

summary: Three types of interactions occur in ecosystems: (1) Interactions between abiotic and biotic components that influence life processes like nutrition, respiration, and reproduction (e.g., earthworms live in moist soil, fish lay eggs in water); (2) Interactions between two abiotic components that influence physical characteristics (e.g., sunlight causes water evaporation, air current creates waves); (3) Interactions among biotic components that influence resource availability (e.g., frog eats insects, frogs and fish compete for larvae).
diagram_description: Table 12.3 categorizes interactions in a pond habitat across three criteria with examples like earthworms in moist soil, water evaporation, and frogs eating insects. Fig. 12.5 shows various interactions in a pond ecosystem
subject: biology

summary: Organisms in an ecosystem are classified based on how they obtain food. Producers make their own food, consumers depend on others for food, and decomposers break down dead matter. This classification helps understand the flow of energy and nutrients in ecosystems.
diagram_description: Table 12.4 shows organisms categorized by whether they perform photosynthesis, feed on plants, feed on animals, or feed on both
subject: biology

summary: Producers or autotrophs (auto=self + troph=food) are organisms that make their own food. Plants make food by the process of photosynthesis using sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water. They are at the first trophic level in food chains. Examples include grass, trees, and aquatic plants.
subject: biology

summary: Consumers or heterotrophs (hetero=other + troph=food) are organisms that cannot produce their own food and depend on other organisms. They are classified into three types: (1) Herbivores eat only plants (e.g., deer, hare, grasshopper); (2) Carnivores eat only animals (e.g., leopard, snake, vulture); (3) Omnivores eat both plants and animals (e.g., crows, foxes, mice). In food chains, herbivores are at the second trophic level, small carnivores at the third level, and large carnivores at the next level.
diagram_description: Table 12.4 categorizes organisms by their feeding habits. Fig. 12.8 shows a simple food chain with grass (producer), hare (herbivore), and leopard (carnivore)
subject: biology

summary: Decomposers or saprotrophs (sapro=rotten + trophs=food) are microorganisms like fungi and bacteria that break down complex substances in dead plants and animals into simpler ones. This process is called decomposition and returns important nutrients to the soil. Examples include mushrooms growing on dead plants or trees during rainy season, and tiny insects like beetles and flies on animal droppings that help break down and recycle nutrients. Decomposers play an important role in recycling nutrients.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.12 shows mushrooms (umbrella-like fungi) growing on dead organic matter
subject: biology

summary: Energy flows through ecosystems via feeding relationships. Food chains show linear 'who eats whom' sequences, while food webs show interconnected food chains forming a network. Trophic levels indicate the position of organisms in these feeding hierarchies.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.8, 12.9 show food chains. Fig. 12.11 shows a food web with multiple interconnected feeding relationships. Fig. 12.10 shows a food chain represented as a pyramid
subject: biology

summary: A food chain is a simple sequence showing 'who eats whom' in an ecosystem. It represents the interactions between biotic components based on feeding relationships in a linear chain. Examples: (1) Grass → Hare → Leopard; (2) Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle; (3) Millet → Mouse → Eagle. The arrow points from what is eaten to what eats it, showing the direction of energy flow.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.8 shows grass → hare → leopard. Fig. 12.9 shows grass → grasshopper → frog → snake → eagle with sun at top. Fig. 12.10a shows millet → mouse → eagle in a crop field
subject: biology

summary: Each organism in a food chain has a specific position called a trophic level (troph=food). First trophic level: Producers (green plants). Second level: Herbivores. Third level: Small carnivores. Next level: Large carnivores. The number of organisms typically decreases at higher trophic levels, forming a pyramid shape when represented graphically. For example, in a millet field: many millet plants (base), fewer mice (middle), one eagle (top).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.10b shows a pyramid with millet at base, mouse in middle, and eagle at top, representing decreasing numbers at higher trophic levels
subject: biology

summary: A food web is formed when food chains in an ecosystem are interlinked with each other to form a network. Each organism may be eaten by two or more types of organisms, creating complex feeding relationships. Food webs show the realistic complexity of feeding relationships in nature. For example, in a grassland, grass is eaten by both grasshopper and hare; the hare is eaten by both fox and owl; the grasshopper is eaten by both frog and bird.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.11 shows an interconnected food web with grasses at base, multiple herbivores (grasshopper, hare, mouse), and multiple carnivores (frog, bird, snake, hawk, fox, owl) with arrows showing multiple feeding relationships. Fig. 12.20 shows a partial food web with sun, grass/plants, hare, deer, fox, and eagle
subject: biology

summary: Besides feeding relationships, organisms interact in various ways. Competition occurs when organisms compete for common resources. Symbiotic relationships include mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, one harmed). These interactions are part of the complex web of life and help maintain balance in ecosystems.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.15 shows flamingos competing for resources in a pond. Fig. 12.16 shows three types of symbiotic interactions with examples: honeybee-flower (mutualism), orchid-tree (commensalism), tick-dog (parasitism)
subject: biology

summary: Organisms compete for common resources like food, water, physical space, or sunlight. This competition helps control population size and keeps the ecosystem balanced. Without it, one species could multiply too much causing an imbalance. Example: Different species of birds may compete for the same fruit, or flamingos compete for food in a pond.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.15 shows multiple flamingos in a pond competing for algae and small organisms (shown in circular insets)
subject: biology

summary: Mutualism is a relationship where both organisms benefit. Example: Honeybees and flowers—the flower gets pollinated (benefitted) while the bee gets nectar (benefitted). This type of interaction is essential for processes like pollination, which leads to fruit and seed formation.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.16 shows a honeybee on a flower with checkmarks indicating both flower (gets pollinated) and bee (gets nectar) benefit
subject: biology

summary: Commensalism is a relationship where one organism benefits while the other is not affected. Example: Orchids on trees—the orchid gets physical support (benefitted) while the tree branch is unaffected. The orchid uses the tree for support but does not harm or benefit it.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.16 shows an orchid growing on a tree branch with checkmark for orchid (gets support) and neutral symbol for tree (unaffected)
subject: biology

summary: Parasitism is a relationship where one organism benefits while the other is harmed. Example: Ticks on the body of dogs—the tick feeds on dog blood (benefitted) while the dog gets skin irritation (harmed). The organism that benefits is called a parasite, and the organism that is harmed is called the host.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.16 shows a tick on a dog with checkmark for tick (feeds on blood) and X mark for dog (gets skin irritation)
subject: biology

summary: An ecosystem stays in balance when interactions among organisms and their environment keep populations and resources stable. This balance is dynamic, not fixed, and can be disrupted by natural or human-made changes. One small change can lead to many others (cascading effect). Example from pond study: Fish eat dragonfly larvae → fewer dragonflies → more bees/butterflies (dragonflies eat them) → more pollination → more seeds in nearby plants.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.4 shows arrows indicating direct (red) and indirect (green) effects in a pond ecosystem: fish → larval dragonfly → dragonfly → St. John's wort (plant). Fig. 12.13 shows cascade: plants die → less oxygen → fish die → more insects → plants damaged by pests. Fig. 12.14 shows Indian bullfrog
subject: biology

summary: Biotic and abiotic components of ecosystems support various life processes. Humans benefit from ecosystems in many ways. Forests provide fresh air (oxygen), fertile soil, food, fibres, timber, and medicines. Aquatic ecosystems provide water and food.
subject: biology

summary: Humans have created artificial ecosystems like fish ponds, farms, and parks to meet their needs. When well designed, these can help reduce pollution, support biodiversity, and provide recreational spaces for people. Unlike natural ecosystems, these need human care and management. Examples include agricultural fields, urban parks, and aquaculture ponds.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.21 shows various farming practices including ploughing with oxen, monoculture crop field, mixed cropping with vegetables, dairy farming, and fruit orchards
subject: biology

summary: Farming is a major livelihood in India but can become unsustainable if not managed with environment-friendly practices. Between 1950-1965, India faced food crisis. The mid-20th century Green Revolution used tractors, machines, synthetic fertilizers, and pesticides to increase food production. However, these methods are now considered unsustainable due to overuse of synthetic chemicals, excessive groundwater extraction, and monoculture (growing only one type of crop).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.18 shows a ladybird beetle (natural predator) feeding on pests, illustrating natural pest control. Fig. 12.21 shows various farming practices
subject: biology

summary: A flower has stalks, sepals (green leafy structures), coloured petals, and two reproductive parts—Carpels (female) and stamens (male). Stamens burst and release yellow dust-like pollen grains. Wind, water, insects, bats, and birds help carry pollen from stamens to carpels of the same or different flowers. This process is called pollination and is essential for formation of fruits and seeds. Insects like bees and butterflies act as pollinators.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.2 shows an insect on a flower performing pollination
subject: biology

summary: The Sundarbans have the largest mangrove forests in the world, located where Ganges and Brahmaputra Rivers meet between India and Bangladesh. Home to various flora and fauna, many endangered. Mangroves protect by slowing strong winds and waves during storms and floods, absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen. UNESCO declared it World Heritage Site in 1987.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.17 shows mangrove forest in Sundarbans with characteristic aerial roots visible above water
subject: biology

summary: Protected areas are parts of land or water set aside to conserve wildlife and their habitats. India has many protected areas like national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves, and community conserved areas. These help protect entire habitats including endangered animals, birds, and rare plants.
subject: biology

summary: India has diverse habitats and seasons attracting migratory birds that fly thousands of miles from different parts of world. They migrate to avoid harsh climate and search for food. Birds enhance aesthetics and play significant role in ecosystem balance as pollinators, seed dispersers along migration path, linking two habitats. They are predators of insect pests helping farmers control pest populations, indirectly helping healthy crop growth.
subject: biology

summary: In several parts of India (Odisha, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Assam, Chhattisgarh), elephants often enter farms and villages when vegetation is scarce and waterholes dry up in natural habitat. They search for food like bananas and sugarcane, leading to crop damage and sometimes harm to people and domestic animals. Changes in rainfall and temperature affect vegetation; cutting trees for roads and buildings worsens situation, leading to drying and shrinking of forests (natural home of animals). Loss of habitat causes animals to move into human habitats.
diagram_description: Chapter opening image shows elephant herd crossing under railway bridge through designated elephant corridor with bilingual signboard
subject: biology

summary: Asir Jawahar Thomas Johnsingh (A.J.T. Johnsingh) was a famous Indian wildlife biologist who helped understand forest ecosystems through eyes of animals. Pioneer in studying wildlife through modern tracking system. His research in Bandipur National Park, Karnataka showed how predators like tigers and leopards rely on prey such as deer and wild boar.
subject: biology

summary: The ancient text Vrikshayurveda emphasizes soil health and nourishment. The text strongly advocates for continuous nourishment of soil through organic manure like Kunapa Jala (a liquid fertilizer made from animal and plant waste by process of fermentation that breaks complex substances into simpler ones) and other composted materials. This represents traditional Indian sustainable farming practices.
subject: biology

concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
summary: Earth is unique among known planets because it supports life. Key factors include its position in the solar system (habitable zone), its size (maintains atmosphere via gravity), presence of an ozone layer, and a protective magnetic field. The thin crust where all life exists is comparatively tiny — if Earth were an apple, the crust would be as thin as the apple's skin.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.1 shows a cross-section of Earth with labelled layers: Crust, Upper mantle, Lower mantle, Outer core, Inner core — compared to the thin skin of an apple. Students must identify the layer where life exists (crust) and relate its relative thinness to the apple analogy.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

parent_concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
summary: The habitable zone (also called the Goldilocks zone) is the range of distances from a star within which the temperature allows water to remain in liquid form. Earth lies in this zone around the Sun. If Earth were closer, water would evaporate; if farther, it would freeze. Liquid water is considered essential for life to evolve. Mars lies at the edge of the Sun's habitable zone but currently lacks liquid water and a thick atmosphere.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.4 shows a star with three zones labelled: 'Too hot' (closest), 'Just right' (middle — habitable zone), and 'Too cold' (farthest). Students must identify which zone Earth occupies and what it enables.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

parent_concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
summary: Earth's size gives it just the right gravitational pull to hold an atmosphere. If Earth were much smaller (same density), gravity would be too weak and gases would escape into space — as seen on Mars (atmosphere 100 times thinner than Earth's) and Mercury (no atmosphere). If Earth were too large, gravity would be so strong it could crush living beings. Earth's nearly circular orbit also keeps sunlight and heat steady throughout the year, preventing extreme seasonal temperature swings.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

parent_concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
summary: Some oxygen in the atmosphere is converted into ozone (O₃, a three-atom oxygen molecule), forming the ozone layer. This layer acts as a shield, blocking harmful ultraviolet (UV) rays from the Sun that can damage living cells. The ozone layer is part of the atmosphere and is essential for protecting life on Earth's surface.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

parent_concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
summary: Earth behaves like a giant magnet. Its magnetic field is believed to originate from the movement of molten iron in Earth's core. Earth is constantly bombarded by harmful high-energy particles: cosmic rays (from across the universe) and solar wind (from the Sun). These particles can damage the atmosphere, reduce the ozone layer, and allow more harmful UV rays to reach Earth. The magnetic field deflects most of these particles, protecting the atmosphere and all life on Earth.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Life on Earth is sustained by the interaction of four major systems: the atmosphere (air), hydrosphere (water), geosphere (solid Earth — rocks, soil, minerals), and biosphere (all living beings and their environments). These systems interact and depend on each other. A change in one system — like deforestation — can affect rainfall, soil quality, air, and biodiversity. This balance is essential for keeping Earth habitable.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The greenhouse effect occurs when gases like carbon dioxide in the atmosphere absorb and trap heat (radiation) given off by Earth after it is warmed by the Sun. On Earth, this mild effect keeps temperatures just high enough for water to remain liquid — essential for life. Without the atmosphere, Earth would lose heat to space and become too cold. On Venus, an extreme greenhouse effect (almost entirely CO₂ atmosphere) makes it the hottest planet in the solar system — hotter even than Mercury, which is closer to the Sun.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.2 shows a schematic of the greenhouse effect on Earth: sunlight enters the atmosphere, warms the Earth, and heat radiation is absorbed/trapped by atmospheric gases (shown as arrows). Fig. 13.3 shows a plant greenhouse with glass walls for comparison. Students must distinguish the two mechanisms.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The hydrosphere includes all water on Earth — ponds, lakes, rivers, springs, seas, oceans, and groundwater — covering about 70% of Earth's surface, making Earth appear blue from space (Blue Planet). Water is a good solvent and is essential for life: it transports nutrients in plants, regulates body temperature in animals, aids digestion, and ensures hydration. The hydrosphere hosts millions of life forms from tiny planktons to giant whales. Water vapour forms clouds and brings rainfall/snow, refilling freshwater sources and shaping weather, farming, and ecosystems.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.5 shows Earth photographed from space appearing blue due to extensive water coverage. Fig. 13.8 shows aquatic life (fish, whale) representing biodiversity of the hydrosphere. Students must identify why Earth is called the Blue Planet.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The geosphere includes all solid parts of Earth: rocks, soils, and minerals. Soil contains nutrients like nitrogen and potassium (from slow breakdown of rocks and remains of organisms) that plants need to grow. The geosphere also provides minerals like salt, coal, oil, and metals (iron, copper) that support ecosystems and human life. Geodiversity refers to the variety of landforms, rocks, soils, and the processes that shape and alter them (e.g., mountains, valleys, deserts).
diagram_description: Fig. 13.9 shows a cross-sectional landscape illustrating geodiversity — mountains, valleys, rivers, plains, and varied soil/rock types in one image. Students must identify what geodiversity means and how it supports habitats.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The biosphere includes all living beings (plants, animals, microorganisms) along with the places where they live — encompassing land, water, and air. Living beings interact with their surroundings to survive and grow. Plants make food through photosynthesis, animals eat plants or other animals, and decomposers break down dead matter returning nutrients to soil. The biosphere thus forms a self-sustaining system that supports life through interdependence.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Reproduction ensures continuity of life on Earth. Without it, life would disappear. Parents pass on genetic material (genes) — stored in every cell — to offspring, acting as a detailed instruction manual guiding development (e.g., blood, bones, muscles, skin). Reproduction also allows small changes (variations) to be passed on, enabling adaptation and evolution over generations.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.10 shows a calf growing into a cow, illustrating how genetic instructions ensure offspring develop into the same type as the parent.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: In asexual reproduction, a single parent produces new individuals that are genetically exact copies of itself (identical genetic instructions). In plants, this is called vegetative propagation — new plants grow from parts like leaves, stems, or roots (e.g., money plant stem cuttings, potato 'eyes', ginger pieces, bamboo, sugarcane). In microorganisms: bacteria and amoeba divide into two identical individuals (binary fission). In simple animals: Hydra grows buds that break off into new individuals; Planaria (flatworm) can regenerate from a fragment of its body.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.11 shows vegetative propagation examples: (a) ginger rhizome with shoots and (b) sprouted potato with 'eyes'. Students must identify these as examples of vegetative propagation and asexual reproduction. Fig. 13.12 shows Planaria.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Sexual reproduction involves two parents (male and female). Instructions (genes) from both parents combine in the offspring, so the offspring is not identical to either parent — it shares traits with both but also shows new combinations. This mixing allows useful features to be kept while new ones appear, enabling adaptation and evolution over generations. Bacteria and yeast also have two mating types that act like two parents. Sexual reproduction produces variation, unlike asexual reproduction.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Gametes are specialised reproductive cells carrying only half the parent's genetic material, preventing doubling of genetic instructions each generation. Male gametes are sperm; female gametes are eggs (in animals) or pollen/ovules (in plants). When male and female gametes join, fertilisation occurs, forming a zygote with a complete set of instructions — half from each parent. Each gamete carries a different subset of the parent's instructions, so each child is genetically unique even among siblings.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.14 shows sperm cell + egg cell → Fertilisation → Zygote. Students must label the gametes, identify the process as fertilisation, and name the resulting cell as zygote.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Flowering plants produce male gametes (pollen grains, found in the anther) and female gametes (ovules, found deep inside the flower). Pollen is transferred to another flower by wind, insects, or animals — this is pollination. When male and female gametes combine, fertilisation occurs, forming a zygote that develops into a seed. The fleshy part of the flower around the ovule develops into a fruit.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.13 shows a cross-section of a flower with Anther (contains pollen — male gametes) and Ovule (female gametes) labelled. Students must identify which part produces which gamete and trace the sequence: pollination → fertilisation → seed → fruit.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: In animals, male gametes are sperm and female gametes are eggs. In fish and frogs, fertilisation is external — sperm and eggs are released into water where they combine, and the zygote develops into an embryo in water. In birds and most mammals (including humans), sperm are deposited inside the female's body (internal fertilisation). In birds, the fertilised egg (zygote) is laid; the embryo develops during hatching, nourished by nutrients stored in the egg.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The three biggest environmental challenges threatening life on Earth are climate change, biodiversity loss, and pollution — collectively called the triple planetary crisis. Human actions disturb the delicate balance of Earth's interconnected systems. Even small changes in global temperature, oxygen levels, or the ozone layer can endanger life.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil) releases greenhouse gases (CO₂, methane) that trap extra heat, causing global warming. Normally, trees, plants, and ocean planktons absorb CO₂, but fossil fuels release carbon locked underground for millions of years faster than Earth can absorb. Consequences include melting ice caps, rising sea levels (threatening coastal cities), extreme weather, and extinction of species. These long-term changes in temperature, rainfall, and weather patterns are collectively called climate change.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: When natural habitats are destroyed, plants and animals disappear, upsetting ecosystems. Every organism has a role in the food chain: if grasses vanish, herbivores (deer, grasshoppers) struggle; without herbivores, predators (tigers, foxes) lose their food source too. Losing even a few species weakens nature's ability to support life. Diverse ecosystems are stronger and more balanced. Preserving biodiversity is key to maintaining Earth's life-sustaining balance.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Air pollution from factories, vehicles, and burning fuels causes breathing problems, crop damage, smog, and acid rain. Water pollution from factory, farm, and plastic waste harms aquatic life and makes water unsafe. Soil pollution from excess fertilisers and poor waste disposal reduces crop yield and spreads harmful substances through food chains. CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons) damage the ozone layer; the Montreal Protocol (1987) helped reduce CFCs and allowed the ozone layer to slowly recover.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.15 shows industrial chimneys with smoke (air pollution). Fig. 13.16 shows a polluted water body with waste/debris (water pollution). Students must identify the source and type of pollution from each image.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: The solar system has 8 planets orbiting the Sun in nearly circular orbits. In order of increasing distance: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars (small, rocky planets) and Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune (large, mostly gaseous planets). Generally, planets farther from the Sun are cooler. Key data for comparison: Mercury (avg. 170°C, no atmosphere), Venus (avg. 450°C, radius 0.95× Earth, yes atmosphere), Earth (avg. 15°C, radius 1, yes atmosphere).
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Eight planets orbit the Sun. Rocky (terrestrial) planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars — relatively small. Gas giants: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune — large, mostly gaseous. Distance from the Sun generally determines temperature (closer = hotter), with the notable exception of Venus. Planets' orbits are nearly circular, keeping sunlight and heat relatively steady year-round.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

aliases: Why Venus is the Hottest Planet
summary: Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system (avg. 450°C) despite being the second planet from the Sun — not the closest (Mercury is closer). This is because Venus has a thick atmosphere composed almost entirely of carbon dioxide, which traps heat through an extreme greenhouse effect and does not let heat escape. This makes Venus hotter than Mercury. This example illustrates that a planet's temperature depends not only on distance from the Sun but also on its atmospheric composition.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Genes (genetic material) are instructions stored inside every cell of a living being, guiding development from a single cell (e.g., how to make blood, bones, muscles, skin). In asexual reproduction, offspring receive identical genetic instructions. In sexual reproduction, offspring receive a mix of instructions from both parents, creating variation. Small variations can help organisms survive better in new environments (e.g., camels developing humps to store fat for deserts).
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Mars lies at the edge of the Sun's habitable zone. Multiple spacecraft and rovers have explored Mars but found no proof of life yet. Scientists believe Mars may have had liquid water (possibly lakes) and conditions for simple life forms in the past. India's Mangalyaan (Mars Orbiter Mission), launched by ISRO in 2013, studied Mars's atmosphere, surface, and signs of past water using low-cost technology.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.6 shows a photograph of Mars. Fig. 13.7 shows the Mangalyaan spacecraft. Students may be asked to identify India's contribution to Mars exploration.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

concept_name: Asexual Reproduction in Microbes and Simple Animals
summary: Asexual reproduction is not limited to plants. Bacteria and amoeba reproduce by binary fission — dividing into two identical individuals. Algae (multicellular) can regrow from small cut parts. Hydra (simple animal) produces small buds on its body that break off and grow into new individuals. Planaria (a flatworm) can regenerate an entire organism from just a fragment of its body — scientists study Planaria to understand regeneration in animals.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.12 shows Planaria with arrows indicating how a fragment can grow into a new individual. Students must identify Planaria as an example of regeneration/asexual reproduction.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Several international agreements address environmental threats: Montreal Protocol (1987) — reduced CFCs, allowing the ozone layer to slowly recover. Earth Summit (1992) — led to international efforts on climate change and biodiversity. Kyoto Protocol (2005) — committed countries to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
