Paris Agreement (2015) — set a goal to limit global warming to below 1.5°C; as of 2025, the world is not on track to meet this goal.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

summary: Scientific models are simplified representations of real systems that focus only on the most important features for a given question. Because the natural world is too complex to study in full detail, models deliberately ignore certain details. In physics, a moving car may be treated as a single point; in chemistry, atoms are drawn as spheres; in biology, cells are shown as diagrams highlighting key parts; in earth science, the Earth is treated as a smooth layered sphere. These choices are intentional, not mistakes — the goal is to keep things simple enough to find answers.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: A model is a simplified way of looking at a real system that focuses only on what is most important for a given question. Examples: a moving car represented as a single point (physics); atoms drawn as spheres (chemistry); cells shown as diagrams of key parts (biology); Earth treated as a smooth layered sphere (earth science). Building a model requires choosing what to include and what to deliberately ignore.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Building a model involves making assumptions and deliberately ignoring certain details that are less relevant to the question being asked. For example, when studying a falling object, air resistance may be neglected to focus on gravity. In biology, individual cells may be ignored when studying the heart as a functioning system. These are purposeful choices — not errors — made to keep the model tractable while still answering the question.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Science uses language in a careful and precise way. Words like force, work, cell, and reaction have specific scientific meanings distinct from everyday usage. Scientists worldwide share a common language of specific terms, symbols, and units to describe observations, compare results, and build ideas together. Quantities such as mass, velocity, force, and electric current are represented by symbols (m, v, F, I) each associated with a defined unit.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Science uses words with precise, specific meanings that may differ from everyday usage (e.g., force, work, cell, reaction). Quantities are represented by internationally agreed symbols: mass (m), velocity (v), force (F), electric current (I). This shared language allows scientists across the world to communicate clearly and unambiguously, compare results, and build on each other's work. Mathematics extends this precision by expressing relationships between quantities in equations, which are compact statements about how things relate — not merely calculation tools.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Standard units (SI) are internationally agreed measurements that ensure a kilogram or any other unit means the same quantity everywhere. Using standard units avoids conversion errors, allows scientific results to be compared across the world, and ensures fairness in daily life and trade. A famous example: an aircraft ran out of fuel mid-flight because fuel density was calculated in pounds per litre instead of kilograms per litre, leaving the aircraft ~15,000 litres short — illustrating how unit mix-ups can have serious real-world consequences.
diagram_description: Fig. 1.1: A vegetable seller using a pan balance — students should note this illustrates the use of standard weights (kilograms) in everyday trade.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: As observations are repeated, measurements refined, and ideas tested, scientific understanding is organised into laws, theories, and principles — each with a specific meaning in science. A law describes a regular observed pattern (often mathematically). A theory explains why those patterns occur, based on evidence. A principle is a broad guiding idea applicable to a given situation.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: A scientific law describes a regular pattern observed in nature, often expressed in words or as a mathematical relationship. Example: Newton's laws of motion explain the jerk felt when a bus stops suddenly. Laws describe what happens but do not necessarily explain why.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: A scientific theory goes beyond describing patterns — it provides an explanation of why those patterns occur, based on evidence gathered and tested over time. Example: atomic theory explains how molecules are formed. Crucially, in science a theory does NOT mean a guess or untested idea; it is an explanation based on careful testing and critical examination. Theories are always open to improvement and may change as new evidence becomes available — this openness to revision is a key feature that makes science reliable.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Principles are broad ideas that help make sense of a given situation. Example: the principle of conservation of energy, applied when climbing stairs. Principles serve as guiding frameworks rather than precise descriptions or detailed explanations.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: One of science's greatest strengths is its ability to make predictions — reasoned expectations about what will happen under new or different conditions, based on established laws, theories, and models. When predictions match observations, confidence in the science grows. When they do not match, scientists re-examine assumptions, models, or measurements. Even the most successful theories have limits and may fail under new conditions or more precise measurements.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Scientific predictions are reasoned expectations based on evidence and careful thinking — not guesses. Well-established laws, theories, and models allow us to anticipate outcomes before or even without performing an experiment. Examples: predicting how far a kicked football will travel (physics); estimating CO₂ produced in a chemical reaction (chemistry); predicting how breathing changes during running (biology). A scientifically testable prediction must be based on measurable evidence and past patterns, not vague observations like 'clouds look dark'.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Even the most successful scientific theories can fail when new conditions are explored or when measurements become more precise. Such failures are science's greatest strength, not a weakness — they prompt re-examination of assumptions, models, or measurements. Scientists reject ideas only on the basis of evidence, never on opinion or belief. No scientific theory is ever final or beyond question. This openness to being corrected by nature is what makes science reliable and progressive.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Science values careful reasoning, often more than exact calculations. A helpful scientific strategy is to first understand the situation, identify quantities that matter, and make a rough estimate to check whether an answer is reasonable. Approximate estimates can reveal whether a result is plausible or impossible without requiring exact values. Mathematics in science is a language for thinking — equations are compact statements about how quantities relate, not merely calculation tools.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Estimation is making a rough calculation to check whether a result is reasonable or impossible, without needing an exact value. Example from chapter: estimating litres of air breathed in a day — ~12–15 breaths/minute × 1440 minutes/day × 0.5 litres/breath ≈ 10,000 litres/day, cross-checked using a balloon analogy (~8640 litres). Learning to estimate builds intuition, helps detect errors, and develops confidence. Science values careful reasoning perhaps more than accurate calculations.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Mathematics in science is a language for thinking clearly about the world, not a hurdle or mere calculation tool. An equation is a compact statement about how certain quantities are related. Mathematical expressions are used to describe rates of chemical reactions, patterns of population growth, changes in energy, and motion (distance, time, velocity). Using maths in science means understanding the situation first, identifying relevant quantities, and then using mathematical relationships to reason carefully — not simply memorising equations.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Science is not merely a collection of facts, equations, or experiments — it is a human activity shaped by curiosity, creativity, collaboration, and careful questioning. It grows through asking questions, testing ideas, sharing results, and learning from mistakes, developed over time across many cultures and generations. Scientific thinking is valuable beyond the classroom: it helps evaluate information critically and make sense of the world.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: The natural world has no inherent boundaries — divisions into physics, chemistry, biology, earth science, and mathematics are made by humans to organise knowledge, not because these fields are truly independent. Most real-world problems (e.g., climate change, medicine, sustainable technology) require ideas from multiple disciplines. Science also connects naturally with mathematics, technology, arts, and social sciences.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Science is a human activity shaped by curiosity, creativity, collaboration, and careful questioning. It develops over time through the work of many individuals across different cultures and generations. Scientific thinking — understanding how we know what we know, evaluating evidence critically, and making sense of technology and nature — is valuable in any career or walk of life, not just for those who continue studying science after Grade 10.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Physicist Meghnad Saha studied light from stars by treating stellar matter as a hot gas and ignoring many complex processes, focusing only on temperature, pressure, and how atoms form ions. This deliberate simplification allowed him to explain how the colour of stars is deeply connected to their temperature — a landmark application of scientific modelling. His work illustrates that science often begins by ignoring details to uncover fundamental relationships.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: The speed of light is denoted by the symbol 'c', derived from the Latin word celeritas meaning speed. This illustrates that scientific symbols often come from historical and international agreements rather than simple abbreviations. The speed of light is a defined physical constant equal to exactly 299,792,458 m/s. This is an example of how scientific language, including symbols, has specific, internationally agreed meanings.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: A real-world incident in which a passenger aircraft ran out of fuel mid-flight because the ground crew used fuel density in pounds (lb) per litre instead of kilograms (kg) per litre. The aircraft needed 22,300 kg of fuel but ended up ~15,000 litres short. It survived by gliding to an emergency landing with no casualties. This demonstrates why using standard SI units universally is critical — mixing unit systems causes dangerous and costly errors.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: Weather forecasts use measurements and models of many changing factors — temperature, pressure, humidity, wind — but tiny differences in initial conditions can grow over time and lead to very different outcomes. This is why forecasts are relatively reliable for a few hours or days, but become less certain further into the future. This illustrates the concept that even well-constructed scientific models and predictions have inherent limits.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

summary: The claim that 'food becomes harmful during a solar eclipse' is a commonly circulated belief that can be evaluated scientifically. An eclipse is simply a play of shadows — no significant physical, chemical, or biological change occurs that would make food harmful. Asking mechanistic questions (Does temperature change significantly? Does food go bad in shadow?) reveals no evidence supporting the claim.
diagram_description: Fig. 1.2: A total solar eclipse — students should recognise this as a play of shadows with no special physical or chemical effect on food.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

concept_name: How a Mask Works — Multidisciplinary Example
summary: Understanding how a surgical/N95 mask works requires concepts from multiple scientific disciplines: physics (particle motion and electrostatic attraction of fibres), chemistry (properties of polymer fibres), biology (size and behaviour of viruses), and mathematics (modelling airflow and filtration efficiency). This example illustrates that most real-world problems cannot be solved using any single branch of science alone, reinforcing the interdisciplinary nature of science.
diagram_description: Fig. 1.4: A collection of surgical masks — students should associate this image with the multidisciplinary scientific concepts required to understand mask function.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
summary: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Every living organism is made of one or more cells. Even when organised into tissues, organs, and organ systems, the cell remains the fundamental unit.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

parent_concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
summary: The cell represents the basic level at which life exists. It is the fundamental unit of structure and function in all living organisms, from bacteria to humans. All cellular activities are ultimately carried out at the cell level.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell as the Basic Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

parent_concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
summary: Unicellular organisms (e.g., bacteria, yeast, Amoeba) consist of only one cell that performs all life functions. Multicellular organisms (e.g., plants, fish, birds, humans) are made of millions of cells that work together, each potentially specialised for a specific function.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

parent_concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
summary: Similar cells performing similar functions form tissues. Different tissues organise to form an organ. Several organs work together to form organ systems. Example: nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea, and lungs form the respiratory system.

builds_upon.concept_name: Levels of Organisation in Living Organisms
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Cells are too small to be seen by the unaided eye. Microscopes use convex lenses or combinations of lenses (objective lens and eyepiece) to magnify objects. Robert Hooke first observed cells in 1665 using a self-designed microscope (200–300X) while examining thin slices of cork, naming the compartments 'cells'. Three key features of microscopes are resolution, contrast, and magnification.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.1: Scale diagram showing size of objects from atoms (0.1 nm) to rockets (100 m), with ranges indicating visibility by unaided eye, light microscope, and electron microscope. Students must identify which structures fall in each visibility range. Fig. 2.2: Labelled diagram of a light microscope showing eyepiece, body tube, objective lens, stage, mirror, coarse adjustment knob, fine adjustment knob, handle, and base.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: The limit of resolution is the ability of the eye to see two very close objects as separate and distinct. When viewed from 25 cm (near point of human eye), two points separated by about 0.1 mm can be seen as distinct; anything closer appears as a single point. The limit of resolution of the human eye is 0.1 mm.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Light microscopes use visible light and combinations of lenses (objective lens and eyepiece) to achieve magnification and resolution. Common objective lenses are 10X and 40X. Total magnification = magnifying power of eyepiece × magnifying power of objective lens. If both are 10X, total magnification = 100X. Used in school labs to observe fine structures.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.2: Students must label and identify parts: eyepiece, body tube, objective lens, stage, mirror, coarse adjustment knob, fine adjustment knob, handle, base.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Electron microscopes use a beam of electrons instead of light to produce highly magnified images. They reveal fine details of cell structure at the nanometre scale (1 nanometre = one-billionth of a metre = 0.000001 mm). They can reveal structures too small for a light microscope. Example: Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) image of lower surface of Colocasia leaf showing stomata.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.3: Image of an electron microscope. Fig. 2.4: SEM image of lower surface of Colocasia leaf showing stomata with scale bar 0.05 mm.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Cell size is estimated using a transparent ruler placed on the microscope stage. Formula: Estimated size of cell = Diameter of visible field (in µm) ÷ Number of cells along the diameter. Unit conversion: 1 mm = 1000 µm. Example: field diameter = 5 mm = 5000 µm; 25 cells along diameter → onion cell size = 5000/25 = 200 µm. Most plant and animal cells are 10–100 µm; most bacteria are 1–10 µm.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: All cells are surrounded by a cell membrane (plasma membrane). Cells of plants, fungi, and bacteria additionally have a cell wall outside the cell membrane. These boundary structures regulate what enters and exits the cell and provide structural support.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: The cell membrane (plasma membrane) is a thin boundary (7–10 nm thick) surrounding all cells. It is selectively permeable — allows some substances through while blocking others. Structure explained by the fluid-mosaic model: (1) lipid bilayer with water-attracting heads outward and water-repelling tails inward; (2) proteins embedded in the bilayer act as gatekeepers; (3) molecules can move sideways, flip, and rotate (fluid); (4) arrangement resembles tiles in a mosaic. Made of lipids (fats) and proteins.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.7: Structure of cell membrane showing lipid bilayer with water-attracting heads facing outward and tails facing inward, and various types of proteins embedded within the bilayer.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Diffusion is the net movement of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration (occurs without a membrane). Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane, from a region of more water (dilute/lower solute) to less water (concentrated/higher solute) until concentrations equalise. Example: potato piece in plain water swells (water enters); potato piece in 20% salt solution shrinks (water exits). In plants, water from soil enters root cells by osmosis.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.5: Experimental set-up showing potato pieces in Beaker A (plain water — piece swells) and Beaker B (20% salt solution — piece shrinks). Initial and final states shown.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Three solution types based on solute concentration relative to cell contents: (1) Isotonic — extracellular solute concentration = intracellular; no net water movement, cell unchanged. (2) Hypotonic — extracellular solute < intracellular; water enters cell, cell swells. (3) Hypertonic — extracellular solute > intracellular; water exits cell, cell shrinks. In plant cells placed in hypertonic solution, the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall (plasmolysis), but cell shape is maintained due to rigid cell wall.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.6: Three diagrams showing effect of isotonic, hypotonic, and hypertonic solutions on a cell — cell unchanged, swollen, or shrunken respectively.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Present in plant, fungal, and bacterial cells; absent in animal cells. Located outside the cell membrane. Functions: provides rigidity, maintains shape, withstands environmental stresses (wind, rain), keeps leaves and flowers firm, helps plants stay upright. Permeable (not selectively permeable) — water and dissolved minerals can pass through.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Most cells have three basic parts: plasma membrane, cytoplasm (semi-fluid, jelly-like), and nucleus. In eukaryotic cells, the cytoplasm contains membrane-bound organelles (nucleus, ER, Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, lysosomes, plastids, vacuoles) and ribosomes. A cytoskeleton of fine fibres provides structural support and enables movement. Cytoplasm may also contain cell inclusions (starch granules, calcium oxalate or silica crystals in some plant cells).
diagram_description: Fig. 2.10: Diagrams of (a) bacterial cell showing nucleoid, cytoplasm, cell wall, cell membrane, ribosome, appendage for locomotion; (b) plant cell showing cell wall, cell membrane, vacuole, chloroplast, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, nucleus, nucleolus, cytoplasm; (c) animal cell showing cell membrane, nucleus, nucleolus, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, Golgi body, ribosome, lysosome, vacuole, cytoplasm. Students must identify structures present/absent in each cell type.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Prokaryotic cells (pro = primitive, karyon = nucleus): no well-defined nucleus; genetic material in nucleoid region (single circular DNA molecule with proteins); no membrane-bound organelles; most cellular activities in cytoplasm; typically unicellular; diameter 1–10 µm. Example: bacteria. Eukaryotic cells (eu = true, karyon = nucleus): well-defined membrane-bound nucleus; several membrane-bound organelles; can be unicellular or multicellular; diameter 10–100 µm. Examples: plant cells, animal cells, yeast.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.10a vs 2.10b/c: Bacterial cell (prokaryote) compared with plant and animal cells (eukaryotes). Table 2.1 and Table 2.2 compare structures present/absent in each type.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell. Surrounded by a double-layered nuclear membrane with pores (allow material transfer between nucleus and cytoplasm). Contains nucleolus (dense round body; site of ribosomal subunit synthesis — subunits exit to cytoplasm where they assemble into ribosomes). Contains chromosomes (rod-shaped, visible only during cell division) made of DNA and specific proteins.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.11: Structure of nucleus showing double-layered nuclear membrane, nuclear pores, nucleolus, chromatin. Fig. 2.12: Hierarchy from nucleus → chromatin → chromosome → DNA.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Ribosomes are tiny structures that are the sites of protein synthesis. They can be found freely in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER). Ribosomal subunits are synthesised in the nucleolus, exit to the cytoplasm, and assemble (one large + one small subunit) to form functional ribosomes. Present in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) is a large network-like organelle in the cytoplasm, continuous with the outer membrane of the nuclear envelope. It plays a key role in synthesis and transport of proteins, fats (lipids), and some hormones. Two types: (1) Rough ER (RER) — has ribosomes on surface; looks rough under EM; involved in protein synthesis and secretion (e.g., pancreatic gland cells). (2) Smooth ER (SER) — no ribosomes; looks smooth; involved in synthesis and storage of fats and hormones.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.13: Shows rough ER (with ribosomes on surface) and smooth ER connected to Golgi apparatus, with vesicles budding off toward plasma membrane. Pathway for protein processing and secretion is illustrated.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Stacks of flattened, sac-like structures forming the Golgi apparatus. Functionally linked to ER, cell membrane, and other organelles. Acts as the cell's post office: modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and/or lipids into vesicles for transport, secretion, or lysosome formation. First observed in 1898 by Italian scientist Camillo Golgi in nerve cells of a barn owl (confirmed later by electron microscopy).
diagram_description: Fig. 2.13: Golgi apparatus shown as stacked flattened sacs, receiving material from ER, with vesicles budding off toward plasma membrane.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Lysosomes are single membrane-bound sacs filled with enzymes that break down unwanted proteins, carbohydrates, fats, and damaged cell parts (keeping the cell clean and healthy). Breakdown products are released into cytoplasm for reuse. Formed via the Golgi apparatus. Note: Human sperm cells contain lysosomal enzymes that break down the outer layer of the egg during fertilisation.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Mitochondria are the 'powerhouses of the cell' — they supply energy for most cellular activities. Each mitochondrion has two membranes: outer membrane (smooth, porous) and inner membrane (folded into finger-like projections called cristae — increase surface area for chemical reactions). Glucose and other molecules are broken down during cellular respiration to release energy stored as ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate — the energy currency of the cell). Mitochondria have their own DNA and ribosomes, so they can make some of their own proteins.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.14: Structure of mitochondrion showing outer membrane, inner membrane, cristae (finger-like folds of inner membrane), intermembrane space, ribosomes, and DNA.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Plastids are organelles found only in plant cells, used for food synthesis and storage. Three types: (1) Chloroplasts — double membrane-bound; contain chlorophyll (green pigment) that absorbs sunlight for photosynthesis; have stroma (semi-fluid) with disc-shaped membrane structures containing chlorophyll; sugars and starch granules stored in stroma; have own DNA and ribosomes. (2) Chromoplasts — contain pigments other than chlorophyll (yellow, orange, red); responsible for colours in flower petals and fruits; attract pollinators and seed-dispersing animals.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.15: Structure of chloroplast showing outer membrane, inner membrane, stroma, ribosomes, DNA, and disc-shaped membrane structures (thylakoids) containing chlorophyll.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Vacuoles are membrane-bound storage organelles. In mature plant cells: one large central vacuole surrounded by a single selectively permeable membrane; filled with cell sap (watery fluid); stores water, minerals, sugars, and waste material. By storing large amounts of water, vacuoles maintain turgor pressure inside the cell, keeping plant cells firm. When a plant lacks water, vacuoles lose water → cells become less firm → plant wilts.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

aliases: How do Normal Cells Grow and Divide
summary: Cells grow and divide to replace old, dead, or damaged cells and for growth of the organism. Cells can grow only up to a certain size; growth of the organism occurs through cell division. Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells divide, but eukaryotic cells divide in a more controlled and orderly manner through the cell cycle. New cells are always formed from pre-existing cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.17: Microscope images of different stages of cell division in onion root tip cells.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Cell division is the process by which new cells form from pre-existing cells. It allows organisms to grow, repair damaged tissues, and reproduce. Some cells (e.g., skin cells) divide continuously. An estimated hundreds of billions of cells are replaced daily in the human body (~1% of total cells). Two major types: mitosis (growth, repair, asexual reproduction) and meiosis (sexual reproduction, genetic diversity).
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Mitosis is the most common type of cell division. One parent cell produces two genetically identical daughter cells. Each daughter cell receives the same DNA and the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell. Important for normal growth, repair, maintenance, and asexual reproduction.
diagram_description: Fig. 2.18: Diagram showing one parent cell dividing into two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same chromosome number.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Meiosis occurs only in cells of reproductive organs and produces gametes. The parent cell divides twice (two sequential divisions) to form four daughter cells, each with half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell. First division: chromosomes reduced to half in each daughter cell. Second division: similar to mitosis, each daughter cell divides into two → four cells with half chromosome number (haploid gametes).
diagram_description: Fig. 2.19: Two-step meiosis diagram: parent cell → two daughter cells (first division, chromosomes halved) → four gametes (second division). Shows reduction in chromosome number at each stage.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Cell Theory is the unifying principle of biology. Key contributors: Matthias Schleiden (1838, German botanist) — all plants made of cells; Theodor Schwann (1839, German zoologist) — all animals made of cells; Rudolf Virchow (1855, German scientist) — new cells arise only from pre-existing cells. Together they formulated the classical Cell Theory.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell - The Basic Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Three postulates of classical Cell Theory: (1) All living organisms are made up of one or more cells. (2) The cell is the basic unit of structure and function in living beings. (3) All cells arise from pre-existing cells. This theory unifies all biology from bacteria to humans and explains life's continuity through cell division.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Normal cells grow, divide in a controlled way, perform functions, and die when no longer needed (cells have a definite lifespan). In animal cells, cell division normally stops when cells contact neighbouring cells — this is contact inhibition. Cancer cells lose contact inhibition and divide uncontrollably, forming tumours (benign or malignant). Malignant/cancerous tumours can invade nearby tissues and spread to form new tumours.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Life is believed to have originated in water, possibly in small pools with changing conditions rather than oceans. Hot springs (e.g., Puga Valley, Ladakh) maintain near-boiling temperatures resembling early Earth ~3.5 billion years ago. Organisms there are thermophiles (heat-loving, unicellular bacteria).

builds_upon.concept_name: Conditions Making Earth Suitable for Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: In eukaryotic cells, a network of fine fibres forms the cytoskeleton, which provides structural support, maintains cell shape, and enables cell movement and internal transport. It is visible only under an electron microscope as a separate entity.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: In 2010, J. Craig Venter's team studied the complete DNA sequence of Mycoplasma mycoides and chemically synthesised an exact copy of its DNA. This synthetic DNA was inserted into a closely related bacterium from which the original DNA had been removed (cytoplasm and cell membrane kept intact). The cell grew and divided following instructions from the synthetic DNA, demonstrating that DNA controls the structure and activities of a cell.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Cell culture is the technique of growing plant or animal cells outside the body in special conditions. Cells are placed in a nutrient-rich medium under controlled temperature, pH, moisture, and sterile conditions. Used for studying cell function and producing biochemicals, food, medicines, and vaccines. Austrian botanist Gottlieb Haberlandt (1902) proposed totipotency — any living plant cell, even a fully mature one, can develop into a complete plant given suitable nutrients and conditions.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Exploring the Investigative World of Science

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Programmed Cell Death (PCD) is a genetically regulated and organised process of selective cell destruction essential for normal development, cellular quality control, and immune function. Example: during embryo development, PCD eliminates cells between digits to form separate fingers — without it, we would have webbed hands.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Viruses, viroids, and prions are acellular (no cells) infectious agents too small to be seen under a light microscope. Viruses: composed of genetic material with a protein coat. Viroids: lack protein coat around genetic material. Prions: misfolded proteins that lack genetic material entirely.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

summary: Arun Kumar Sharma was a famous Indian scientist (botanist) known for his work on chromosomes, plant taxonomy, evolution, and development. He invented useful lab methods to study chromosomes in plants and received honours including the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Award and Padma Bhushan.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology
chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subtopic_names: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms
Why Plant and Animal Tissues Differ
summary: A tissue is a group of structurally similar cells that work together to perform a specific function. In multicellular organisms, cells form tissues, tissues form organs, organs form organ systems, and organ systems form the organism. This hierarchy leads to division of labour, increasing the efficiency of the body.

builds_upon.concept_name: Levels of Organisation in Living Organisms
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology

summary: A tissue is a group of cells similar in structure that work together to perform a specific function. In unicellular organisms like amoeba, a single cell performs all life functions, whereas in multicellular organisms, groups of cells specialise for different functions.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

concept_name: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms
summary: Division of labour means different groups of cells perform different functions, increasing the body's efficiency and enabling complex life processes. For example, muscle tissue enables movement while nervous tissue carries messages in animals, and xylem transports water while phloem transports food in plants.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

concept_name: Why Plant and Animal Tissues Differ
summary: Plants are fixed in one place and have rigid cell walls for support, while animals can move and have flexible cells suited for locomotion. Plants synthesise food via photosynthesis, while animals digest food from external sources, leading to distinct tissue types. Growth patterns also differ because tissues responsible for growth differ in structure and function.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Meristematic tissues consist of actively dividing cells responsible for growth in plants. Plants have three types — apical meristem (length), lateral meristem (girth), and intercalary meristem (regrowth after cutting). These cells are small with thin walls, dense cytoplasm, a large nucleus, and no vacuoles, enabling continuous rapid division.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.3 shows the location of shoot apical meristem and root apical meristem in a sapling.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Apical meristems are located at the tips of roots and shoots and are responsible for increase in the length (height) of the plant. Cells at root and shoot tips divide continuously, as demonstrated by cutting onion root tips stopping further root growth. Both root apical meristem and shoot apical meristem contribute to growth in length.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.3 shows shoot apical meristem at the top and root apical meristem at the bottom of a sapling; Fig. 3.2 shows a graph of onion root growth (Jar A vs Jar B) after root tips are cut.
subject: biology

summary: Lateral meristem is arranged in a ring along the circumference of dicot stems and is responsible for increase in girth (diameter) of the stem. Cells divide and produce new cells inward and outward in a concentric manner. Annual growth rings visible in cut tree trunks are a result of lateral meristem activity, and their count indicates the age of the tree.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.4 shows a T.S. of a tree trunk with visible annual growth rings on the cut surface.
subject: biology

summary: Intercalary meristem is located at the base of internodes or just above nodes and enables regrowth after cutting or grazing. When the tip of a stem is cut, the stem stops growing in length but new branches arise from nodes due to intercalary meristem activity. This is why grass regrows after mowing and hedges become bushy after trimming.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.5 shows new branches arising from the node of a stem after the tip is cut; Fig. 3.6 shows lawn mowing.
subject: biology

summary: Meristematic cells are small with thin cell walls, a large and prominent nucleus, and dense cytoplasm with many organelles. Vacuoles are generally absent and cells are tightly packed with little or no intercellular space. These features allow continuous and rapid cell division.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Differentiation is the process by which meristematic cells lose the ability to divide and undergo changes in structure and function to become specialised permanent tissues. Some newly formed cells remain meristematic while others differentiate to perform specific functions such as support, transport, or storage. Permanent tissues can be simple (one cell type) or complex (more than one cell type).
subject: biology

summary: Permanent tissues are formed from meristematic cells that have differentiated and lost the ability to divide. They are classified as simple (one cell type: parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma) or complex (more than one cell type: xylem, phloem). Functionally, plant tissues are categorised as protective, supporting, or conducting.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.7 shows the internal structure of a sunflower stem T.S. labelling epidermis, cuticle, collenchyma, parenchyma, sclerenchyma, phloem, lateral meristem, xylem, and ground tissue.
subject: biology

summary: The epidermis is the outermost, tightly packed single layer of flat rectangular cells that protects all parts of the plant. It is covered by a waxy layer called the cuticle, which reduces water loss and protects against mechanical injury and parasites. Roots have root hair projections for absorption, and leaves have stomata for gaseous exchange and transpiration.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.7 labels the epidermis, epidermal hair, and cuticle on the outer layer of the sunflower stem T.S.
subject: biology

summary: Parenchyma consists of living cells with thin walls that are loosely packed with intercellular spaces. It mainly stores food and also performs photosynthesis in green parts of the plant. In aquatic plants, specialised parenchyma forms air spaces that help the plant float.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.8a shows parenchyma cells with thin walls and visible intercellular spaces.
subject: biology

summary: Collenchyma consists of living cells with unevenly thickened corners due to deposition of pectin, a chemical that gives flexibility like rubber. This tissue provides support and flexibility, allowing stems and tendrils to bend without breaking. It is found in the young stems and leaf stalks of plants like coriander.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.8b shows collenchyma cells with thick, unevenly thickened walls at corners.
subject: biology

summary: Sclerenchyma cells have very thick walls due to deposition of lignin, making them hard and strong, and most are dead at maturity. This tissue forms the woody structure and is found in stems, leaf veins, and hard coverings of seeds and nuts such as coconut husk and walnut shell. It provides rigidity and strength but no flexibility.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.8c shows sclerenchyma cells with thick lignified walls.
subject: biology

summary: Xylem is a complex permanent tissue that transports water and minerals from roots to other parts of the plant and also provides structural strength. It consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibres; xylem parenchyma is the only living component, while tracheids, vessels, and xylem fibres are primarily sclerenchymatous. Tracheids and vessels are tubular and thick-walled, facilitating upward water transport.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.9a shows xylem structure with labelled xylem fibre, tracheid, xylem parenchyma, and vessel.
subject: biology

summary: Phloem is a complex permanent tissue that transports food (sugars) prepared in leaves to other parts of the plant, and is mostly made up of living cells. It consists of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem parenchyma, and phloem fibres; sieve tubes are long tubular cells joined end to end by perforated walls. Companion cells regulate the cellular functions of sieve tube cells and monitor loading and unloading of sugars, while phloem fibres provide structural strength.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.9b shows phloem structure with labelled phloem parenchyma, sieve tube, sieve pore, and companion cell.
subject: biology

summary: Plant tissues are organised into three tissue systems: the dermal tissue system (outer covering, protection, reduces water loss), the ground tissue system (main body between dermal and vascular tissues, includes parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma), and the vascular tissue system (conducting tissues — xylem and phloem). These three systems are present in the root, stem, and leaf, each with distinct arrangement.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.10 shows tissue system organisation in root, stem, and leaf cross-sections, labelling epidermal tissue, ground tissue, xylem, and phloem.
subject: biology

summary: Animal tissues are mainly of four types: epithelial, connective, muscular, and nervous tissues, each adapted to perform specific functions. Epithelial tissue provides covering and lining; connective tissue connects and supports; muscular tissue produces movement; nervous tissue receives and transmits messages. Together these tissues coordinate all body activities.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Epithelial tissue forms the outer covering of the body and lines internal organs such as the mouth, lungs, blood vessels, and intestine, with closely packed cells and very little intercellular space. Structural types are adapted to function: flat cells for rapid diffusion, layered cells for protection, cuboidal or columnar cells for secretion, and ciliated or tall cells for absorption and movement. Together these adaptations prevent pathogen entry, reduce water loss, and facilitate absorption, secretion, and substance transport.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.11 shows five types of epithelial tissues (a–e) in different parts of the body, illustrating exchange, protection, secretion, sensory, and absorption types.
subject: biology

summary: Connective tissue connects and supports other tissues; both blood and bones are connective tissues that differ due to the nature of their matrix — watery in blood, rigid in bone. Blood (with RBCs, WBCs, platelets, and plasma) transports nutrients and gases; bone provides structural support; cartilage provides flexibility and cushioning at joints; tendons connect muscle to bone; ligaments connect bone to bone and limit excessive movement.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.12 shows (a) components of blood including WBC, RBCs, platelets, and plasma; (b) types of bones (collar bone, long bone, kneecap); (c) arrangement of tendon, cartilage, and ligament at a joint.
subject: biology

summary: Muscular tissue produces voluntary and involuntary movements; there are three types. Skeletal (striated) muscles are attached to the skeleton, have long cylindrical multinucleate unbranched fibres, and control voluntary movements. Smooth muscles in organs like the stomach and intestines have spindle-shaped uninucleate cells without striations and control involuntary slow movements. Cardiac muscle, found only in the heart, has cylindrical branched fibres with a single nucleus and faint striations, beating rhythmically and continuously without fatigue.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.13 shows three types of muscles: skeletal muscle (striated, cylindrical, unbranched), smooth muscle (spindle-shaped), and cardiac muscle (cylindrical, branched).
subject: biology

summary: Nervous tissue forms the body's control and coordination network, enabling sensing, communication, and response. Its cells, called neurons (nerve cells), have three main parts: the cell body (contains nucleus, controls cell activities), dendrites (receive signals), and an axon (long fibre carrying messages to axon terminals that transmit signals to other cells). The brain acts as the control centre, and neurons signal muscles for both voluntary and involuntary actions.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.14 shows the structure of a neuron with labelled cell body, nucleus, dendrites, axon, and axon terminals.
subject: biology

summary: The musculoskeletal system consists of bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments, and functions under the control of the nervous system. Muscles pull on bones via tendons to produce movement at joints; the adult human skeleton makes up about 12–15 per cent of body weight. This system helps in standing upright, movement, maintaining posture, and protecting delicate organs.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.15 shows the musculoskeletal system in the human body with labelled cartilage, bones, joint, tendon, ligament, and muscles.
subject: biology

summary: A joint is a junction between two or more bones that allows movement. The ball and socket joint (shoulder, hip) allows movement in all directions; the hinge joint (elbow, knee) allows bending in one direction; the pivot joint (neck-skull) allows side-to-side rotation; fixed joints (skull) allow no movement and protect the brain. The type of joint determines the range and direction of movement possible.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.16 shows types of joints on a human skeleton diagram, labelling fixed joint (skull), pivot joint (neck), ball and socket joints (shoulder, hip), and hinge joints (elbow, knee).
subject: biology

summary: The skeletal system provides a framework of bones for strength and protection of delicate organs, including the skull, vertebral column (spine), and rib cage. The vertebral column is made up of small bones called vertebrae with cartilage discs between them for cushioning and flexibility, protecting the spinal cord. The rib cage (12 pairs of ribs) protects the heart and lungs and can expand and contract during breathing due to flexible cartilage connecting ribs to the sternum.
subject: biology

summary: Totipotency is the ability of a mature plant cell to dedifferentiate (regain the ability to divide), form an undifferentiated mass of cells, and then redifferentiate into a complete plant under specific conditions. F. C. Steward (1958) demonstrated this using phloem cells of carrot grown in nutrient medium with simple sugars and hormones. Sipra Guha Mukherjee and S. C. Maheshwari made a breakthrough in plant tissue culture by developing a complete plant through anther culture, contributing to crop improvement.
diagram_description: Fig. 3.19 shows the sequential steps of carrot plant regeneration from phloem fragments through single cell suspension, embryonic plant development, plantlet on agar, and finally an adult plant.
subject: biology

summary: As plants grow older, cells below the epidermis develop the ability to divide and act as lateral meristematic cells, forming the cork cambium. Cork cambium produces cork cells that are dead, compactly arranged, and contain a substance making them impermeable to water and gases, forming the bark of the tree. This replaces the single-layered epidermis as the protective outer covering in older plants.
subject: biology

summary: Asexual reproduction involves a single parent producing offspring that are genetically identical to the parent. The central cell division mechanism is mitosis, which produces daughter cells with the same chromosome number as the parent. It is seen in bacteria, amoeba, yeast, hydra, sponge, and many plants.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Vegetative propagation is a form of asexual reproduction in plants where new plants arise from vegetative parts such as stems, leaves, or roots. Methods include cutting, grafting, layering, and tissue culture, all producing genetically identical plants. Examples include potato, ginger, money plant, sugarcane, and Bryophyllum.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.1 shows Bryophyllum leaf sprouting tiny plantlets; Fig. 11.2 shows cutting technique; Fig. 11.3 shows steps of grafting with Plant A (rootstock) and Plant B (scion) joined at a slit; Fig. 11.4 shows layering of a twig buried in soil.
subject: biology

summary: Budding is a form of asexual reproduction where repeated cell division at a specific site on the parent body produces a small outgrowth called a bud. The bud enlarges and eventually separates from the parent to live independently. It is seen in yeast (unicellular) and hydra (multicellular), and multiple buds can form on one parent simultaneously.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.6 shows yeast cells with small round outgrowths (buds); Fig. 11.7 shows budding in hydra with buds visible on the parent body.

builds_upon.concept_name: Asexual Reproduction in Microbes and Simple Animals
builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Spore formation is an asexual reproductive method in which organisms like fungi produce millions of lightweight, usually single-celled spores in sac-like structures or on swollen vesicles on fungal hyphae. Spores float through air currents and germinate into new individuals when moisture and nutrients are available. Examples include bread mould fungi Rhizopus and Aspergillus.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.8 shows (a) Rhizopus with sac and spores labelled, and (b) Aspergillus with hyphae and spore-bearing structures.

builds_upon.concept_name: Asexual Reproduction in Microbes and Simple Animals
builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Sexual reproduction involves two parents contributing genetic material to form a new individual, producing offspring with a mix of characteristics. It requires the formation of specialised cells called gametes through meiosis, followed by their fusion (fertilisation) to form a zygote. This mixing of genetic material creates variation among individuals, which is important for adaptation and evolution.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Meiosis is a special type of cell division that reduces the chromosome number of a parent cell (diploid) to half (haploid) in the resulting gametes. In humans, body cells have 46 chromosomes, but gametes (sperm and eggs) have 23. The random segregation of chromosome pairs during meiosis creates enormous genetic variation among gametes — with 23 chromosome pairs, over 8 million combinations are possible.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.9 shows three pairs of coloured beads (green, blue, red) representing chromosome pairs segregating into gametes during meiosis, illustrating how one combination (blonde and straight hair with brown eyes) is formed.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: In angiosperms, the flower is the reproductive organ, consisting of four whorls: sepals (protective), petals (attract pollinators), stamen (male part with filament and anther producing pollen), and pistil (female part with stigma, style, and ovary containing ovules with egg cells). Flowers may be complete (having all four parts) or incomplete. The stamen produces pollen grains carrying male gametes, while the ovule in the ovary contains the female gamete (egg cell).
diagram_description: Fig. 11.10 shows a longitudinal section of a flower with all parts labelled: sepal, petal, stamen (anther and filament), and pistil (stigma, style, ovary); Fig. 11.11 shows the structure of a pistil with stigma, style, ovary, and ovule labelled.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of a flower. Self-pollination occurs when pollen reaches the stigma of the same flower or another flower on the same plant, while cross-pollination involves pollen transfer between flowers of different plants of the same species. Pollination depends on external agents (pollinators) such as wind, water, insects (bees, butterflies), and birds, each associated with specific flower adaptations.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.13 shows diagrams of self-pollination (pollen transferred within same flower) and cross-pollination (pollen transferred between two different flowers on different plants), with stigma and anther labelled.
subject: biology

summary: After pollination, pollen grains germinate on the stigma and produce a pollen tube that grows through the style into the ovary, delivering the male gamete to fuse with the egg cell in the ovule — this fusion is fertilisation, forming a zygote. The zygote develops into an embryo, the ovule becomes the seed, and the ovary develops into the fruit. Seeds are dispersed by wind, water, or animals, and germinate under favourable conditions to grow into new plants.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.14 shows germination of a pollen grain on stigma with pollen tube growing down to ovule, labelling male germ cell, pollen tube, ovary, ovule, and egg cell; Fig. 11.15 shows transformation of an ovary into a fruit.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Sexual reproduction in animals involves the fusion of male and female gametes, with fertilisation occurring either outside (external) or inside (internal) the female body. External fertilisation is common in aquatic animals like frogs and fish, producing large numbers of eggs with low survival rates; internal fertilisation in reptiles, birds, and mammals provides greater protection and higher survival of offspring. The mode of fertilisation, number of eggs, and parental care vary widely across animal groups.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.17 shows the life cycle of a butterfly with stages: egg, larva, pupa, emergence, and adult.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: External fertilisation occurs when the female releases eggs into water and the male releases sperm over them; it is seen in frogs and most fish and results in many eggs being lost to predators or currents. Internal fertilisation occurs inside the female body and is seen in reptiles, birds, and mammals, giving the fertilised egg or embryo greater protection and a higher survival rate. The number of eggs produced is inversely related to the degree of parental protection.
subject: biology

summary: Animals show varied reproductive strategies balancing the number of eggs produced against offspring survival. Fish, amphibians, and insects produce hundreds to thousands of eggs; the yolk provides just enough nutrition for a larva to hatch, which then feeds independently before metamorphosing into an adult (e.g., butterfly, frog). Reptiles and birds produce fewer eggs with sufficient yolk for full development; mammals develop the zygote internally and typically provide extended post-birth care including breastfeeding.
subject: biology

summary: Human reproduction is sexual, involving the fusion of sperm (male gamete) and egg (female gamete) to form a zygote that develops into a foetus in the uterus. Reproductive organs mature during puberty, when physical and hormonal changes equip the body for reproduction. The process includes gametogenesis, fertilisation, implantation, pregnancy lasting about nine months, and childbirth.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: The male reproductive system produces sperm in the testes, housed in the scrotum which keeps them slightly cooler than body temperature — a requirement for sperm formation. Sperm travel through the vas deferens to the urethra; glands such as the seminal vesicles and prostate add fluids to nourish and activate sperm. Each sperm has a head containing genetic material and a tail for motility, and the testes also produce hormones that regulate puberty changes in males.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.18 shows the male reproductive system with labelled parts: testis, scrotum, vas deferens, urethra, prostate gland, seminal vesicle, urinary bladder, and penis.
subject: biology

summary: The female reproductive system consists of a pair of ovaries (producing eggs and hormones), oviducts/fallopian tubes (connecting ovaries to the uterus), the uterus (where the foetus develops), and the vagina. The uterus opens into the vagina through the cervix. Ovaries also produce hormones that bring about puberty changes in females.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.19 shows the female reproductive system with labelled parts: ovary, fallopian tube, uterus, cervix, and vagina.
subject: biology

summary: Gametogenesis is the formation of gametes (sperm and eggs) in the testes and ovaries respectively, through meiosis, which halves the chromosome number from 46 to 23. In males it produces millions of small, motile sperm; in females it produces a single large, non-motile egg. This size, number, and motility asymmetry between male and female gametes is characteristic across most animals.
subject: biology

summary: During ovulation, a mature egg is released from an ovary and travels to the oviduct; if a sperm fuses with it, a zygote is formed (fertilisation). The zygote undergoes mitotic divisions and implants into the thickened uterine lining, marking the start of pregnancy. Human pregnancy lasts approximately nine months, divided into three trimesters: first (embryo formation, organ development), second (foetal growth, mother feels movements), and third (rapid growth, birth preparation), ending in childbirth.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.20 shows egg + sperm = zygote; Fig. 11.22 shows stages of pregnancy across three trimesters with foetal development images.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: The menstrual cycle is a roughly 28-day cycle (range 21–35 days) beginning at puberty (ages 10–14) and continuing until menopause (around age 50). It involves four key stages: menstruation (Days 1–5, shedding of uterine lining), rebuilding of lining and egg maturation (Days 6–14), ovulation on approximately Day 14, and thickening of lining awaiting possible fertilisation (Days 15–28). If no fertilisation occurs, the lining sheds again as menstruation.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.21 shows key stages of the menstrual cycle across a typical 28-day period as four labelled boxes: menstruation (Days 1–5), uterine lining rebuilds (Days 6–14), ovulation (Day 14), and uterine lining thickens (Days 15–28).
subject: biology

summary: Contraceptive methods prevent unwanted pregnancy and include barrier methods (condoms, vaginal covers), hormonal methods (oral contraceptive pills), and intrauterine devices (IUDs such as Copper-T); surgical methods block the vas deferens or fallopian tubes permanently. Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) such as gonorrhoea, herpes, syphilis, genital warts, and HIV are transmitted through sexual contact, and condoms help prevent their spread. Prenatal sex determination is prohibited by law in India to prevent sex-selective abortion and maintain a healthy sex ratio.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.23 shows a Copper-T Intra-Uterine Device placed in the uterus.
subject: biology

summary: Tissue culture propagates plants from the shoot tip (apical meristem), producing mass quantities of genetically identical, virus-free plantlets. This technique has revolutionised banana farming by eliminating virus-infected plants and ensuring high yields. It is an example of asexual reproduction applied at an industrial scale.
diagram_description: Fig. 11.5 shows a jar of tissue-cultured plantlets alongside a banana plantation.
subject: biology

summary: In-vitro fertilisation (IVF) is a medical technique where an egg and sperm are combined outside the female body in a laboratory dish, and the fertilised egg is implanted in the uterus. A baby born through this method is commonly called a test tube baby. India's first IVF baby was born in 1978 through the work of Subhash Mukhopadhyay of Kolkata; P. Maheshwari pioneered in-vitro fertilisation techniques in flowering plants.
subject: biology

summary: Biological sex in humans is determined by sex chromosomes: females have XX and males have XY. The mother always contributes an X chromosome, while the father contributes either an X (resulting in female offspring) or a Y (resulting in male offspring). Therefore, it is the father's contribution that determines the biological sex of the baby.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology

summary: Biodiversity refers to the immense variety of living organisms found on Earth, from microscopic bacteria to giant trees. Every organism plays a role in keeping ecosystems stable and functioning, such as algae producing oxygen, fungi decomposing matter, and bees pollinating flowers. Humans depend on biodiversity for food, shelter, medicines and livelihoods.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Biodiversity is the immense variety of living organisms found on Earth across countless forms and habitats. It is essential because every organism plays an interconnected role — algae produce oxygen, fungi and bacteria decompose waste, and birds and bees pollinate flowers. These interconnections sustain ecosystems and make Earth suitable for life.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: India's diverse landscape — mountains, deserts, rainforests, plateaus and coastlines — supports a wide variety of species. Regions that support large numbers of endemic species and have undergone significant habitat loss are called biodiversity hotspots. Examples include the Western Ghats, Indo-Burma (including North East India), the Himalayas and Sundaland (including the Nicobar Islands).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.1 shows four Indian endemic species: (a) Nilgiri tahr, (b) Lion-tailed macaque, (c) Nepenthes khasiana, and (d) Neelakurinji.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Endemic species are those restricted to particular regions of the world and not found naturally anywhere else. Examples from India include the Nilgiri tahr, Lion-tailed macaque, Nepenthes khasiana and Neelakurinji. Protecting regions rich in endemic species is critical for biodiversity conservation.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.1 illustrates four endemic species of India: Nilgiri tahr, Lion-tailed macaque, Nepenthes khasiana, and Neelakurinji.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: The biodiversity seen today is the outcome of continuous changes over vast spans of time, shaped by interactions between organisms and their surroundings. Small differences among individuals affected survival and reproduction, accumulated over generations and gave rise to new life forms. Similar features in organisms suggest evolution from common ancestors.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Biological classification is the scientific system of grouping living organisms based on shared characteristics and evolutionary relationships. It helps organise information, understand relationships among organisms, identify newly discovered species and support biodiversity conservation. Classification follows a hierarchical order from Kingdom down to Species.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Classification is necessary to manage the millions of organisms on Earth in a systematic and organised way, just as a library organises books. It helps scientists understand similarities and differences, identify and name new organisms, support conservation of threatened species and provide a common language for scientists worldwide.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Scientists use multiple characteristics to classify organisms: external features (shape, size), mode of nutrition (autotrophic or heterotrophic), internal structures (skeletal patterns, organs, tissues), cell structure (unicellular/multicellular, eukaryote/prokaryote, presence of cell wall), ecological role (producer, consumer, decomposer), reproduction method and genetic similarity via DNA. Broader, more fundamental features like cell type are prioritised over superficial characteristics.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Classification systems evolved from Aristotle's habitat-based grouping (4th century BCE) to the two kingdom system (Plantae and Animalia) by Linnaeus in 1758. Haeckel added Protista in 1866 for unicellular organisms, Copeland added Monera in 1938 for prokaryotes, and Whittaker proposed the five kingdom system in 1969, adding Fungi. Each revision addressed limitations revealed by new observations and technologies.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.4 shows a timeline of classification systems from Aristotle (4th century BCE) through Linnaeus, Haeckel, Copeland to Whittaker (1969) with the kingdoms each system proposed.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Whittaker's five kingdom classification (1969) groups all life into Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia based on cell type (prokaryote/eukaryote), level of organisation (unicellular/multicellular), cell structure (presence/absence of cell wall and its composition) and mode of nutrition (autotrophic/heterotrophic). This system is a simple and widely used framework to understand the diversity of life.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.5 is a concept map showing the five kingdom classification branching by cell type, level of organisation, cell structure, mode of nutrition and ecological role, with examples for each kingdom.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Monera includes all unicellular prokaryotes — organisms with a primitive nucleus lacking a membrane-bound envelope — such as bacteria, Archaea and cyanobacteria. Bacteria are found in diverse environments including soil, water, hot springs and inside living bodies, and play roles as pathogens, decomposers and mutualists (e.g., Lactobacillus, Rhizobium). Cyanobacteria are autotrophic and were among the first organisms to produce oxygen through photosynthesis.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.6 shows members of Kingdom Monera including various bacterial cell shapes and cyanobacteria.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Protista includes all unicellular eukaryotes — organisms with a true membrane-bound nucleus — that live in water or moist places and may or may not have a cell wall. Examples include Amoeba, Paramecium, Euglena and Chlamydomonas. Some protists are autotrophic, others heterotrophic; they form an important link in aquatic food chains and some function as decomposers.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.7 shows microscopic illustrations of common protists: Amoeba, Paramecium and Euglena.

builds_upon.concept_name: Variation in Shape and Structure of Cells
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Fungi consists mostly of multicellular eukaryotes (with yeast as a unicellular exception) that have cell walls made of chitin and obtain nutrients by absorbing from dead or decaying matter through a network of filaments called mycelium. Most fungi are saprophytes that decompose dead organic matter, playing a vital ecological role in nutrient recycling; others are parasitic or mutualistic. Common examples include yeast, mushrooms and Aspergillus; some fungi produce antibiotics like Penicillium.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.8 shows common fungi: (a) Yeast, (b) Aspergillus, and (c) Mushrooms.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Plantae comprises multicellular autotrophic eukaryotes that perform photosynthesis and have rigid cell walls primarily made of cellulose. Plants form the base of most food chains and release oxygen essential for life. Kingdom Plantae is divided into five classes: Thallophyta, Bryophyta, Pteridophyta, Gymnosperm and Angiosperm.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Animalia consists of multicellular heterotrophic eukaryotes without a cell wall that depend on other organisms for food. Most animals show locomotion, rapid response to stimuli and coordinated behaviour. Animals are classified into non-chordata (invertebrates, lacking a notochord) and chordata (vertebrates and protochordates, possessing a notochord at some stage).
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Plantae is divided into five classes showing increasing structural complexity and decreasing dependence on water: Thallophyta (algae, simple body), Bryophyta (mosses, first land plants), Pteridophyta (ferns, with vascular tissue), Gymnosperms (seed plants with naked seeds) and Angiosperms (flowering plants with seeds enclosed in fruits). Each group represents evolutionary adaptations that improved survival on land.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Thallophytes are the simplest plant forms, mostly found in water or moist environments, forming a simple undifferentiated body called a thallus that allows direct exchange of gases, nutrients and water with the surroundings. An example is Spirogyra. They cannot survive on land and are well-adapted to aquatic habitats.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.9 shows Spirogyra, an example of Thallophyta.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Bryophytes such as mosses and liverworts represent the first plants to colonise land, though they remain strongly dependent on moisture. They have a slightly differentiated body with root-like rhizoids but lack true vascular tissues (xylem and phloem) and require water for reproduction, earning them the name 'amphibians of the plant kingdom'. Examples include Marchantia and mosses.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.10 shows members of class Bryophyta: (a) Marchantia and (b) Moss.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Pteridophytes such as ferns possess true roots, stems and leaves, and have specialised vascular tissues — xylem for water transport and phloem for food transport — throughout the plant. They represent a major adaptation to land life but still require water for reproduction and do not produce seeds. A cross section of a fern stem shows these vascular tissues.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.11 shows (a) a fern plant and (b) a cross section of a fern stem with xylem and phloem labelled.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Gymnosperms (gymnos = naked, spermos = seed) such as pines and cycads are well-adapted to cold and dry regions with needle-like or scale-like leaves that reduce water loss.
