They produce seeds that protect the embryo and contain stored food, and do not require water for fertilisation. Their seeds are not enclosed in fruits and are typically exposed on cones.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.12 shows a cone of pine, an example gymnosperm.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Angiosperms (angeion = vessel, spermos = seed) or flowering plants are the most complex and diverse plant group, producing flowers that attract pollinators and fruits that enclose seeds and aid dispersal. These features allow angiosperms to occupy a wide range of environments. They are further divided into monocots and dicots based on leaf venation and other features.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.13 shows an angiosperm tree (Gulmohar) as an example of the most diverse plant group.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Kingdom Animalia is classified based on the presence or absence of a notochord (a flexible rod-shaped structure) into non-chordata (invertebrates) and chordata (protochordates and vertebrates). Invertebrates show a progressive increase in body complexity from Porifera to Echinodermata. Vertebrates possess a vertebral column (backbone) and are classified into fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.16 shows the animal kingdom with images, habitats, levels of organisation and skeletal characteristics for Porifera, Cnidaria, Platyhelminthes, Nematoda, Annelida, Arthropoda, Mollusca and Echinodermata.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Invertebrates lack a notochord and show increasing complexity from sponges (Porifera, cellular level, no tissues) through Cnidaria (tissue level, tentacles), Platyhelminthes (bilateral symmetry, organ level), Nematoda (two openings, organ system level), Annelida (segmentation, body cavity), Arthropoda (jointed appendages, exoskeleton), Mollusca (soft body, organ system level) to Echinodermata (internal calcium carbonate skeleton). Each group introduced structural innovations improving feeding, movement and protection.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.16 presents all eight invertebrate phyla with representative images and a comparison table of habitat, level of organisation and skeleton type.

builds_upon.concept_name: Levels of Organisation in Living Organisms
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Protochordates (e.g., Amphioxus) possess a notochord at least once during their life, bridging invertebrates and vertebrates. Vertebrates possess a vertebral column (backbone) that supports the body, protects vital organs and allows complex organ systems; they are classified into fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals based on habitat, body covering and reproduction. The vertebral column is an internal skeletal structure that evolved from the notochord.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Taxonomic hierarchy organises organisms in a step-by-step order from broad groups (Kingdom) to specific ones (Species): Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species. At each lower level, organisms share more features. Each organism is given a unique two-part scientific name through binomial nomenclature introduced by Carolus Linnaeus.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.17 shows fan-shaped pyramids illustrating the hierarchical classification of a tiger and a pea plant from Kingdom to Species with all levels labelled.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Classification follows a hierarchical order from Kingdom (broadest) to Species (most specific), where each lower level contains organisms sharing more features in common. For example, a tiger is classified as Kingdom Animalia → Phylum Chordata → Sub-phylum Vertebrata → Class Mammalia → Order Carnivora → Family Felidae → Genus Panthera → Species P. tigris. Genus has fewer members than Class but more features in common.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.17 shows pyramids with levels from Kingdom at top to Species at base, using tiger (Panthera tigris) and pea plant (Pisum sativum) as examples.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Binomial nomenclature, introduced by Carolus Linnaeus in the 18th century, is the universal system of giving each organism a two-part scientific name written in Latin or a Latinised form. The first part is the genus name (capitalised) and the second is the species name (lowercase); the name is printed in italics or underlined when handwritten. For example, Panthera tigris (tiger) and Mangifera indica (mango).
diagram_description: Fig. 12.18 shows an example of a scientific name label on camphor (Cinnamomum camphora) illustrating binomial nomenclature format.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

concept_name: Diversity, Adaptation and Evidence of Evolution
summary: The diversity of life today reflects structural adaptations that evolved over millions of years in response to environmental challenges, with fossils providing preserved evidence of past life forms. Human activities such as pollution, deforestation, overuse of resources and climate change are currently threatening biodiversity. The loss of one species can cause cascading effects on others that depend on it.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

parent_concept_name: Diversity, Adaptation and Evidence of Evolution
summary: Fossils are preserved remains of plants and animals found in layers of rocks, sand and mud, with older layers generally containing simpler organisms and newer layers showing more complex forms. They act as natural records helping us understand how life has changed over millions of years. India has yielded important fossils ranging from dinosaurs to early humans and ancient plants, studied notably by Birbal Sahni.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

parent_concept_name: Diversity, Adaptation and Evidence of Evolution
summary: Animal diversity reflects a wide range of structural adaptations enabling survival in different environments: fins and gills allow fish to move and breathe in water, feathers and hollow bones make flight possible in birds, fat storage in camels and thick fur in polar bears support survival under extreme conditions, and mammary glands in mammals improve the survival of young ones. These structural features are outcomes of changes over long periods of time.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Carl Woese (1977) proposed the three domain system — Bacteria, Archaea and Eukarya — based on genetic data comparing organisms at the DNA level. This system revealed that microscopic life forms are far more diverse than previously believed and that the five kingdom system could not fully explain the diversity of life. Organisms with similar DNA are considered to have a common ancestry.
diagram_description: Fig. 12.19 shows the three domains of life — Bacteria, Archaea and Eukarya — branching from a common base.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) were among the first organisms to produce oxygen through photosynthesis about 2.5 billion years ago, allowing oxygen to accumulate in the atmosphere and making Earth suitable for other life forms. Fossils of ancient cyanobacteria are found in structures called stromatolites, discovered in Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh, providing some of the earliest evidence of life on Earth.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology
chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

summary: The Earth system is made up of five interacting spheres: geosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere. Living organisms and their habitats belong to the biosphere, which includes mangroves, forests, farms, ocean plankton, and coral reefs. A disturbance in any one sphere can cascade into changes in others.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.1 shows features of the Earth's surface illustrating examples of geosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: The biosphere encompasses all living organisms and their habitats on Earth, including mangroves, forests, farms, ocean plankton, and coral reefs. It interacts continuously with the geosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, and atmosphere. Disruptions such as rising sea levels or habitat loss directly affect the biosphere's ecosystems.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: A change in one of Earth's spheres triggers changes in the others, demonstrating their delicate balance. For example, warmer Arabian Sea water increases evaporation, causing monsoon fluctuations that affect the hydrosphere and biosphere. Melting glaciers (cryosphere) raise sea levels, threatening coastal ecosystems in the biosphere.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: The hydrosphere includes all liquid water on Earth such as oceans, rivers, lakes, and groundwater, while the cryosphere is the solid form of water including glaciers, snow, and polar ice caps. Snow from the cryosphere melts and becomes part of the hydrosphere, directly linking the two spheres. Reduced snowfall decreases lake levels and affects the availability of water for organisms.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Biogeochemical cycles describe the cyclic movement of matter and energy between the living (biotic) and non-living (abiotic) components of Earth. They ensure essential nutrients such as carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are recycled and remain available to support life. These cycles interconnect all of Earth's spheres and help ecosystems maintain environmental balance.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: A biogeochemical cycle is the cyclic movement of matter and energy between the abiotic (non-living) and biotic (living) components of Earth. These cycles make essential nutrients available to living organisms, sustain life, regulate climate, and balance ecosystems. The interconnectedness of these cycles helps ecosystems recover from disturbances.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: In the water cycle, water evaporates from water bodies and is released by plants through transpiration, then condenses to form clouds and returns as precipitation. Water dissolves minerals from soil and rocks, transporting nutrients to oceans to support marine life and terrestrial organisms. Climate change intensifies the water cycle, causing heavier monsoons in some regions and droughts in others.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.12 illustrates the water cycle with labels for condensation, clouds, precipitation, evaporation, transpiration, run off, and ground water.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Carbon forms the backbone of life, present in every protein, carbohydrate, fat, and DNA molecule. In the fast cycle, plants convert atmospheric CO2 into glucose via photosynthesis, and CO2 is returned through respiration and decomposition. In the slow cycle, dead organisms become fossil fuels over millions of years, and burning these fuels rapidly releases CO2, intensifying the greenhouse effect and disrupting marine life including plankton.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.13 shows the carbon cycle with labels for CO2 in atmosphere, photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, absorption, CO2 in water, plankton, coal, gas, oil, and combustion of fossil fuels.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: The nitrogen cycle describes the movement of nitrogen between air, soil, water, and organisms, encompassing nitrogen fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification, and denitrification. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria such as Rhizobium (in legume root nodules) and Azotobacter convert atmospheric N2 into ammonia, which nitrifying bacteria like Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter convert to nitrites and nitrates for plant uptake. Decomposers return nitrogen to the soil through ammonification, while denitrifying bacteria such as Pseudomonas return nitrogen gas to the atmosphere, completing the cycle.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.15 shows the nitrogen cycle with labels for atmospheric N2, nitrogen fixation, ammonia, nitrification, nitrite, nitrate, assimilation, ammonification, denitrification, and decomposers.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: The oxygen cycle regulates atmospheric oxygen levels through a balance between consumption and production. Organisms use oxygen in respiration and release CO2, while combustion also consumes oxygen. Plants restore oxygen through photosynthesis, using sunlight, water, and CO2 to produce glucose and O2, sustaining life across all spheres of Earth.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.16 illustrates the oxygen cycle showing O2 in the atmosphere, photosynthesis by plants, plant and animal respiration, and industrial combustion releasing CO2.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

subtopic_names: Role of Photosynthesis in Energy Flow
concept_name: Human Impact on Earth's Biological Processes
summary: Human activities such as burning fossil fuels and deforestation disrupt biogeochemical cycles across Earth's spheres. Excess CO2 makes seawater more acidic, threatening plankton and coral reefs, while warmer oceans reduce the capacity of seas to act as carbon sinks. Overuse of fertilisers and deforestation further damage terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

parent_concept_name: Human Impact on Earth's Biological Processes
concept_name: Role of Photosynthesis in Energy Flow
summary: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use visible sunlight, water, and CO2 to produce glucose and release oxygen, making it the primary source of food for most organisms. It is the main mechanism restoring atmospheric oxygen and removing CO2, forming the foundation of the oxygen and carbon cycles. If photosynthesis stopped, oxygen levels would fall and CO2 would accumulate, making Earth uninhabitable.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

parent_concept_name: Human Impact on Earth's Biological Processes
summary: Greenhouse gases such as CO2, CH4, and water vapour trap outgoing infrared radiation from Earth's surface, keeping the planet warm enough to support life. Excess CO2 from burning fossil fuels and deforestation intensifies this effect, causing global warming, melting of glaciers, rising sea levels, and more extreme weather. In biological terms, this threatens agriculture, intensifies monsoons, and disrupts marine ecosystems including plankton and coral reefs.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

parent_concept_name: Human Impact on Earth's Biological Processes
summary: Eutrophication occurs when overuse of fertilisers adds excessive nitrogen via nitrates to rivers and lakes, causing widespread growth of algae (algal blooms). These algal blooms deplete dissolved oxygen in the water, killing fish and other aquatic organisms. This process threatens water bodies and coastal fisheries, disrupting aquatic ecosystems.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.17 shows eutrophication depicted as an algal bloom covering a water body surface.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

parent_concept_name: Human Impact on Earth's Biological Processes
summary: Clearing forests reduces photosynthesis and transpiration, leading to a decline in local rainfall and altered surface conditions. Without tree roots holding the soil, erosion increases and nutrient cycling is disrupted. Over time, habitat destruction causes biodiversity loss as many species lose their natural homes, with cascading effects across air, water, land, and living organisms.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Living organisms play specific roles in driving biogeochemical cycles, from bacteria that fix nitrogen to decomposers that return nutrients to soil. Plants and microbes cycle nutrients continuously between the atmosphere, soil, and water. Marine organisms including phytoplankton are critical to the carbon cycle by absorbing CO2 and forming the base of ocean food chains.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria such as Rhizobium (in root nodules of legumes) and Azotobacter (in soil) convert atmospheric N2 into ammonia, making nitrogen available to plants. Nitrifying bacteria like Nitrosomonas convert ammonia to nitrite, and Nitrobacter converts nitrite to nitrate for plant assimilation. Denitrifying bacteria such as Pseudomonas return nitrates to atmospheric nitrogen gas, completing the cycle.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Decomposers such as bacteria and fungi break down dead plants and animals and organic waste, returning nitrogen compounds like ammonia to the soil through ammonification. This process is essential for recycling nutrients back into the ecosystem, making them available for plant uptake. Without decomposers, nutrients would remain locked in dead organic matter and ecosystems would collapse.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Phytoplankton use dissolved CO2 in ocean water (absorbed as carbonate and bicarbonate ions) for photosynthesis, playing a critical role in the carbon cycle. Some marine organisms also use these ions to form shells, and when they die, they sink to the ocean floor, storing carbon for long periods. Increased ocean acidity from excess CO2 and warmer ocean temperatures threaten plankton and coral reefs, disrupting marine ecosystems.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

concept_name: Urban Heat Island Effect and Biology
summary: Cities are warmer than surrounding rural areas partly because urban surfaces absorb and re-radiate more solar radiation, while rural areas and forests stay cool through shade and plant transpiration. This difference shows how the presence or absence of vegetation and its biological processes directly influence local climate. The urban heat island effect illustrates how human land use alters the local biosphere.
diagram_description: Fig. 13.6 shows the urban heat island effect comparing a dense city to surrounding rural areas.
subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

concept_name: Haber-Bosch Process and Nitrogen in Biology
summary: The Haber-Bosch process artificially fixes atmospheric nitrogen to produce ammonia, which is the basis for most fertilisers used in modern agriculture. This process enabled India's Green Revolution and feeds billions of people, with more than half the nitrogen atoms in the human body estimated to come from this industrial process. However, overuse of these fertilisers has degraded soil and water through eutrophication.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Oceans hold approximately 71% of the total global carbon, making them the largest carbon reservoir on Earth and a key regulator of atmospheric CO2 levels. Ocean water absorbs atmospheric CO2, which phytoplankton use for photosynthesis, and marine organisms incorporate into shells that eventually sink to the ocean floor. Warmer ocean temperatures reduce the ocean's capacity to absorb CO2, weakening its role as a carbon sink and accelerating atmospheric warming.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Our Home: Earth, a Unique Life Sustaining Planet

subject: biology
chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

summary: Life processes are the maintenance functions that living organisms perform continuously to sustain life, prevent breakdown of organized structures, and enable molecular movements necessary for survival. These include nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion. Even during rest or sleep, these processes must continue to maintain the ordered nature of living systems.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Life processes are defined as the collective maintenance functions that keep organisms alive and prevent structural breakdown caused by environmental effects. They require continuous molecular movement and energy to maintain the ordered, organized nature of living structures.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Maintenance functions are the essential processes that must occur continuously in living organisms to prevent damage and breakdown of structures. These include nutrition to obtain energy, respiration to convert energy, transportation to distribute materials, and excretion to remove wastes.

builds_upon.concept_name: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms

subject: biology

summary: Nutrition is the process by which organisms obtain food and raw materials from outside their bodies to meet energy and growth requirements. Different organisms use different nutritional strategies: autotrophs synthesize their own food from inorganic sources, while heterotrophs obtain food prepared by other organisms.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Autotrophic nutrition is the process by which autotrophs (green plants and some bacteria) obtain energy and carbon from inorganic sources (CO₂ and water) and convert them into stored organic compounds like carbohydrates through photosynthesis. Chlorophyll absorbs light energy, which is converted to chemical energy to drive the synthesis of glucose. Carbohydrates not used immediately are stored as starch for later use.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.1 shows a cross-section of a leaf with labeled structures including chloroplasts, upper and lower epidermis, guard cells, and vascular tissue (xylem and phloem).

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Photosynthesis is the process by which autotrophs convert light energy into chemical energy stored in carbohydrates. The process occurs in three main steps: absorption of light energy by chlorophyll, conversion of light energy to chemical energy with splitting of water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen, and reduction of carbon dioxide to carbohydrates.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.1 shows chloroplasts in leaf cells; Fig. 5.2 shows variegated leaf starch test results demonstrating that starch is produced only in green regions where photosynthesis occurs.

builds_upon.concept_name: Role of Photosynthesis in Energy Flow
builds_upon.chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

subject: biology

summary: Stomata are tiny pores on leaf surfaces through which plants exchange gases (CO₂ and O₂) by diffusion for photosynthesis and respiration. Guard cells control stomatal opening and closing: they swell when water enters to open the pore and shrink to close it, preventing excessive water loss through transpiration.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.3 shows open and closed stomatal pores with guard cells and chloroplasts labeled.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Heterotrophic nutrition is the mode of nutrition in which organisms (animals and fungi) obtain complex organic food prepared by other organisms and break it down using enzymes. Heterotrophs include animals that ingest whole food material, fungi that break down food externally before absorption, and parasites that derive nutrition from host organisms without killing them.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology

summary: In Amoeba, food is taken in using temporary finger-like extensions of the cell surface (pseudopodia) that fuse over the food particle, forming a food vacuole where complex substances are broken down into simpler ones before diffusing into the cytoplasm. In Paramecium, the cell has a definite shape and food is taken at a specific spot, moved there by cilia that cover the cell surface.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.5 shows four stages of nutrition in Amoeba: food particle approaching, pseudopodia engulfing, food vacuole formation, and nutrient diffusion into cytoplasm.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Nutrition in human beings involves the breakdown of complex food molecules into simpler, absorbable forms as food travels through the alimentary canal from the mouth to the anus. Different regions of the canal are specialized for ingestion, digestion, absorption, and elimination of waste.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.6 shows the human alimentary canal with labeled parts: mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, and anus.
subject: biology

summary: In the mouth, food is crushed by teeth and mixed with saliva containing the enzyme salivary amylase, which breaks down starch into simple sugars. The tongue manipulates the food during chewing to help mix it thoroughly with saliva.
subject: biology

summary: In the stomach, food is mixed with gastric juices containing hydrochloric acid, pepsin (a protein-digesting enzyme), and mucus. The acidic medium facilitates enzyme action and helps kill pathogens, while mucus protects the stomach lining from acid damage.
subject: biology

summary: The small intestine is the site of complete digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Bile from the liver emulsifies fats into smaller globules, pancreatic juice provides enzymes (trypsin for proteins, lipase for fats), and intestinal juice completes the breakdown into glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids.
subject: biology

summary: Digested food is absorbed in the small intestine through finger-like projections called villi that increase surface area and contain blood vessels. Unabsorbed food moves to the large intestine where water is reabsorbed, and remaining material is excreted as feces.
subject: biology

summary: Respiration is the process by which organisms break down organic compounds (like glucose) to release energy in the form of ATP, which powers all cellular activities. The first step occurs in the cytoplasm where glucose is broken into pyruvate, and further breakdown depends on oxygen availability.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.8 shows multiple pathways of glucose breakdown: aerobic respiration in mitochondria producing CO₂ and water, anaerobic respiration in muscle cells producing lactic acid, and fermentation in yeast producing ethanol and CO₂.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Aerobic respiration is the complete breakdown of pyruvate in the mitochondria in the presence of oxygen to produce carbon dioxide, water, and large amounts of energy. This process releases significantly more energy than anaerobic pathways and is the primary energy-generating mechanism in most organisms.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.8 shows the aerobic pathway where pyruvate is broken down in mitochondria to produce three molecules of CO₂ and water.
subject: biology

summary: Anaerobic respiration is the breakdown of pyruvate in the absence of oxygen, occurring either through fermentation (producing ethanol and CO₂ in yeast) or through lactic acid formation (in muscle cells during oxygen deficiency). This process releases much less energy than aerobic respiration and accumulation of lactic acid causes muscle cramps.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.8 shows two anaerobic pathways: in yeast producing ethanol and CO₂, and in muscle cells producing lactic acid.
subject: biology

summary: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the energy currency of the cell, synthesized from ADP and inorganic phosphate using energy released during respiration. When ATP is broken down, the energy released (30.5 kJ/mol) drives endothermic reactions including muscle contraction, protein synthesis, and nervous impulse conduction.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Transportation is the process of moving materials (food, oxygen, water, minerals, and waste products) from one part of an organism to another. In multi-cellular organisms, simple diffusion is insufficient, so specialized transport systems (circulatory in animals, vascular in plants) have evolved.

builds_upon.concept_name: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms

subject: biology

summary: The human circulatory system consists of the heart (a muscular pump), blood (the transport fluid), and blood vessels (arteries, capillaries, and veins) that distribute oxygen, food, and other materials to all body cells and remove waste products.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.6 shows the alimentary canal; Fig. 5.10 shows the heart structure with four chambers; Fig. 5.11 shows the complete circulatory pathway with lungs, heart, and body organs.
subject: biology

summary: The heart is a four-chambered muscular organ with two upper chambers (atria) and two lower chambers (ventricles) separated by a septum to prevent mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood. The left side receives oxygenated blood from the lungs and pumps it to the body; the right side receives deoxygenated blood from the body and pumps it to the lungs.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.10 shows a cross-sectional view of the human heart with labeled chambers (left/right atrium and ventricle), septum, and major vessels (aorta, vena cava, pulmonary arteries and veins).
subject: biology

summary: Blood vessels are tubes that transport blood throughout the body. Arteries have thick elastic walls to withstand high pressure and carry blood away from the heart. Veins have thinner walls with valves to prevent backflow and carry blood toward the heart. Capillaries are one-cell thick and allow exchange of materials between blood and body cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.11 shows the branching pattern of blood vessels: arteries branching into capillaries and capillaries joining to form veins.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Double circulation refers to blood passing through the heart twice in each cycle: once to the lungs for oxygenation (pulmonary circulation) and once to the body tissues (systemic circulation). This separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood allows highly efficient oxygen delivery to meet the high energy demands of mammals and birds.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.11 shows the complete double circulation pathway with blood flow from body to right atrium, to right ventricle, to lungs, back to left atrium, to left ventricle, and out to body.
subject: biology

summary: Blood is a fluid connective tissue consisting of plasma and suspended cells. Red blood corpuscles contain haemoglobin, a respiratory pigment with high oxygen affinity that transports oxygen from the lungs to body tissues. Carbon dioxide is transported in dissolved form in the blood.
subject: biology

summary: Blood platelets are cells that circulate through the body and plug leaks by helping to clot blood at points of injury. Clotting prevents blood loss and maintains the pressure gradient needed for efficient blood circulation.
subject: biology

summary: Lymph is a colorless tissue fluid formed when plasma, proteins, and blood cells escape through capillary pores into intercellular spaces. It drains absorbed fats from the intestines and returns excess fluid from tissues back into the blood through lymphatic vessels.
subject: biology

summary: The plant transport system consists of xylem (which transports water and minerals from roots upward) and phloem (which transports photosynthetic products and other substances in both directions). These two independently organized conducting tubes distribute materials to all parts of the plant.
subject: biology

summary: Water is transported from roots to leaves through continuous xylem vessels by root pressure (active uptake of ions creating osmotic pressure at the root) and transpiration pull (evaporation of water from leaf cells creating suction). Transpiration is the major driving force during the day, while root pressure is more important at night.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.12 shows a tree with water vapor being released from leaves and water being drawn up through the root system.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Transpiration is the loss of water in the form of vapor from the aerial parts of plants, primarily through open stomata. It creates a suction (transpiration pull) that draws water and dissolved minerals from the roots through the xylem to leaves, and also helps regulate plant temperature.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.12 shows water vapor rising from tree leaves as an illustration of transpiration.
subject: biology

summary: Transport of food (translocation) occurs in phloem sieve tubes with help from adjacent companion cells, moving photosynthetic products and other substances in both upward and downward directions. Unlike xylem transport, phloem transport requires ATP energy: sucrose is actively transferred into phloem, increasing osmotic pressure and causing water to move in, which creates pressure to transport materials to tissues with lower pressure.
subject: biology

summary: Organisms require specialized organs to exchange gases with their environment for respiration. Plants exchange gases through stomata and intercellular spaces, while animals have evolved different gas exchange organs depending on their habitat (gills in fish, lungs in terrestrial animals).
diagram_description: Fig. 5.9 shows the human respiratory system with labeled parts: nasal passage, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, lungs, alveoli, ribs, and diaphragm.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Plants exchange gases through stomata and large intercellular spaces that ensure all cells contact air. At night, CO₂ elimination is the major exchange activity. During the day, CO₂ produced by respiration is used up in photosynthesis, so oxygen release is the major event.
subject: biology

summary: Aquatic animals like fish use gills to extract dissolved oxygen from water. Because dissolved oxygen is much lower than oxygen in air, fish breathe faster than terrestrial animals: they take water through their mouths, force it past the gills where blood absorbs the oxygen.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology

summary: Terrestrial organisms breathe oxygen from the atmosphere using specialized organs (lungs in humans and mammals) with protected, delicate surfaces for gas exchange. Air is conducted through passages to the lungs, and a mechanism (muscular diaphragm in humans) moves air in and out.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.9 shows the complete human respiratory system from nose to diaphragm with all major structures labeled.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology

summary: The lungs contain branching passages that divide into smaller tubes (bronchioles) and terminate in balloon-like alveoli. The alveolar surface (approximately 80 m²) is lined with extensive capillary networks that allow oxygen to diffuse into blood and carbon dioxide to diffuse from blood into alveolar air for exhalation.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.9 shows alveoli as balloon-like structures with surrounding capillaries where gas exchange occurs.
subject: biology

summary: Human breathing involves lifting the ribs and flattening the diaphragm to enlarge the chest cavity, which causes air to be sucked into the expanded lungs. The breathing cycle ensures that lungs always contain residual air, allowing sufficient time for oxygen absorption and carbon dioxide release.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.9 shows the diaphragm positioned below the lungs and ribs surrounding the lungs and heart.
subject: biology

summary: Excretion is the biological process of removing harmful metabolic waste products from the body. Different organisms use varied strategies: unicellular organisms remove wastes by simple diffusion, while complex multi-cellular organisms use specialized organs to filter and eliminate wastes.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: The human excretory system includes a pair of kidneys (which filter waste from blood to produce urine), ureters (tubes carrying urine to the urinary bladder), a bladder (for storage), and a urethra (for elimination). Nitrogenous wastes like urea and uric acid are removed from the blood in the kidneys.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.13 shows the human excretory system with kidneys positioned on either side of the backbone, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra.
subject: biology

summary: Each kidney contains thousands of nephrons, the functional filtration units. Each nephron consists of a glomerulus (a cluster of thin-walled capillaries) associated with Bowman's capsule that collects ultrafiltrate. As the filtrate flows along the tubule, useful substances (glucose, amino acids, salts, water) are selectively reabsorbed, while waste products form urine.
diagram_description: Fig. 5.14 shows the structure of a single nephron with labeled parts: glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, branch of renal artery, tubular part, and collecting duct.
subject: biology

summary: Urine production involves ultrafiltration of blood in the glomerulus (producing approximately 180 L of filtrate daily) followed by selective reabsorption of useful substances and water. The amount of water reabsorbed depends on the body's water status and dissolved waste levels, resulting in typically 1-2 L of urine excreted daily.
subject: biology

summary: Plants use different excretion strategies than animals: oxygen produced during photosynthesis is released through stomata, excess water is lost through transpiration, and other wastes are stored in cell vacuoles or as resins and gums in old xylem. Some wastes are removed when leaves fall off or are excreted into surrounding soil.
subject: biology

concept_name: Oxygen as Waste Product in Plants
summary: Oxygen itself is a waste product generated during photosynthesis and is released into the atmosphere through stomata. This oxygen is essential for respiration in the plant and other organisms, making plants crucial for oxygen cycling in ecosystems.
subject: biology

summary: Dental caries or tooth decay occurs when bacteria act on sugars to produce acids that soften the tooth enamel. Regular brushing removes plaque (bacterial cells and food particles) before bacteria produce acids, preventing tooth decay and the subsequent invasion of microorganisms into the pulp.
subject: biology

summary: Blood pressure is the force exerted by blood against vessel walls, measured as systolic (during heart contraction, ~120 mmHg) over diastolic (during relaxation, ~80 mmHg) pressure. High blood pressure (hypertension) results from arteriole constriction and can lead to artery rupture and internal bleeding.
diagram_description: A diagram showing blood pressure measurement using a sphygmomanometer at different stages of the cardiac cycle is included in the More to Know section.
subject: biology

concept_name: Harmful Effects of Tobacco and Smoking
summary: Tobacco use in any form (smoking, chewing) is harmful and damages the respiratory and circulatory systems. Smoking destroys cilia in the respiratory tract, allowing germs and toxins to reach lungs and cause infection, lung cancer, and other diseases. Smokeless tobacco increases risk of heart attacks, strokes, and oral cancer.
subject: biology

summary: An artificial kidney is a medical device that performs dialysis to remove nitrogenous waste products from blood in cases of kidney failure. Blood is passed through tubes with semi-permeable lining suspended in dialysing fluid devoid of nitrogenous wastes, allowing waste products to diffuse out while preserving necessary blood components.
diagram_description: A diagram in the More to Know section shows the artificial kidney setup with patient connection, tubing, dialysing fluid, and waste collection.
subject: biology

summary: Organ donation is the generous act of donating organs to persons suffering from organ failure, potentially saving or transforming their lives. Organs can be donated by deceased persons (after brain death declaration) or by living donors (for organs like kidney, partial liver, or lung), with proper consent and medical procedures.
subject: biology

summary: Haemoglobin content varies among different groups: children have different levels than adults, and males typically have higher levels than females. These differences reflect varying oxygen transport needs and metabolic demands across different ages and sexes.
subject: biology

summary: The nervous system is a specialized tissue network that detects environmental changes through receptors and transmits information via electrical impulses to coordinate body responses. It consists of neurons connected by synapses and is organized into the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system. This system enables both rapid reflex actions and voluntary thought-based movements.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.1(a) shows neuron structure with nucleus, dendrite, cell body, axon, and nerve ending labeled. Fig. 6.1(b) shows neuromuscular junction with axon, muscle fibre, capillary, and mitochondrion.
subject: biology

summary: A neuron is a specialized nerve cell consisting of a cell body (containing the nucleus), dendrites (which receive information), and an axon (which transmits information). Receptors at dendritic tips detect environmental stimuli and convert them into electrical impulses. The nerve ending releases chemicals to transmit signals to other cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.1(a) illustrates complete neuron structure with dendrite, nucleus, cell body, axon, and nerve ending clearly labeled.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Nerve impulses travel as electrical signals created by chemical reactions at the dendritic tip, moving from the dendrite through the cell body along the axon to its end. At the synapse (the gap between neurons), electrical impulses trigger the release of chemicals that cross the gap and initiate similar impulses in the next neuron. This process allows rapid information transfer throughout the nervous system.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.1(b) shows the neuromuscular junction where axon terminal meets muscle fibre, demonstrating impulse transmission mechanism.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: A reflex arc is a direct pathway connecting sensory neurons detecting a stimulus to motor neurons causing immediate muscle response, bypassing the brain for speed. This connection occurs in the spinal cord where sensory and motor neurons meet, allowing rapid responses to dangerous situations without the delay of conscious thought. Reflex actions are automatic responses that happen without deliberate control.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.2 shows complete reflex arc pathway from receptors (heat/pain in skin), through sensory neuron to spinal cord (with relay neuron), to motor neuron, to effector muscle in arm, with message pathway to brain shown separately.
subject: biology

summary: The human brain has three major regions: the fore-brain (the main thinking part receiving sensory impulses and controlling voluntary movements), the mid-brain (connecting upper and lower brain regions), and the hind-brain (containing the cerebellum for balance and posture, and the medulla for involuntary functions like breathing and heart rate). The fore-brain has separate sensory areas for hearing, smell, and sight, with association areas that interpret information and make decisions. Different brain regions control different activities ranging from conscious thought to automatic body functions.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.3 shows sagittal section of human brain with cerebrum, cranium, hypothalamus, pituitary gland, mid-brain, pons, medulla, cerebellum, and spinal cord labeled.
subject: biology

summary: The spinal cord is protected by the vertebral column (backbone), a hard bumpy structure that can be felt running down the middle of the back. This bony enclosure shields the delicate nervous tissue of the spinal cord from injury. The brain similarly sits inside a bony box and is further protected by a fluid-filled balloon that absorbs shock.
subject: biology

summary: Plants respond to environmental stimuli through two types of movements: rapid, non-growth-dependent movements (like sensitive plant leaf folding) and slow, growth-dependent directional movements. Unlike animals, plants lack nervous and muscular tissues but still detect stimuli and coordinate responses using electrical-chemical signals and hormones that diffuse from where they are synthesized to their sites of action. Plant cells change shape by altering water content to produce immediate movements, while directional growth responses occur through differential growth rates.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.4 shows the sensitive plant with open and folded leaf positions. Fig. 6.6 shows plant with roots growing downward (positively geotropic) and shoots growing upward (negatively geotropic).
subject: biology

summary: Plants like the sensitive plant respond immediately to touch stimulus by folding leaves without any growth involved. Information of the touch is communicated from the point of contact to other parts through electrical-chemical signals carried cell-to-cell. Leaf movement occurs when plant cells change shape by changing their water content, causing swelling or shrinking (turgor pressure changes).
diagram_description: Fig. 6.4 displays the sensitive plant in two states: open-leafed and fully folded, demonstrating immediate response to touch.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Plant movement can result from directional growth where parts grow at different rates in response to stimuli. For example, tendrils of pea plants respond to touch by growing more slowly on the contact side than the opposite side, causing them to coil around supports. This type of movement depends entirely on growth and will not occur if growth is prevented.
subject: biology

summary: Tropisms are directional growth movements where plant parts bend toward or away from environmental stimuli like light, gravity, water, or chemicals. In phototropism, shoots bend toward light while roots bend away; in geotropism, roots grow downward and shoots upward in response to gravity's pull. These responses help plants optimize their positioning for survival and nutrient acquisition.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.5 shows seedlings in a partially lit box with shoots bending toward light. Fig. 6.6 illustrates a plant with shoots that are negatively geotropic (away from gravity) and roots that are positively geotropic (toward gravity).

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Plant hormones are chemical compounds synthesized in one location and transported to another where they regulate growth, development, and responses to the environment. Auxins promote cell elongation and are produced at shoot tips; gibberellins and cytokinins promote growth; abscisic acid inhibits growth and causes leaf wilting. Unlike animal nervous systems, hormones diffuse gradually through plant tissues, making plant responses slower but able to reach all cells regardless of specialized tissue connections.
subject: biology

summary: The endocrine system uses chemical hormones secreted directly into the blood by endocrine glands to coordinate body functions alongside the nervous system. Hormones travel throughout the body and reach all cells, providing wide-ranging, slower but persistent changes compared to rapid nervous system responses. A feedback mechanism regulates hormone secretion based on hormone levels and their effects, ensuring precise control of bodily processes.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7(a) and (b) show locations of endocrine glands in male and female human bodies respectively: hypothalamus, pituitary, pineal, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, adrenal, pancreas, testis (male), and ovary (female).
subject: biology

summary: Adrenaline is a hormone secreted by the adrenal glands during scary or stressful situations, preparing the body for fight or flight responses. It increases heart rate to supply more oxygen to muscles, redirects blood from digestive and skin systems to skeletal muscles, and increases breathing rate through diaphragm and rib muscle contractions. These coordinated changes enable the animal body to respond quickly to threatening situations.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7 shows the location of adrenal glands above the kidneys in both male and female anatomical diagrams.
subject: biology

summary: The thyroid gland produces thyroxin hormone, which regulates carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism to maintain balanced growth. Iodine is essential for thyroxin synthesis, and its dietary deficiency can lead to goitre (swollen neck caused by thyroid enlargement). Adequate iodized salt consumption prevents thyroid disorders and ensures proper metabolic regulation.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7 shows thyroid gland location in the neck region on both male and female body diagrams.
subject: biology

summary: Growth hormone, secreted by the pituitary gland, regulates body growth and development. Deficiency of this hormone in childhood causes dwarfism, while excessive secretion leads to gigantism. The hypothalamus controls pituitary hormone release through releasing factors that stimulate or inhibit hormone secretion.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7 shows pituitary gland location below the hypothalamus in the brain.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates blood sugar levels by promoting glucose uptake into cells. When blood sugar rises, pancreas cells detect this and produce more insulin; as sugar levels fall, insulin secretion is reduced. Insulin deficiency or improper secretion leads to diabetes, requiring dietary management or insulin injections to maintain normal blood glucose levels.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7 shows pancreas location in both male and female body diagrams.
subject: biology

summary: Testosterone, secreted by the testes in males, and oestrogen, secreted by the ovaries in females, control sexual development and secondary sexual characteristics during puberty. These hormones also regulate menstrual cycles in females and maintain reproductive function. The dramatic physical and developmental changes occurring around ages 10-12 are driven by increased secretion of these reproductive hormones.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.7(a) shows testis location in male anatomy; Fig. 6.7(b) shows ovary and uterus in female anatomy.
subject: biology

summary: Hormonal feedback mechanisms maintain precise control of hormone secretion by detecting changes in hormone levels or their effects and adjusting secretion accordingly. For example, when blood sugar rises, the pancreas detects this and increases insulin secretion; as blood sugar falls, insulin production decreases. This self-regulating system ensures hormones are secreted in appropriate quantities at appropriate times without external control.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Nervous system responses are classified into three categories: reflex actions (automatic, unconscious, rapid responses to stimuli), voluntary actions (conscious, deliberate movements based on thinking and decision-making), and involuntary actions (automatic, unconscious but not reflexive responses like breathing and heartbeat). Reflex actions bypass the brain via spinal cord pathways for speed, while voluntary actions involve brain processing, and involuntary actions are controlled by mid-brain and hind-brain regions.
diagram_description: Fig. 6.2 illustrates the reflex arc pathway for reflex actions, showing the distinction from brain-mediated responses.
subject: biology

summary: Voluntary actions are deliberate, conscious movements that involve thinking and decision-making, such as writing, talking, moving a chair, or clapping. These actions require information processing by the brain's motor areas (fore-brain), which receive sensory information, interpret it, make decisions, and send signals to voluntary muscles. The time required for brain processing makes these responses slower than reflex actions.
subject: biology

summary: Involuntary actions are automatic, unconscious responses controlled by the mid-brain and hind-brain that occur without deliberate thinking or conscious control. The medulla controls vital functions like heart rate, breathing, digestion, blood pressure, salivation, and vomiting; the cerebellum coordinates precision and balance for activities like walking and bicycle riding. These actions continue whether we think about them or not and cannot be easily controlled by conscious effort.
subject: biology

concept_name: Nervous vs Hormonal Communication in Multicellular Organisms
summary: Multicellular organisms use electrical impulses through nervous systems for rapid, localized responses and chemical signals (hormones) for slower, widespread, persistent responses. Electrical impulses are fast but reach only connected cells and require reset time between signals; hormones diffuse throughout the body reaching all cells regardless of nervous connections but work more slowly. Together, these complementary systems enable organisms to respond to stimuli with appropriate speed and range of coordination.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Nervous vs Hormonal Communication in Multicellular Organisms
summary: Electrical impulses transmitted through the nervous system have two main limitations: they reach only those cells directly connected by nervous tissue (not all body cells), and cells require time to reset their mechanisms after generating and transmitting an impulse before they can generate and transmit new impulses. These constraints mean cells cannot continually create and transmit electrical impulses, necessitating complementary chemical communication systems for organism-wide coordination.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Nervous vs Hormonal Communication in Multicellular Organisms
summary: Chemical communication through hormones has advantages over electrical impulses: hormones can diffuse throughout the organism and reach all cells, not just those with nervous connections; hormone effects are slower but persistent and can be maintained steadily; and organisms can regulate widely-ranging changes in multiple tissues simultaneously. Cells detect hormones using special receptor molecules on their surfaces, allowing them to recognize and respond to chemical signals regardless of nervous connections.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Reproduction is the process by which organisms create new individuals through copying of DNA and cellular apparatus. Organisms look similar because they share similar body designs determined by DNA blueprints. DNA copying is not perfectly accurate, creating variations that are subtle but important for species survival and evolution.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell: Structural and Functional Unit of Life
builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: DNA copying is the basic event in reproduction where cells use chemical reactions to create copies of their DNA. Two DNA copies are generated and must be separated with their own cellular apparatus, resulting in cell division into two cells.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: DNA copying mechanisms are not absolutely reliable, resulting in variations in DNA copies. Some variations may be drastic and lethal, while others are subtle and allow the organism to survive. The surviving cells are similar but subtly different from each other and from the original cell.
subject: biology

summary: Variations in populations allow organisms to survive changing environmental conditions such as temperature shifts or water level changes. While variation is not necessarily beneficial to an individual organism, it is crucial for species survival over time. Populations with variation have better chances of adaptation when niches drastically alter.
subject: biology

summary: Asexual reproduction is the creation of new generations from a single individual without involving germ cells from two parents. Various organisms use different modes of asexual reproduction depending on their body design and complexity. All asexual modes involve DNA copying and creation of new individuals genetically identical or nearly identical to the parent.
subject: biology

summary: Fission is cell division in unicellular organisms that leads to creation of new individuals. In binary fission, organisms like Amoeba and bacteria split into two equal halves in any plane, while in multiple fission like Plasmodium, one cell divides into many daughter cells simultaneously.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.1(a) shows binary fission in Amoeba with sequential stages of cell division; Fig. 7.1(b) shows binary fission in Leishmania with oriented division in relation to the whip-like structure.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Budding is a form of asexual reproduction where an outgrowth develops due to repeated cell division at one specific site on the parent organism. The bud grows into a tiny individual and when fully mature, detaches from the parent to become a new independent organism. Yeast and Hydra use budding as a reproductive strategy.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.4 shows budding in Hydra with multiple buds developing on the parent body and a detached bud becoming independent.
subject: biology

summary: Fragmentation is reproduction in multi-cellular organisms with simple body organisation where the organism breaks into smaller pieces or fragments upon maturation. Each fragment grows into a new individual organism. Spirogyra filaments use fragmentation as their primary reproductive mode.
subject: biology

summary: Regeneration is the ability of fully differentiated organisms to grow into new individual organisms from their body parts when cut or broken. Specialised cells proliferate to form large numbers of cells that undergo developmental changes to become various cell types and tissues. Hydra and Planaria are classic examples, though regeneration is not the same as normal reproduction.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.3 shows regeneration in Planaria with a single organism regenerating into multiple complete individuals through sequential stages.
subject: biology

summary: Spore formation is reproduction in multi-cellular organisms like bread mould (Rhizopus) where specialised reproductive parts called sporangia contain spores that can develop into new organisms. Spores are covered by thick walls that protect them until they contact a moist surface and can begin to grow.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.6 shows spore formation in Rhizopus with sporangia containing spores at the tips of specialised hyphae structures.
subject: biology

summary: Vegetative propagation is reproduction in plants where parts like roots, stems, and leaves develop into new plants under appropriate conditions. Methods include layering and grafting, used to grow plants like sugarcane, roses, and grapes. Plants produced by vegetative propagation are genetically similar to the parent and can bear flowers and fruits earlier than seed-grown plants.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.5 shows Bryophyllum leaf with buds along the margin that can develop into new plants.
subject: biology

summary: Sexual reproduction is the creation of new individuals from the combination of germ cells (gametes) from two different individuals. It generates greater variation than asexual reproduction by combining DNA from two individuals with different accumulated variations. The need to prevent doubling of DNA content led to evolution of meiosis, producing germ cells with half the chromosomes.
subject: biology

summary: Germ cells are specialised cells that develop in specialised organs to produce gametes for sexual reproduction. In simple organisms, the two germ cells may be similar, but in complex organisms they specialise: the motile male gamete (sperm) and the larger female gamete (egg) containing stored food. This specialisation supports the energy requirements of embryonic development.

builds_upon.concept_name: Division of Labour in Multicellular Organisms

subject: biology

summary: Meiosis is a specialised process of cell division in germ cells that reduces the chromosome number to half compared to non-reproductive body cells. This prevents doubling of DNA content when two germ cells combine during sexual reproduction. Meiosis occurs in specialised lineages of cells in reproductive organs.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: In flowering plants (angiosperms), sexual reproduction occurs in the flower which contains both male parts (stamens producing pollen) and female parts (pistil containing ovules with egg cells). The stamen is the male reproductive part producing yellowish pollen grains, while the pistil is the female reproductive part made of ovary, style, and stigma.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.7 shows longitudinal section of flower with labelled parts: stamen (anther, filament), pistil (stigma, style, ovary), petals, and sepals.
subject: biology

summary: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from anther to stigma, either on the same flower (self-pollination) or between different flowers (cross-pollination) via wind, water, or animals. After landing on a suitable stigma, a pollen tube grows through the style to reach the ovary. Fertilisation is the fusion of the male germ cell from pollen with the female gamete (egg cell) to form a zygote.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.8 shows germination of pollen on stigma with pollen tube growth and male germ cell approaching female gamete in ovary.
subject: biology

summary: After fertilisation, the zygote divides repeatedly to form an embryo within the ovule. The ovule develops a tough coat and becomes a seed containing the future plant (embryo). The ovary grows rapidly and ripens to form a fruit, while other flower parts may shrivel and fall off. Seeds germinate under appropriate conditions to develop into seedlings.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.9 shows seed germination with labelled parts: plumule (future shoot), radicle (future root), and cotyledon (food store).
subject: biology

summary: Humans use sexual reproduction where germ cells from two individuals combine through mating. In mammals like humans, the baby is carried in the mother's body for an extended period and will be breast-fed later. Sexual reproduction requires internal transfer of germ cells, necessitating special organs for sexual intercourse and specialised reproductive systems.
subject: biology

summary: Puberty is the period during adolescence when reproductive tissues mature and sexual maturation occurs as body growth rate slows. Common changes include hair growth in new body areas, oily skin, and pimples in both sexes. Sex-specific changes include breast development and menstruation in girls, and facial hair growth and voice changes in boys.
subject: biology

summary: The male reproductive system consists of testes (located in scrotum outside abdominal cavity) that produce sperms, and delivery structures including vas deferens, urethra, seminal vesicles, and prostate gland. The testes secrete testosterone hormone that regulates sperm formation and brings about puberty changes. Seminal vesicles and prostate add secretions to form fluid that nourishes sperms and facilitates transport.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.10 shows male reproductive system with labelled parts: testes, scrotum, vas deferens, bladder, prostate gland, seminal vesicles, urethra, and penis.
subject: biology

summary: The female reproductive system consists of ovaries that produce eggs and hormones, oviducts (fallopian tubes) that transport eggs to the uterus, and the uterus (womb) where the embryo implants and develops. The uterus opens into the vagina through the cervix. Eggs mature and are released approximately monthly, with the uterus preparing its lining monthly to receive a fertilised egg.
diagram_description: Fig. 7.11 shows female reproductive system with labelled parts: ovaries, oviduct/fallopian tube, uterus, cervix, and vagina.
subject: biology

summary: After sperm enters through the vagina during sexual intercourse and travels upward, fertilisation occurs in the fallopian tube when sperm fuses with egg to form zygote. The zygote divides repeatedly to form an embryo that implants in the uterine lining and develops organs to become a foetus. The placenta, a specialised disc embedded in uterine wall with villi, enables nutrient and oxygen transfer from mother's blood to embryo and removal of embryo's waste.
subject: biology

summary: The menstrual cycle occurs roughly every month in females when the ovary releases an egg and the uterus prepares its thick, spongy lining to receive a fertilised egg. If fertilisation does not occur, the egg lives for about one day and the uterine lining is no longer needed. The lining breaks down and is expelled through the vagina as blood and mucous in a process lasting about two to eight days called menstruation.
subject: biology

summary: Contraceptive methods prevent pregnancy through various mechanisms: mechanical barriers (condoms) prevent sperm reaching egg, hormonal pills prevent egg release, intrauterine devices (copper-T, loop) prevent pregnancy, and surgical methods block vas deferens or fallopian tubes. Condoms also help prevent transmission of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) like gonorrhoea, syphilis, warts, and HIV-AIDS.
subject: biology

summary: Tissue culture is an advanced technique to grow new plants by removing tissue or cells from the growing tip of a plant and placing them in artificial medium where they divide to form callus (small cell group). The callus is transferred to hormone-containing medium for growth and differentiation, and plantlets are placed in soil to mature. This technique allows many plants to grow from one parent in disease-free conditions.
subject: biology

summary: Tissue culture involves removing tissue or separating cells from the growing tip of a plant and culturing them in artificial medium to divide rapidly and form a callus. The callus is transferred to another medium containing hormones for growth and differentiation into plantlets. Plantlets are then placed in soil for maturation, enabling disease-free mass propagation of ornamental plants.
subject: biology

summary: Reproductive health encompasses the physical and mental readiness for sexual activity and parenthood, disease prevention, and informed choice regarding family planning. Sexual maturation during puberty does not necessarily mean the body and mind are ready for sexual acts or childbearing. Understanding health consequences and prevention methods is essential for responsible reproductive decisions.
subject: biology

summary: Sexually transmitted infections include bacterial infections such as gonorrhoea and syphilis, and viral infections such as warts and HIV-AIDS. These diseases can be transmitted during sexual intercourse due to intimate body contact. Using condoms during sexual intercourse helps prevent transmission of many STIs to some extent.
subject: biology

summary: Pregnancy makes major physical and mental demands on a woman's body; premature or unplanned pregnancy can adversely affect her health. Knowledge of contraceptive methods and informed decision-making about sexual activity and parenthood are important for maintaining reproductive health. The decision to have children should consider the health and well-being of the woman.
subject: biology

summary: The female-male sex ratio must be maintained for a healthy society. Illegal sex-selective abortion of female foetuses through misuse of surgical methods and prenatal sex determination has caused the child sex ratio to decline alarmingly in some sections of society. Prenatal sex determination has been prohibited by law to address this social problem.
subject: biology

summary: Reproductive processes generate new individuals that are similar to parents but contain subtle differences due to genetic variation. These variations accumulate across generations, with inherited traits from parents combining with newly created differences. Asexual reproduction produces minor variations from DNA copying errors, while sexual reproduction generates greater diversity through genetic recombination.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.1 shows successive generations of organisms where the original individual produces two offspring with subtle differences, each of which produces two more individuals in the next generation, demonstrating the accumulation of inherited and new variations.
subject: biology

summary: Offspring inherit basic body design and subtle differences from parents, and these differences are passed to subsequent generations along with newly created variations. Over successive generations, the accumulation of both inherited and new variations increases diversity within a population.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.1 demonstrates how variations accumulate with each generation through inherited differences from parents combined with newly created differences.
subject: biology

summary: In asexual reproduction, a single individual produces offspring that are genetically very similar, with only minor differences arising from small inaccuracies in DNA copying. For example, when one bacterium divides and the resulting bacteria divide again, the four offspring are nearly identical with negligible variations.
subject: biology

summary: Sexual reproduction generates even greater diversity than asexual reproduction through the combination of genetic material from two parents. The visible variations among sexually reproducing organisms like animals and humans are much more distinct than in asexually reproducing organisms like sugarcane.
subject: biology

summary: Mendel's laws explain how traits are reliably inherited through genes, with each organism carrying two versions of each gene from both parents. Dominant traits are expressed when present in one or both copies, while recessive traits appear only when both copies are identical recessive alleles. These inheritance patterns follow predictable ratios across generations.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 illustrates inheritance of traits over two generations, showing F1 and F2 generations with genotypes TT, Tt, and tt demonstrating the 1:2:1 ratio in F2.
subject: biology

summary: Inherited traits are characteristics passed from parents to offspring through genes. While all children share basic human features with their parents, individuals show great variation in specific traits based on genetic inheritance from both mother and father.
subject: biology

summary: A dominant trait is expressed when at least one copy of the dominant allele is present, while a recessive trait appears only when both alleles are recessive. For example, in pea plants, tallness (T) is dominant—both TT and Tt plants are tall, while only tt plants are short. The trait observed in an organism depends on which alleles it inherits from both parents.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 shows dominant (T for tallness) and recessive (t for shortness) trait expression across generations, with TT and Tt being tall and tt being short.
subject: biology

summary: Mendel's Law of Segregation states that each organism carries two copies of genes for a trait, and during reproduction, these separate so that each gamete (sex cell) receives only one copy. When F1 plants (heterozygous) self-pollinate, the two gene copies segregate in F2 offspring, producing a 1:2:1 ratio of homozygous dominant, heterozygous, and homozygous recessive individuals.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 demonstrates segregation through F1 (all tall) and F2 (3 tall to 1 short) generations, showing the 1:2:1 genotypic ratio.
subject: biology

summary: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment states that traits controlled by different genes are inherited independently of each other. When two traits are followed across generations, new combinations appear in F2 offspring that were not present in the F1 or parental plants, such as tall plants with wrinkled seeds or short plants with round seeds.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.5 shows independent inheritance of seed shape (R/r) and colour (Y/y), producing a 9:3:3:1 ratio in F2 with new trait combinations including round yellow, round green, wrinkled yellow, and wrinkled green seeds.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Genes control traits by encoding proteins, particularly enzymes that regulate biochemical processes. For example, plant height depends on the amount of growth hormone produced, which is controlled by the efficiency of enzymes made according to genetic instructions. If a gene produces a functional enzyme, more hormone is made and the plant is tall; if the enzyme is less efficient, less hormone is made and the plant is short.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Genes are organized into separate independent pieces of DNA called chromosomes, with each cell carrying two copies of each chromosome—one from each parent. Each germ cell (sex cell) contains only one chromosome from each pair, and when two germ cells combine during fertilization, they restore the normal chromosome number in offspring, ensuring genetic stability across generations.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.6 shows chromosome pairs in male and female humans, including the sex chromosomes, and demonstrates how sex chromosomes segregate during gamete formation.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: Each somatic (body) cell contains two copies of each chromosome—one maternal and one paternal—while germ cells (gametes) contain only one chromosome from each pair. During reproduction, a germ cell from each parent contributes one chromosome to the zygote, restoring the normal pair in offspring. This mechanism explains independent inheritance and maintains genetic stability across generations.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.6 illustrates how chromosomes are paired in body cells and how they segregate into germ cells, with one from each pair going into each gamete.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Cell: The Building Block of Life

subject: biology

summary: In humans, sex is genetically determined by sex chromosomes: females have two X chromosomes (XX) while males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). All offspring inherit an X chromosome from their mother; the sex of the child is determined by the paternal chromosome—an X from the father produces a daughter, while a Y from the father produces a son. Different species use different strategies for sex determination, some relying on environmental cues like temperature.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.6 shows male (XY) and female (XX) chromosomes in humans, and illustrates gamete formation and offspring determination, showing that male offspring receive Y from father and female offspring receive X from father.
subject: biology

summary: Not all variations in a species have equal chances of survival in their environment. Different individuals possess different advantages depending on the nature of their variations; for example, bacteria that can withstand heat survive better during a heat wave. Environmental selection of favorable variants forms the basis for evolutionary processes.
subject: biology

summary: Variations that confer survival advantages in a particular environment are more likely to be preserved and passed to future generations. The process by which environmental factors select favorable variants while eliminating disadvantageous ones forms the biological basis for evolution.

builds_upon.concept_name: Diversity, Adaptation and Evidence of Evolution
builds_upon.chapter_name: Patterns in Life: Diversity and Classification

subject: biology

summary: Environmental conditions directly influence the survival and success of different variations within a population. Different individuals have varying advantages depending on their traits—bacteria with heat resistance survive better in hot environments, and environmental factors like temperature can even determine sex in some species.

builds_upon.chapter_name: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life

subject: biology

concept_name: Gregor Johann Mendel And His Contributions
