summary: Respiratory substrates are organic compounds oxidized during respiration to release energy. Carbohydrates (especially glucose) are the most common and preferred substrates, but fats, proteins, and organic acids can also be respired under certain conditions in plants.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: ATP and NADH Synthesis in Glycolysis
summary: Glycolysis is the partial oxidation of glucose to pyruvic acid occurring in the cytoplasm in all living organisms. This ten-step enzymatic pathway, also called the EMP pathway, is the initial stage of respiration and is the only process in anaerobic organisms.
diagram_description: Figure 12.1 shows the ten sequential steps of glycolysis from glucose (6C) to pyruvic acid (3C), with ATP consumption/production and NADH + H⁺ formation marked at specific steps.
subject: biology

summary: Glycolysis consists of ten enzymatic reactions that convert one glucose molecule into two pyruvate molecules. Glucose is phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate, then to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, which is split into two triose phosphates that are ultimately converted to pyruvate through successive oxidation and phosphorylation steps.
diagram_description: Figure 12.1 depicts all ten steps with intermediate compounds, showing phosphorylation reactions, triose formation, and the final conversion to pyruvate.
subject: biology

concept_name: ATP and NADH Synthesis in Glycolysis
summary: During glycolysis, ATP is consumed at two steps (glucose phosphorylation and fructose-6-phosphate conversion) and produced at two steps (BPGA to PGA and PEP to pyruvate). One NAD⁺ is reduced to NADH + H⁺ when 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde is oxidized and phosphorylated to 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate, yielding a net gain of 2 ATP and 2 NADH per glucose molecule.
diagram_description: Figure 12.1 shows ATP consumption (red arrows) and production (red arrows), NADH + H⁺ formation at the PGAL oxidation step, with cofactors NAD⁺ and ADP labeled.
subject: biology

summary: Fermentation is the incomplete oxidation of glucose under anaerobic conditions, occurring in many prokaryotes, unicellular eukaryotes, and muscle cells during oxygen deficit. It allows organisms to regenerate NAD⁺ for glycolysis continuation but releases less than 7% of glucose's energy and produces toxic byproducts (alcohol or lactic acid).
diagram_description: Figure 12.2 shows major pathways of anaerobic respiration with glucose entering glycolysis, pyruvate being converted to either lactic acid or ethanol + CO₂, with NAD⁺/NADH + H⁺ cycling.
subject: biology

summary: Lactic acid fermentation is the conversion of pyruvate to lactic acid by the enzyme lactate dehydrogenase, regenerating NAD⁺ from NADH + H⁺. This occurs in animal muscle cells during anaerobic exercise and in some bacteria, allowing continued glycolysis without oxygen.
diagram_description: Figure 12.2 shows pyruvate being reduced to lactic acid with NADH + H⁺ oxidized back to NAD⁺.
subject: biology

summary: Alcoholic fermentation is the conversion of pyruvate to CO₂ and ethanol under anaerobic conditions, catalyzed by pyruvic acid decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase. NADH + H⁺ is reoxidized to NAD⁺, allowing glycolysis to continue. Yeasts die when ethanol concentration reaches ~13%.
diagram_description: Figure 12.2 shows pyruvate decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, then reduced to ethanol with NAD⁺ regeneration.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Pyruvate Oxidation and Acetyl CoA Formation
Electron Transport System and Oxidative Phosphorylation
summary: Aerobic respiration is the complete oxidation of glucose to CO₂ and H₂O in the presence of oxygen, occurring primarily in eukaryotic mitochondria. It comprises pyruvate oxidation, the Krebs cycle, and the electron transport system, yielding ~38 ATP per glucose molecule through oxidative phosphorylation.
subject: biology

concept_name: Pyruvate Oxidation and Acetyl CoA Formation
summary: Pyruvate undergoes oxidative decarboxylation in the mitochondrial matrix by the pyruvic dehydrogenase complex, producing Acetyl CoA, CO₂, and NADH + H⁺. This irreversible reaction requires NAD⁺ and Coenzyme A as cofactors, converting pyruvate (3C) into Acetyl CoA (2C) for entry into the Krebs cycle.
subject: biology

summary: The Krebs cycle is an eight-step cyclic pathway in the mitochondrial matrix that completely oxidizes the acetyl group of Acetyl CoA to CO₂. It regenerates oxaloacetic acid, produces three NADH + H⁺ and one FADH₂ per Acetyl CoA, generates one GTP (converted to ATP), and provides precursors for biosynthesis.
diagram_description: Figure 12.3 shows the Krebs cycle with all eight intermediates (citrate, isocitrate, α-ketoglutarate, succinyl-CoA, succinate, fumarate, malate, oxaloacetate), NADH + H⁺ formation (three steps), FADH₂ formation (one step), CO₂ release (two steps), and GTP production.
subject: biology

concept_name: Electron Transport System and Oxidative Phosphorylation
summary: The electron transport system (ETS) is a series of protein complexes (I–IV) embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane that accept electrons from NADH and FADH₂, transferring them ultimately to O₂ to form H₂O. Energy released during electron transfer drives proton pumping, creating a gradient that ATP synthase (Complex V) uses to synthesize ATP from ADP—a process called oxidative phosphorylation.
diagram_description: Figure 12.4 shows Complexes I–V in the inner mitochondrial membrane: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), ubiquinone, Complex III (cytochrome bc₁), cytochrome c, Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), and Complex V (ATP synthase) with proton flow and ATP/ADP labeling.
subject: biology

summary: ATP synthesis in mitochondria couples the energy of electron transport to phosphorylation through ATP synthase and the chemiosmotic hypothesis. The enzyme complex uses electrochemical proton gradients across the inner membrane to phosphorylate ADP, with each ATP requiring 4 H⁺ to pass through the synthase.
diagram_description: Figure 12.5 shows ATP synthase with F₀ (integral membrane component) and F₁ (catalytic headpiece) with 4H⁺ flowing through F₀ and ATP being synthesized at the F₁ site.
subject: biology

summary: ATP synthase is a membrane protein complex (Complex V) consisting of F₁ (peripheral headpiece containing the catalytic site) and F₀ (integral membrane channel). F₁ synthesizes ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate, while F₀ forms the proton conduit across the inner mitochondrial membrane.
diagram_description: Figure 12.5 shows the structure of ATP synthase with F₀ anchored in the membrane forming the proton channel and F₁ headpiece extending into the matrix where ATP is synthesized.
subject: biology

summary: The chemiosmotic hypothesis explains ATP synthesis: electron transport energy pumps H⁺ ions from the mitochondrial matrix to the intermembrane space, creating an electrochemical proton gradient. This gradient drives H⁺ back through ATP synthase down the concentration gradient, coupling the energy of this flow to ATP synthesis.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Respiratory Balance Sheet and ATP Yield
summary: The respiratory balance sheet calculates the theoretical net ATP yield from complete glucose oxidation: approximately 38 ATP per glucose molecule through glycolysis, the Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation. However, this theoretical value assumes sequential, orderly pathways and complete substrate utilization, conditions rarely met in living cells.
subject: biology

concept_name: Respiratory Balance Sheet and ATP Yield
summary: The respiratory balance sheet sums ATP production across all respiration stages: 2 net ATP from glycolysis, 1 GTP (~1 ATP) from Krebs cycle, plus ~32–34 ATP from oxidation of 10 NADH + H⁺ (3 ATP each) and 2 FADH₂ (2 ATP each) through the ETS. Realistic in vivo yield is lower due to simultaneous pathway operation and substrate withdrawal for biosynthesis.
subject: biology

summary: The respiratory quotient (RQ) is the ratio of CO₂ evolved to O₂ consumed during respiration, reflecting the type of respiratory substrate oxidized. For carbohydrates, RQ = 1.0; for fats, RQ ≈ 0.7; for proteins, RQ ≈ 0.9. Living organisms typically respire mixed substrates, yielding intermediate RQ values.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Catabolic and Anabolic Functions of Respiratory Pathway
summary: The respiratory pathway is amphibolic, meaning it serves both catabolic (breakdown) and anabolic (synthesis) functions. Respiratory intermediates are withdrawn for biosynthesis of fats, proteins, and other cellular components, while the same intermediates are produced during substrate breakdown. Thus, the pathway operates in both directions.
diagram_description: Figure 12.6 shows interrelationship among metabolic pathways with respiration serving as a hub: fats, carbohydrates, and proteins enter at different points (acetyl CoA, pyruvate, glycolytic intermediates) and exit for biosynthesis via the Krebs cycle and its intermediates.
subject: biology

concept_name: Catabolic and Anabolic Functions of Respiratory Pathway
summary: The respiratory pathway functions catabolically during substrate breakdown for energy release and anabolically when intermediates are withdrawn for biosynthesis. For example, acetyl CoA is produced during fat oxidation but consumed during fatty acid synthesis; Krebs cycle intermediates serve as carbon skeletons for amino acid and other biosynthetic pathways.
diagram_description: Figure 12.6 illustrates pathways converging at and branching from the Krebs cycle, showing both catabolic entry (acetyl CoA from fats and pyruvate from carbohydrates/proteins) and anabolic withdrawal for biosynthesis.
subject: biology

summary: Different respiratory substrates enter the respiratory pathway at different points: glucose enters as glucose-6-phosphate at the start of glycolysis; fatty acids are degraded to acetyl CoA; glycerol enters as PGAL (triose phosphate); amino acids (after deamination) enter either as pyruvate, acetyl CoA, or as Krebs cycle intermediates depending on their structure.
diagram_description: Figure 12.6 shows multiple entry points: carbohydrates enter via glucose-6-phosphate, fats via acetyl CoA (through fatty acid and glycerol breakdown), and proteins via amino acids entering at various Krebs cycle stages or as pyruvate.
subject: biology

summary: Growth is an irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ or its parts, accompanied by metabolic processes. In plants, it is indeterminate due to meristems that retain capacity for unlimited cell division. Growth is measured by parameters like fresh weight, dry weight, length, area, volume, and cell number.
diagram_description: Figure 13.1 shows germination and seedling development in bean with labeled parts: seed coat, epicotyl hook, cotyledons, hypocotyl, and developing root system.
subject: biology

summary: Growth is defined as an irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ, its parts, or an individual cell. It is accompanied by anabolic and catabolic metabolic processes that consume energy. For example, expansion of a leaf is growth, while swelling of wood in water is physical absorption, not growth.
subject: biology

summary: Plant growth is unique because plants retain the capacity for unlimited growth throughout their life due to meristems at specific locations. Meristematic cells have the capacity to divide and self-perpetuate, with products soon losing divisibility. This open form of growth allows continuous addition of new cells to the plant body.
diagram_description: Figure 13.2 shows diagrammatic representation of root apical meristem, shoot apical meristem, and vascular cambium locations with arrows indicating direction of growth.
subject: biology

summary: Growth at cellular level is a consequence of increase in protoplasm amount, which is difficult to measure directly. Growth is therefore measured by parameters proportional to protoplasm: fresh weight, dry weight, length, area, volume, and cell number. Different organs show growth measured differently—maize root produces 17,500 new cells per hour, watermelon cells increase in size 350,000 times.
subject: biology

summary: Growth is divided into three phases. Meristematic phase occurs at root and shoot apices with constantly dividing cells rich in protoplasm and large nuclei. Elongation phase (proximal to meristematic zone) shows increased vacuolation, cell enlargement, and new cell wall deposition. Maturation phase farther from apex shows maximal cell size with wall thickening and protoplasmic modifications.
diagram_description: Figure 13.3 shows detection of zones of elongation by parallel line technique, with zones A, B, C, D immediately behind apex showing maximum elongation.
subject: biology

summary: Increased growth per unit time is termed growth rate and can be expressed mathematically. Arithmetic growth occurs when one daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates, producing a linear curve (Lt = L0 + rt). Geometric growth occurs when both progeny cells divide, producing an S-shaped sigmoid curve with lag, exponential, and stationary phases (W1 = W0 e^rt).
diagram_description: Figure 13.5 shows constant linear growth plotting length L against time t. Figure 13.6 shows idealised sigmoid growth curve with lag phase, exponential phase, and stationary phase.
subject: biology

summary: Essential conditions for plant growth include water (required for cell enlargement and turgidity), oxygen (for releasing metabolic energy), and nutrients (macro and microelements for protoplasm synthesis). Plant cells grow in size by cell enlargement which requires water, providing medium for enzymatic activities. Additionally, optimum temperature range, light, and gravity signals affect specific growth phases.
subject: biology

summary: Development is the sum of growth and differentiation, encompassing all changes an organism undergoes from germination to senescence. Cells from meristems differentiate and mature to perform specific functions through structural and protoplasmic changes. Plants show plasticity—the ability to form different structures in response to environment or life phases.
diagram_description: Figure 13.8 shows sequence of developmental process in a plant cell from meristematic cell through cell division, plasmatic growth, expansion, differentiation, and maturation to mature cell, with senescence pathway.
subject: biology

summary: Differentiation is the act leading to cell maturation in which cells undergo structural changes in both cell walls and protoplasm to perform specific functions. For example, tracheary elements lose their protoplasm and develop strong, elastic, lignocellulosic secondary cell walls to carry water long distances under extreme tension. Anatomical features in plants correlate with the functions they perform.
subject: biology

summary: Dedifferentiation is the phenomenon where living, differentiated cells that have lost the capacity to divide can regain division capacity under certain conditions. Examples include formation of interfascicular cambium and cork cambium from fully differentiated parenchyma cells. Redifferentiation occurs when such meristems produce cells that again lose divisibility but mature to perform specific functions.
subject: biology

summary: Plasticity is the ability of plants to follow different developmental pathways in response to environment or life phases to form different structures. Heterophylly exemplifies this—in cotton, coriander, and larkspur, juvenile leaves differ in shape from mature plant leaves. In buttercup, leaves produced in air differ from those produced in water.
diagram_description: Figure 13.9 shows heterophylly in (a) larkspur with juvenile and adult forms and (b) buttercup with terrestrial and water habitat forms.
subject: biology

summary: Differentiation in plants is open because cells arising from the same meristem have different structures at maturity depending on their position within the organ. Cells positioned away from root apical meristems differentiate as root-cap cells, while those at the periphery mature as epidermis. Final structure at maturity is determined by the cell's location within the developing organ.
subject: biology

summary: Plant growth regulators (PGRs) are small, simple molecules of diverse chemical composition that control plant growth and development. They are divided into two groups: growth promoters (auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins) involved in cell division, enlargement, and flowering, and growth inhibitors (abscisic acid, ethylene) involved in stress responses and dormancy. PGRs act both synergistically and antagonistically in controlling developmental processes.
subject: biology

summary: Plant growth regulators are small, simple molecules of diverse chemical composition including indole compounds (IAA), adenine derivatives (kinetin), carotenoid derivatives (ABA), terpenes (gibberellic acid), and gases (ethylene). PGRs are variously called plant growth substances, plant hormones, or phytohormones in literature. They are divided into two functional groups: promoters (auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins) and inhibitors (abscisic acid, ethylene).
subject: biology

summary: Discovery of PGRs began with Darwin's observations of coleoptile phototropism, leading to isolation of auxin by F.W. Went from oat seedling tips. Gibberellins were discovered through E. Kurosawa's work with rice seedling disease caused by Gibberella fujikuroi fungus. Cytokinins were identified by F. Skoog's tobacco callus tissue culture experiments. Abscisic acid was characterized from three independently discovered inhibitors in mid-1960s.
diagram_description: Figure 13.10 shows experiments demonstrating that coleoptile tip is source of auxin, with arrows indicating direction of light and showing bending responses.
subject: biology

summary: Auxins (from Greek 'auxein': to grow) include indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) and synthetic compounds like NAA and 2,4-D. Produced by growing apices of stems and roots, auxins initiate rooting in stem cuttings, promote flowering in pineapples, and prevent early fruit and leaf drop. They cause apical dominance—the growing apical bud inhibits lateral bud growth—and are used as herbicides to kill dicotyledonous weeds.
diagram_description: Figure 13.11 shows apical dominance in plants with (a) plant with apical bud intact and (b) plant with apical bud removed showing growth of lateral buds into branches.
subject: biology

summary: Gibberellins are a large group of plant hormones (more than 100 types) denoted GA1, GA2, GA3, etc., with gibberellic acid (GA3) being the most studied. They increase length of grapes stalks, cause apple fruit elongation, and delay senescence extending market period. GA3 accelerates malting in brewing, gibberellins increase sugarcane yield by up to 20 tonnes per acre, and promote bolting in rosette plants.
subject: biology

summary: Cytokinins have specific effects on cytokinesis and were discovered as kinetin (modified adenine) from herring sperm DNA, though natural cytokinins like zeatin occur in corn kernels and coconut milk. They are synthesised in regions of rapid cell division (root apices, developing buds). Cytokinins promote new leaf production, chloroplast development, lateral shoot growth, adventitious shoot formation, and help overcome apical dominance.
subject: biology

summary: Ethylene is a simple gaseous PGR synthesised in large amounts by senescing tissues and ripening fruits. It promotes horizontal growth of seedlings, apical hook formation in dicots, senescence and abscission of leaves and flowers, and is highly effective in fruit ripening via increased respiration (respiratory climactic). Ethylene breaks seed and bud dormancy, promotes internode elongation in deep water rice, and is applied via ethephon to hasten ripening and induce flowering in pineapples and mangoes.
subject: biology

summary: Abscisic acid (ABA) was discovered for its role in regulating abscission and dormancy. It acts as a general plant growth inhibitor and metabolism inhibitor, inhibiting seed germination. ABA stimulates stomatal closure and increases plant tolerance to various stresses, earning its designation as the stress hormone. It plays crucial roles in seed development, maturation, and dormancy, typically acting antagonistically to gibberellins.
subject: biology

summary: Meristems are regions of constantly dividing cells responsible for plant growth. Root and shoot apical meristems are responsible for primary growth, principally contributing to elongation of plants along their axis. Lateral meristems like vascular cambium and cork cambium appear later in dicots and gymnosperms, causing secondary growth through increase in girth of organs.
diagram_description: Figure 13.2 shows diagrammatic representation of locations of root apical meristem, shoot apical meristem, and vascular cambium with arrows indicating direction of growth.
subject: biology

summary: The root apical meristem is located at the root apex and is responsible for primary growth of the root system. Cells in this region are constantly dividing and are rich in protoplasm with large conspicuous nuclei. A single maize root apical meristem can generate more than 17,500 new cells per hour.
diagram_description: Figure 13.2 shows location of root apical meristem at the root tip with arrows showing direction of growth.
subject: biology

summary: The shoot apical meristem is located at the shoot apex and is responsible for primary growth of the aerial portions of the plant. Like root apical meristem, it contains constantly dividing cells rich in protoplasm with large nuclei and thin cellulosic primary cell walls with abundant plasmodesmatal connections.
diagram_description: Figure 13.2 shows location of shoot apical meristem at the shoot apex with arrows showing direction of growth.
subject: biology

summary: In dicotyledonous plants and gymnosperms, lateral meristems (vascular cambium and cork cambium) appear later in life and cause secondary growth—the increase in girth of organs. Vascular cambium produces secondary xylem and phloem, while cork cambium produces the protective cork layer. This secondary growth allows woody plants to continue increasing in diameter throughout their lifespan.
diagram_description: Figure 13.2 shows location of vascular cambium in both shoot and root with arrows indicating direction of lateral growth.
subject: biology

summary: Quantitative comparisons between growth of living systems are made using two measures. Absolute growth rate is the total growth per unit time measured in absolute terms. Relative growth rate is the growth per unit time expressed on a common basis, such as per unit initial parameter, representing the plant's efficiency in producing new material.
diagram_description: Figure 13.7 shows diagrammatic comparison of absolute and relative growth rates with two leaves A and B of different sizes increasing in area by 5 cm² each.
subject: biology

summary: Absolute growth rate is the measurement and comparison of total growth per unit time expressed in absolute terms. It measures the actual increase in size, weight, length, or other parameters without accounting for the initial size of the organism or organ. For example, two leaves increasing by 5 cm² each have the same absolute growth rate.
subject: biology

summary: Relative growth rate is the growth per unit time expressed on a common basis, such as per unit initial parameter, also called the efficiency index. It represents the plant's ability to produce new plant material relative to its starting size. A small leaf with 5 cm² area increasing by 5 cm² has higher relative growth rate than a large leaf with 50 cm² area increasing by the same amount.
subject: biology

summary: The human respiratory system consists of air passages from the nostrils through the pharynx, larynx, and trachea, which divides into bronchi and bronchioles. These conducting airways ultimately terminate in alveoli, the primary sites of gas exchange. The system is protected by pleural membranes and positioned in the thoracic cavity bounded by the ribs, sternum, vertebral column, and diaphragm.
diagram_description: Figure 14.1 shows a diagrammatic sectional view of the human respiratory system with labeled structures including epiglottis, larynx, trachea, bronchus, bronchiole, alveoli, pleural membranes, pleural fluid, and diaphragm.
subject: biology

summary: The conducting airway extends from the external nostrils through the nasal chamber, pharynx, and larynx into the trachea, which divides at the 5th thoracic vertebra into right and left primary bronchi. These bronchi undergo repeated divisions into secondary and tertiary bronchi and bronchioles, all supported by incomplete cartilaginous rings. This pathway transports, filters, humidifies, and warms atmospheric air before it reaches the alveoli.
diagram_description: Figure 14.1 shows the conducting airway pathway from nostrils through the trachea and its branching into bronchi and bronchioles.
subject: biology

summary: Each terminal bronchiole gives rise to numerous thin-walled, irregular, vascularized alveoli that form the respiratory or exchange part of the system. Alveoli are the primary sites where O₂ and CO₂ diffuse between atmospheric air and blood. The extremely thin diffusion membrane consists of the alveolar squamous epithelium, capillary endothelium, and supporting basement membranes.
diagram_description: Figure 14.1 shows alveoli as thin, irregular structures at the terminal end of bronchioles, and Figure 14.4 provides detailed cross-section showing squamous epithelium, basement substance, and blood capillary.
subject: biology

summary: The lungs are covered by a double-layered pleura with pleural fluid between the layers, reducing friction during breathing. The outer parietal pleural membrane contacts the thoracic lining while the inner visceral pleural membrane contacts the lung surface. This anatomical arrangement enables the lungs to expand and contract smoothly within the air-tight thoracic chamber.
diagram_description: Figure 14.1 labels the pleural membranes and pleural fluid surrounding the lungs within the thoracic cavity.
subject: biology

summary: Breathing involves two stages: inspiration, when atmospheric air enters the lungs due to decreased intra-pulmonary pressure, and expiration, when air is expelled as intra-pulmonary pressure increases. Pressure gradients are created through the contraction and relaxation of the diaphragm and external/internal intercostal muscles. A healthy human breathes 12-16 times per minute, with air volumes measured and analyzed clinically using spirometry.
diagram_description: Figure 14.2 shows two diagrams: (a) inspiration with diaphragm contracted, ribs and sternum raised, increasing thoracic volume; (b) expiration with diaphragm relaxed and returned to original position.
subject: biology

summary: Inspiration is initiated by diaphragm contraction, which increases the antero-posterior diameter of the thoracic chamber. Simultaneously, contraction of external intercostal muscles lifts the ribs and sternum, increasing the dorso-ventral dimension. This overall increase in thoracic volume causes pulmonary volume to increase and intra-pulmonary pressure to drop below atmospheric pressure, forcing air into the lungs.
diagram_description: Figure 14.2(a) shows inspiration with arrows indicating increased thoracic volume, contracted diaphragm, and raised ribs and sternum.
subject: biology

summary: Expiration occurs when the diaphragm and intercostal muscles relax, returning the diaphragm and sternum to their normal positions and reducing thoracic volume. This compression decreases pulmonary volume and increases intra-pulmonary pressure to slightly above atmospheric pressure, forcing air out of the lungs. Forced expiration can be enhanced using abdominal muscles.
diagram_description: Figure 14.2(b) shows expiration with diaphragm relaxed and arched upward, ribs and sternum returned to original position, and decreased thoracic volume.
subject: biology

summary: Respiratory volumes include Tidal Volume (TV ~500 mL), Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV ~2500-3000 mL), Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV ~1000-1100 mL), and Residual Volume (RV ~1100-1200 mL). Pulmonary capacities combine these volumes: Inspiratory Capacity (IC=TV+IRV), Expiratory Capacity (EC=TV+ERV), Functional Residual Capacity (FRC=ERV+RV), Vital Capacity (VC=TV+IRV+ERV), and Total Lung Capacity (TLC=VC+RV). These measurements are clinically significant for assessing pulmonary function using spirometry.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Diffusion of Gases at Alveoli and Tissues
summary: Respiration involves five coordinated steps: breathing/pulmonary ventilation, diffusion of O₂ and CO₂ across the alveolar membrane, blood transport of gases throughout the body, diffusion of gases between blood and tissues, and utilization of O₂ by cells. O₂ and CO₂ are exchanged by simple diffusion based on concentration/pressure gradients, with factors like gas solubility and membrane thickness affecting diffusion rates. Approximately 97% of O₂ is transported by hemoglobin while 70% of CO₂ is carried as bicarbonate.
diagram_description: Figure 14.3 shows diagrammatic representation of gas exchange at alveoli and body tissues with partial pressures (pO₂ and pCO₂) labeled and arrows indicating O₂ and CO₂ movement.
subject: biology

concept_name: Diffusion of Gases at Alveoli and Tissues
summary: O₂ and CO₂ are exchanged at alveoli and tissues through simple diffusion based on pressure/concentration gradients. The diffusion membrane consists of three layers: alveolar squamous epithelium, capillary endothelium, and the basement substance between them, totaling less than a millimeter in thickness. CO₂ has 20-25 times higher solubility than O₂, enabling greater diffusion per unit pressure difference. All body factors favor O₂ diffusion from alveoli to tissues and CO₂ diffusion from tissues to alveoli.
diagram_description: Figure 14.4 shows a cross-section of an alveolus with pulmonary capillary, labeling squamous epithelium, basement substance, endothelium of blood capillary, and red blood cells.
subject: biology

summary: Partial pressure is the pressure contributed by an individual gas in a mixture, represented as pO₂ for oxygen and pCO₂ for carbon dioxide. In atmospheric air, pO₂ is 159 mm Hg and pCO₂ is 0.3 mm Hg; in alveoli, pO₂ is 104 mm Hg and pCO₂ is 40 mm Hg. Clear concentration gradients exist for O₂ from alveoli (104) to deoxygenated blood (40) to tissues (40), and for CO₂ in the opposite direction, driving diffusion according to these pressure differences.
diagram_description: Table 14.1 and Figure 14.3 present partial pressures of O₂ and CO₂ in atmospheric air, alveoli, blood (deoxygenated and oxygenated), and tissues.
subject: biology

summary: Hemoglobin, an iron-containing pigment in RBCs, binds reversibly with O₂ to form oxyhaemoglobin, with each hemoglobin molecule carrying a maximum of four O₂ molecules. Binding is primarily related to pO₂, while pCO₂, H⁺ concentration, and temperature also influence it. In alveoli with high pO₂, low pCO₂, low H⁺, and lower temperature, O₂ binds to hemoglobin; in tissues with low pO₂, high pCO₂, high H⁺, and higher temperature, O₂ dissociates. The oxygen dissociation curve is sigmoid-shaped and shows that 100 mL of oxygenated blood delivers ~5 mL O₂ to tissues.
diagram_description: Figure 14.5 shows the oxygen dissociation curve as a sigmoid plot of percentage saturation of hemoglobin with oxygen against partial pressure of oxygen (0-100 mm Hg).
subject: biology

summary: Nearly 20-25% of CO₂ is transported as carbamino-hemoglobin bound to hemoglobin, while 70% is carried as bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻), and 7% dissolves in plasma. At tissue sites with high pCO₂, CO₂ diffuses into blood and forms HCO₃⁻ and H⁺ via the enzyme carbonic anhydrase (CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺). At alveoli with low pCO₂, the reaction reverses, releasing CO₂ as H₂O. Every 100 mL of deoxygenated blood delivers approximately 4 mL CO₂ to alveoli.
subject: biology

summary: The neural system regulates respiratory rhythm to match body tissue demands. The respiratory rhythm centre in the medulla region of the brain is primarily responsible, while the pneumotaxic centre in the pons moderates its functions. A chemosensitive area adjacent to the rhythm centre responds to CO₂ and H⁺ concentration changes, and receptors at the aortic arch and carotid artery also signal the rhythm centre. Oxygen has minimal role in respiratory regulation.
subject: biology

summary: The respiratory rhythm centre in the medulla oblongata region is the primary controller of breathing, maintaining regular respiratory rhythm. The pneumotaxic centre in the pons region can moderate the respiratory rhythm centre's functions by reducing inspiration duration and altering respiratory rate through neural signals. This dual-centre system ensures coordinated and adjustable breathing in response to varying body demands.
subject: biology

summary: A chemosensitive area adjacent to the respiratory rhythm centre is highly sensitive to CO₂ and hydrogen ions; increases in these substances activate this centre, which signals the rhythm centre to adjust respiration and eliminate excess CO₂ and H⁺. Receptors associated with the aortic arch and carotid artery also monitor changes in CO₂ and H⁺ concentration and send remedial signals to the rhythm centre. Oxygen concentration has minimal effect on respiratory regulation.
subject: biology

summary: Common respiratory disorders include asthma, emphysema, and occupational respiratory diseases. Asthma causes difficulty in breathing and wheezing due to bronchi and bronchiole inflammation. Emphysema is a chronic disorder where alveolar walls are damaged, decreasing respiratory surface area, with cigarette smoking as a major cause. Occupational disorders result from prolonged dust exposure in grinding or stone-breaking industries, causing inflammation and fibrosis.
subject: biology

summary: Asthma is a respiratory condition characterized by difficulty in breathing and wheezing, caused by inflammation of the bronchi and bronchioles. This inflammation narrows the airways, restricting airflow and making breathing labored, particularly during exertion or exposure to allergens.
subject: biology

summary: Emphysema is a chronic respiratory disorder in which alveolar walls are progressively damaged, significantly reducing the respiratory surface area available for gas exchange. Cigarette smoking is identified as one of the major causes of emphysema, leading to permanent lung tissue destruction and impaired oxygen uptake.
subject: biology

summary: Occupational respiratory disorders occur in industries involving grinding or stone-breaking where excessive dust is produced, overwhelming the body's defense mechanisms. Prolonged exposure causes inflammation and fibrosis (proliferation of fibrous tissues), leading to serious lung damage. Workers in such industries should wear protective masks to prevent inhalation of harmful dust particles.
subject: biology

summary: Alfonso Corti was an Italian anatomist (1822-1888) who initially studied cardiovascular systems in reptiles before turning his attention to the mammalian auditory system. In 1851, he published a paper describing the organ of Corti, a structure on the basilar membrane of the cochlea containing hair cells that convert sound vibrations into nerve impulses. This discovery was foundational to understanding the mechanism of hearing.
subject: biology

summary: Blood is a special connective tissue composed of a fluid matrix (plasma) constituting 55% of blood and formed elements (RBCs, WBCs, platelets) constituting 45%. Plasma is 90-92% water and contains proteins (fibrinogen, globulins, albumins), minerals, glucose, and clotting factors. Formed elements function in gas transport, immune defense, and blood coagulation.
diagram_description: Figure 15.1 shows diagrammatic representation of formed elements including RBC, platelets, eosinophil, basophil, neutrophil, monocyte, T lymphocyte, and B lymphocyte.
subject: biology

summary: Plasma is a straw-coloured viscous fluid comprising nearly 55% of blood, with 90-92% water and 6-8% proteins. Major plasma proteins include fibrinogen (for clotting), globulins (immune defense), and albumins (osmotic balance). Plasma also contains minerals, glucose, amino acids, lipids, and inactive coagulation factors; plasma without clotting factors is called serum.
subject: biology

summary: Formed elements (RBCs, WBCs, platelets) constitute 45% of blood. RBCs transport oxygen via haemoglobin; WBCs (granulocytes: neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils; agranulocytes: lymphocytes, monocytes) provide immune defense; platelets (cell fragments) initiate blood clotting. A healthy adult has 5-5.5 million RBCs/mm³, 6000-8000 WBCs/mm³, and 1.5-3.5 million platelets/mm³.
diagram_description: Figure 15.1 displays all formed elements with RBC shown as biconcave disc, various WBC types (neutrophil, eosinophil, basophil, monocyte, T and B lymphocytes), and platelets.
subject: biology

summary: Blood is grouped by surface antigens on RBCs: ABO system (A, B, AB, O based on A and B antigens) and Rh system (Rh+ve with Rh antigen; Rh-ve without). Group O individuals are universal donors; AB individuals are universal recipients. Rh incompatibility between Rh-ve mothers and Rh+ve foetuses can cause erythroblastosis foetalis in subsequent pregnancies.
diagram_description: Table 15.1 shows blood groups with antigens on RBCs, antibodies in plasma, and donor compatibility for groups A, B, AB, and O.
subject: biology

summary: Blood coagulation is a protective mechanism preventing excessive blood loss after injury. Platelets release factors that initiate a cascade of enzymic reactions forming thrombokinase, which converts prothrombin to thrombin. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin threads that trap blood cells forming a clot.
subject: biology

summary: Coagulation is initiated by platelet factors released after injury and tissue factors at the injury site. These activate a cascade of enzymic reactions producing thrombokinase enzyme complex. Calcium ions play a crucial role in the cascade process linking multiple plasma factors in an inactive state.
subject: biology

summary: Thrombin enzyme converts inactive fibrinogen protein to fibrin threads forming a network that traps dead and damaged blood cells. This dark reddish-brown network forms a clot (coagulum) that seals the wound. Fibrin formation is the final step in blood coagulation.
subject: biology

summary: Tissue fluid (interstitial fluid) forms when blood passes through capillaries and water with small solutes move into spaces between tissue cells. This fluid, collected by the lymphatic system and called lymph, is colourless and contains specialised lymphocytes. Lymph transports nutrients, hormones, and absorbed fats via lacteals in intestinal villi.
subject: biology

summary: As blood passes through tissue capillaries, water and small water-soluble substances move out into spaces between cells, leaving larger proteins and most formed elements in blood vessels. This fluid, with the same mineral distribution as plasma, is called tissue fluid or interstitial fluid and enables nutrient and gas exchange between blood and cells.
subject: biology

summary: Lymph is a colourless fluid containing specialised lymphocytes responsible for immune responses. The lymphatic system drains tissue fluid back to major veins, and lymph serves as an important carrier for nutrients, hormones, and absorbed fats. Fats are absorbed through lymph in lacteals present in intestinal villi.
subject: biology

summary: Animals exhibit two circulatory patterns: open systems (arthropods, molluscs) where blood enters body cavities (sinuses), and closed systems (annelids, chordates) where blood circulates through closed blood vessels. Closed systems allow more precise fluid flow regulation. All vertebrates possess muscular chambered hearts with increasing complexity.
subject: biology

summary: In open circulatory systems found in arthropods and molluscs, blood pumped by the heart passes through large vessels into open spaces or body cavities called sinuses. This system is less efficient at regulating fluid flow compared to closed circulatory systems.
subject: biology

summary: Closed circulatory systems in annelids and chordates circulate blood through a closed network of blood vessels. This pattern allows more precise regulation of fluid flow and is considered more advantageous than open systems.
subject: biology

summary: Vertebrate hearts show evolutionary progression: fishes have 2-chambered hearts (atrium, ventricle) with single circulation; amphibians and reptiles have 3-chambered hearts with incomplete double circulation; crocodiles, birds, and mammals have 4-chambered hearts with complete double circulation separating oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
subject: biology

summary: The human heart is a muscular, four-chambered organ (two atria, two ventricles) situated in the thoracic cavity between the lungs, protected by a double-walled membranous pericardium. Septa (interatrial, interventricular, atrioventricular) separate chambers. The heart pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs (right ventricle) and oxygenated blood to body (left ventricle).
diagram_description: Figure 15.2 shows cross-section of human heart with chambers (RA, LA, RV, LV), vessels (vena cava, aorta, pulmonary artery/veins), valves, septa, nodal tissue (SAN, AVN), and bundle of His.
subject: biology

summary: The heart has four chambers: two small upper atria (right and left) and two large lower ventricles (right and left). The interatrial septum separates the atria; the thick interventricular septum separates ventricles; the atrioventricular septum separates atrium from ventricle on each side. Openings between chambers allow blood flow under normal conditions.
diagram_description: Figure 15.2 shows all four chambers with septa labeled: interatrial septum between atria, interventricular septum between ventricles, and atrioventricular septa.
subject: biology

summary: The tricuspid valve (three flaps) guards the opening between right atrium and right ventricle; the bicuspid or mitral valve (two flaps) guards between left atrium and left ventricle. Semilunar valves guard openings from right ventricle to pulmonary artery and from left ventricle to aorta. All valves allow unidirectional blood flow and prevent backflow.
diagram_description: Figure 15.2 labels tricuspid valve between RA and RV, mitral/bicuspid valve between LA and LV, and semilunar valves at pulmonary artery and aorta.
subject: biology

summary: Specialised nodal tissue (autoexcitable cardiac muscle) controls heart rhythms: the sino-atrial node (SAN) in right upper atrium generates maximum action potentials (70-75/min) and acts as pacemaker; the atrio-ventricular node (AVN) conducts signals to ventricles via the atrioventricular bundle and Purkinje fibres. This conduction system maintains rhythmic contractile activity.
diagram_description: Figure 15.2 shows SAN in right upper atrium, AVN near atrioventricular septum, AV bundle passing through septum, right and left branches, and Purkinje fibres in ventricular musculature.
subject: biology

summary: The cardiac cycle consists of sequential, cyclically repeated events: atrial contraction (systole) followed by ventricular contraction coinciding with atrial relaxation (diastole), followed by complete heart relaxation. A normal heart beats 72 times per minute (each cycle 0.8 seconds), pumping approximately 70 mL per beat (stroke volume) or 5 litres per minute (cardiac output).
subject: biology

summary: Atrial systole is simultaneous contraction of both atria initiated by SAN, increasing blood flow to ventricles by ~30%. This is followed by ventricular systole (contraction) coinciding with atrial diastole (relaxation). Ventricular systole increases pressure, closing atrioventricular valves and forcing open semilunar valves to eject blood. Ventricular diastole follows, closing semilunar valves and allowing refilling from atria.
subject: biology

summary: Stroke volume is the volume of blood pumped out by each ventricle per beat, averaging approximately 70 mL in healthy individuals. Cardiac output is the total volume of blood pumped per minute, calculated as stroke volume multiplied by heart rate (beats per minute), averaging 5000 mL (5 litres) in healthy individuals. The body can alter both parameters to adjust cardiac output.
subject: biology

summary: Two prominent heart sounds occur during each cardiac cycle: the first heart sound (lub) is associated with closure of tricuspid and bicuspid valves at ventricular systole; the second heart sound (dub) is associated with closure of semilunar valves at ventricular diastole. These sounds are detectable via stethoscope and have clinical diagnostic significance.
subject: biology

summary: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is a graphical recording of the heart's electrical activity obtained using an electrocardiograph with three leads (wrists and left ankle). The ECG shows characteristic peaks labeled P, Q, R, S, T that correspond to specific electrical events during the cardiac cycle. ECG recordings are clinically significant for detecting cardiac abnormalities and arrhythmias.
diagram_description: Figure 15.3 is a standard ECG showing P-wave, QRS complex (with Q, R, S peaks), and T-wave on a graph with labeled axes.
subject: biology

summary: The P-wave represents electrical excitation (depolarisation) of the atria leading to atrial contraction. The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarisation initiating ventricular contraction (systole begins shortly after Q). The T-wave represents return of ventricles to normal state (repolarisation), with end of T-wave marking end of systole.
diagram_description: Figure 15.3 labels P-wave for atrial activity, QRS complex for ventricular depolarisation, and T-wave for ventricular repolarisation.
subject: biology

summary: ECG is clinically significant for determining heart beat rate by counting QRS complexes in a given time period. Since ECGs from different individuals have roughly the same shape for a given lead configuration, any deviation from normal shape indicates possible abnormality or disease. This diagnostic value makes ECG essential for cardiac assessment.
subject: biology

summary: Double circulation consists of two separate pathways: pulmonary circulation (right ventricle → lungs → left atrium) and systemic circulation (left ventricle → body tissues → right atrium). Blood flows through three types of vessels: arteries (thick muscular walls), veins (thin walls), and capillaries (single-cell layer for exchange). This dual system ensures complete separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
diagram_description: Figure 15.4 shows schematic plan of human blood circulation including heart, lungs, body parts, with pathways for pulmonary (purple) and systemic (red) circulation, and cross-section showing vessel structure.
subject: biology

summary: Blood vessels have three layers: tunica intima (inner squamous endothelium), tunica media (middle smooth muscle and elastic fibres), and tunica externa (outer fibrous connective tissue with collagen). Arteries have thick muscular walls; veins have comparatively thin tunica media. Capillaries consist of only endothelial layer for efficient substance exchange.
diagram_description: Figure 15.4 shows cross-sections of artery and vein with labeled layers (tunica intima, tunica media, tunica externa) and lumen.
subject: biology

summary: Pulmonary circulation: deoxygenated blood from right ventricle enters pulmonary artery, travels to lungs for oxygenation, and returns via pulmonary veins to left atrium. Systemic circulation: oxygenated blood from left ventricle enters aorta, is distributed via arteries and capillaries to body tissues for nutrient/oxygen delivery and CO₂/waste collection, and returns via veins and vena cava to right atrium.
diagram_description: Figure 15.4 shows both pathways with pulmonary circulation to lungs and systemic circulation to body parts via aorta and return via veins.
subject: biology

summary: The hepatic portal system is a unique vascular connection where the hepatic portal vein carries blood from the digestive tract to the liver before delivery to systemic circulation, allowing liver to process nutrients and filter harmful substances. A special coronary system of blood vessels exclusively supplies the cardiac musculature with blood, ensuring continuous oxygen delivery to the heart muscle.
subject: biology

summary: The heart is myogenic (self-exciting) due to autoexcitable nodal tissue; normal cardiac activity is intrinsically regulated. However, the medulla oblongata can moderate heart function via the autonomic nervous system: sympathetic signals increase heart rate and contractility, while parasympathetic signals decrease both. Adrenal medullary hormones also increase cardiac output.
subject: biology

summary: The heart is myogenic because it generates its own rhythmic contractions through autoexcitable nodal tissue (SAN, AVN) without requiring external nervous stimuli. This intrinsic self-exciting property ensures the heart continues beating automatically; normal cardiac activities are auto-regulated by specialised cardiac muscles.
subject: biology

summary: The medulla oblongata contains a special neural centre that moderates cardiac function through the autonomic nervous system. Sympathetic nerves increase heart rate, strength of ventricular contraction, and cardiac output; parasympathetic nerves decrease heart rate, speed of action potential conduction, and cardiac output. Adrenal medullary hormones further increase cardiac output.
subject: biology

summary: Common circulatory disorders include hypertension (blood pressure >140/90 mmHg) affecting brain and kidneys; coronary artery disease (atherosclerosis causing arterial narrowing); angina pectoris (chest pain from insufficient oxygen to heart); and heart failure (inadequate cardiac pumping). These conditions reduce oxygen delivery and organ function, requiring medical intervention.
subject: biology

summary: Hypertension is blood pressure higher than normal (120/80 mmHg), where 120 is systolic (pumping) pressure and 80 is diastolic (resting) pressure. Repeated blood pressure readings of 140/90 mmHg or higher indicate hypertension. High blood pressure leads to heart diseases and adversely affects vital organs including brain and kidney.
subject: biology

summary: Coronary Artery Disease (atherosclerosis) affects blood vessels supplying the heart muscle due to deposits of calcium, fat, cholesterol, and fibrous tissues narrowing arterial lumen. Angina (angina pectoris) is acute chest pain occurring when insufficient oxygen reaches the heart muscle due to poor blood flow, affecting people of any age but more common in middle-aged and elderly individuals.
subject: biology

summary: Heart failure is a condition where the heart is not pumping blood effectively enough to meet body needs, often called congestive heart failure because lung congestion is a main symptom. It differs from cardiac arrest (heart stops beating) and heart attack (sudden damage from inadequate blood supply). Heart failure results in reduced oxygen delivery to tissues.
subject: biology

summary: Animals excrete nitrogenous wastes in three main forms depending on their habitat and water availability: ammonia (most toxic, requires water), urea (moderately toxic, moderate water loss), and uric acid (least toxic, minimal water loss). Ammonotelic animals (aquatic) excrete ammonia via diffusion; ureotelic animals (mammals, amphibians) convert ammonia to urea in the liver; uricotelic animals (reptiles, birds, insects) excrete uric acid as paste. The type of nitrogenous waste reflects terrestrial adaptation and water conservation needs.
subject: biology

summary: Ammonotelism is the excretion of ammonia as the primary nitrogenous waste, practised by many bony fishes, aquatic amphibians, and aquatic insects. Ammonia is highly toxic and readily soluble, so it is excreted by diffusion across body surfaces or through gill surfaces as ammonium ions. The kidneys play no significant role in ammonia removal.
subject: biology

summary: Ureotelism is the excretion of urea as the primary nitrogenous waste, practised by mammals, many terrestrial amphibians, and marine fishes. Ammonia produced by metabolism is converted into less toxic urea in the liver and released into the blood, which is then filtered and excreted by the kidneys. Some urea is retained in the kidney matrix to maintain desired osmolarity.
subject: biology

summary: Uricotelism is the excretion of uric acid as the primary nitrogenous waste, practised by reptiles, birds, land snails, and insects. Uric acid is the least toxic nitrogenous waste and can be removed with minimal water loss, making it ideal for terrestrial life. It is excreted in the form of a pellet or paste.
subject: biology

summary: Different animal groups possess specialized excretory structures reflecting evolutionary adaptation. Invertebrates have simple structures like protonephridia (flatworms), nephridia (annelids), and Malpighian tubules (insects), while vertebrates have complex kidneys. All these structures function not only in waste removal but also in osmoregulation and ionic balance maintenance.
subject: biology

summary: Protonephridia are excretory structures found in Platyhelminthes (flatworms like Planaria), rotifers, some annelids, and the cephalochordate Amphioxus. These tubular structures are primarily concerned with ionic and fluid volume regulation, i.e., osmoregulation rather than nitrogenous waste removal.
subject: biology

summary: Nephridia are tubular excretory structures found in earthworms and other annelids. They function in removal of nitrogenous wastes and maintenance of fluid and ionic balance, combining excretion with osmoregulation in a single organ system.
subject: biology

summary: Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most insects, including cockroaches. They help in the removal of nitrogenous wastes and osmoregulation, functioning as simple tubular outgrowths from the digestive tract.
subject: biology

summary: Green glands, also called antennal glands, perform the excretory function in crustaceans like prawns. These structures are named for their characteristic colour and location near the antennae.
subject: biology

summary: Kidneys are complex tubular organs found in vertebrates, with structure consisting of an outer cortex and inner medulla divided into medullary pyramids. Each kidney contains approximately one million nephrons, the functional units responsible for urine formation through filtration, reabsorption, and secretion.
diagram_description: Figure 16.1 shows the human urinary system with two kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra; Figure 16.2 shows a longitudinal section of the kidney with cortex, medulla, renal pelvis, and calyces labelled.
subject: biology

summary: The human kidney is a complex organ with two main zones: the outer cortex containing renal corpuscles and segments of renal tubules, and the inner medulla containing loops of Henle and collecting ducts organized into medullary pyramids. The functional unit, the nephron, has two main parts: the glomerulus and renal tubule, which together filter blood and form urine. Urine flows from collection ducts through the renal pelvis and calyces into the ureter.
diagram_description: Figure 16.2 shows kidney cross-section with cortex, medulla, pyramids, pelvis, and calyces; Figures 16.3 and 16.4 detail nephron structure and malpighian body.
subject: biology

summary: Each human kidney is a bean-shaped organ measuring 10-12 cm in length, 5-7 cm in width, and 2-3 cm in thickness, located between the last thoracic and third lumbar vertebra. The hilum is the concave notch where the ureter, blood vessels, and nerves enter; the renal pelvis is a funnel-shaped space with projections called calyces. The cortex extends between medullary pyramids as renal columns called Columns of Bertini.
diagram_description: Figure 16.2 shows kidney size, position, hilum, renal pelvis, calyces, cortex, medulla, and medullary pyramids.
subject: biology

summary: A nephron is the functional unit of the kidney consisting of two main parts: the glomerulus and the renal tubule. The glomerulus is a tuft of capillaries formed by the afferent arteriole (a branch of the renal artery) and drained by the efferent arteriole. The renal tubule consists of Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct.
diagram_description: Figure 16.3 shows the complete nephron structure with afferent and efferent arterioles, glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, and collecting duct.
subject: biology

summary: The Malpighian body (renal corpuscle) is formed by the glomerulus enclosed within Bowman's capsule. Bowman's capsule is a double-walled, cup-like structure with epithelial cells called podocytes arranged to leave minute filtration slits (slit pores) that allow filtration of blood plasma while retaining proteins and blood cells.
diagram_description: Figure 16.4 shows the malpighian body with glomerulus, afferent and efferent arterioles, Bowman's capsule, and proximal convoluted tubule.
subject: biology

summary: The renal tubule consists of four main segments: the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) with brush border epithelium for reabsorption; Henle's loop with descending and ascending limbs for concentration of filtrate; the distal convoluted tubule (DCT) for conditional reabsorption and selective secretion; and the collecting duct extending from cortex to medulla for final water reabsorption and urine concentration.
diagram_description: Figure 16.3 labels all renal tubule segments including PCT, Henle's loop (descending and ascending), DCT, and collecting duct.
subject: biology

summary: The efferent arteriole forms a fine peritubular capillary network surrounding the renal tubule for reabsorption and secretion. A specialized portion called vasa recta runs parallel to Henle's loop in a U-shaped pattern, forming a counter current mechanism essential for urine concentration. Vasa recta is extensive in juxta medullary nephrons (with long loops of Henle) but absent or reduced in cortical nephrons (with short loops).
diagram_description: Figure 16.3 shows vasa recta running parallel to Henle's loop; Figure 16.6 demonstrates the counter current mechanism between Henle's loop and vasa recta.
subject: biology

summary: Urine formation involves three main processes: glomerular filtration (ultrafiltration of blood plasma), reabsorption (selective return of useful substances and water), and tubular secretion (active secretion of wastes and ions). Together these processes convert 180 litres of daily filtrate into approximately 1-1.5 litres of final urine, with nearly 99% of the filtrate being reabsorbed.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows reabsorption and secretion of major substances at different parts of the nephron with arrows indicating direction of movement.
subject: biology

summary: Glomerular filtration is the first step in urine formation where blood is filtered by the glomerulus through three layers: endothelium of glomerular capillaries, basement membrane, and epithelium of Bowman's capsule with filtration slits between podocytes. This process is called ultrafiltration as it is non-selective, allowing all plasma constituents except proteins to pass into Bowman's capsule. The glomerular filtration rate (GFR) in healthy individuals is approximately 125 ml/minute, forming 180 litres of filtrate daily.
diagram_description: Figure 16.4 shows the malpighian body with the three filtration layers and filtration slits between podocytes.
subject: biology

summary: Reabsorption is the selective return of useful substances and water from the filtrate back into the blood through tubular epithelial cells. Nearly 99% of the filtrate is reabsorbed through active mechanisms (glucose, amino acids, Na+, K+) and passive mechanisms (water, nitrogenous wastes), with different segments of the nephron specializing in reabsorption of specific substances.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows reabsorption pathways with upward arrows indicating substances moving from the tubule lumen back into the blood at different nephron segments.
subject: biology

summary: Tubular secretion is the active transport of substances like H+, K+, and ammonia from the tubular epithelial cells into the filtrate. This process is important for maintaining ionic balance, pH of body fluids, and eliminating additional wastes not captured during filtration.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows tubular secretion with downward arrows indicating H+, K+, and NH3 moving from the tubule into the filtrate.
subject: biology

summary: Each segment of the renal tubule has specialized functions: the PCT performs major reabsorption of nutrients and 70-80% of electrolytes and water; Henle's loop creates an osmolarity gradient for urine concentration; the DCT performs conditional reabsorption of Na+ and water; and the collecting duct concentrates urine and maintains pH and ionic balance.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows functional specialization with different substances being reabsorbed and secreted at each segment.
subject: biology

summary: The proximal convoluted tubule is lined with simple cuboidal brush border epithelium that increases surface area for reabsorption. Nearly all essential nutrients and 70-80% of electrolytes and water are reabsorbed here through active transport. The PCT also maintains pH and ionic balance by selective secretion of H+ and ammonia and reabsorption of HCO3−.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows PCT reabsorption of NaCl, nutrients, K+, water, and secretion of H+ and ammonia.
subject: biology

summary: Henle's loop has minimal reabsorption but plays a crucial role in maintaining the osmolarity gradient of the medullary interstitium. The descending limb is permeable to water but impermeable to electrolytes, concentrating the filtrate. The ascending limb is impermeable to water but allows active or passive transport of electrolytes, diluting the filtrate as it rises.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows the descending limb with water reabsorption and the ascending limb with NaCl transport; Figure 16.6 shows osmolarity changes along Henle's loop.
subject: biology

summary: The distal convoluted tubule performs conditional reabsorption of Na+ and water depending on body requirements and hormone levels. It is also capable of reabsorbing HCO3− and selectively secreting H+, K+, and NH3 to maintain pH and sodium-potassium balance in blood.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows DCT reabsorption of NaCl, water, and HCO3−, and secretion of K+ and H+.
subject: biology

summary: The collecting duct extends from cortex to medulla and allows large amounts of water reabsorption to produce concentrated urine. It allows passage of small amounts of urea into the medullary interstitium to maintain osmolarity and maintains pH and ionic balance through selective secretion of H+ and K+ ions.
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows water and urea reabsorption in the collecting duct, and H+ secretion.
subject: biology

summary: Mammals concentrate urine through a sophisticated counter current mechanism involving Henle's loop and vasa recta. The opposing flow of filtrate in the two limbs of Henle's loop and blood in vasa recta, combined with selective permeability and transport, creates an osmolarity gradient increasing from 300 mOsmol L−1 in cortex to 1200 mOsmol L−1 in inner medulla. This gradient, maintained by NaCl and urea, facilitates water reabsorption from the collecting duct, concentrating urine up to four times.
diagram_description: Figure 16.6 shows the complete counter current mechanism with osmolarity values at different levels, NaCl and water movement in Henle's loop, vasa recta, and collecting duct.
subject: biology

summary: The counter current mechanism is the process by which Henle's loop and vasa recta work together to establish and maintain an osmolarity gradient. Filtrate flows in opposite directions in the descending and ascending limbs of Henle's loop, while blood flows in opposite directions in the parallel limbs of vasa recta. NaCl is actively transported by the ascending limb and exchanged with the descending limb via vasa recta, while the collecting duct returns urea to the interstitium, creating an efficient concentration system.
diagram_description: Figure 16.6 demonstrates the counter current mechanism with detailed osmolarity values, NaCl movement, water movement, and urea transport.
subject: biology

summary: The medullary interstitium maintains an increasing osmolarity gradient from the cortex (300 mOsmol L−1) to the inner medulla (1200 mOsmol L−1). This gradient is primarily established by NaCl transported by the ascending limb of Henle's loop and exchanged with vasa recta, and by urea transported from the collecting duct. The gradient is essential for osmotic water reabsorption from the collecting duct.
diagram_description: Figure 16.6 shows the osmolarity gradient values at different levels: 300 mOsmol L−1 at cortex, 600 at outer medulla, 900 at inner medulla, and 1200 at the tip of Henle's loop.
subject: biology

summary: The osmolarity gradient created by the counter current mechanism in Henle's loop facilitates osmotic reabsorption of water from the collecting duct into the medullary interstitium. This process concentrates the remaining filtrate, allowing the kidney to produce urine nearly four times more concentrated than the initial filtrate (from 300 to 1200 mOsmol L−1).
diagram_description: Figure 16.5 shows water reabsorption from the collecting duct; Figure 16.6 illustrates the osmotic gradient driving water movement.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Juxta Glomerular Apparatus and GFR Regulation
summary: Kidney function is efficiently regulated by hormonal feedback mechanisms involving the hypothalamus, juxta glomerular apparatus, and heart. These mechanisms control glomerular filtration rate, water reabsorption, and ionic balance through the actions of ADH, the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, and atrial natriuretic factor.
subject: biology

concept_name: Juxta Glomerular Apparatus and GFR Regulation
summary: The juxta glomerular apparatus (JGA) is a specialized region formed by cellular modifications in the distal convoluted tubule and afferent arteriole. When glomerular blood flow or GFR falls, JG cells release renin, which stimulates glomerular blood flow and restores GFR to normal. This autoregulatory mechanism maintains relatively constant GFR despite fluctuations in blood pressure.
subject: biology

summary: Osmoreceptors in the body activate the hypothalamus to release ADH (vasopressin) from the neurohypophysis in response to excessive fluid loss or increased body osmolarity. ADH facilitates water reabsorption from the distal tubule and collecting duct, preventing diuresis and conserving water. An increase in body fluid volume suppresses ADH release, completing the negative feedback loop. ADH also has vasoconstrictory effects that increase blood pressure and GFR.
subject: biology

summary: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is a complex regulatory mechanism where a fall in glomerular blood flow activates JG cells to release renin. Renin converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, which is further converted to angiotensin II, a powerful vasoconstrictor. Angiotensin II increases glomerular blood pressure and GFR, and also stimulates the adrenal cortex to release aldosterone, which promotes Na+ and water reabsorption from distal tubule, further increasing blood pressure.
subject: biology

summary: Atrial natriuretic factor (ANF) is released from the heart atria in response to increased blood flow. ANF causes vasodilation (dilation of blood vessels) and thereby decreases blood pressure, acting as a check on the renin-angiotensin mechanism. This dual regulation ensures appropriate blood pressure and kidney function.
subject: biology

summary: Micturition is the voluntary process of releasing urine from the urinary bladder. It is initiated when stretch receptors in the bladder walls send signals to the central nervous system as the bladder fills. The CNS responds by sending motor messages that cause contraction of smooth bladder muscles and relaxation of the urethral sphincter, expelling urine through the urethra. The neural mechanism controlling this process is called the micturition reflex.
subject: biology

summary: The micturition process is controlled by the micturition reflex, which is initiated when stretch receptors in the bladder walls detect filling. These receptors send sensory signals to the central nervous system, which responds with motor messages causing contraction of smooth bladder muscles and simultaneous relaxation of the urethral sphincter, thereby releasing urine through the urethra. This process is partially voluntary and can be controlled by higher brain centres.
subject: biology

summary: An adult human excretes on average 1 to 1.5 litres of urine per day. Urine is a light yellow coloured watery fluid that is slightly acidic (pH 6.0) with a characteristic odour. On average, 25-30 gm of urea is excreted per day. Various conditions can affect urine characteristics, and analysis of urine helps in clinical diagnosis of metabolic disorders such as diabetes mellitus (indicated by presence of glucose or ketone bodies).
subject: biology

concept_name: Role of Other Organs in Excretion
summary: In addition to kidneys, other organs contribute to excretion: lungs remove CO2 and water; liver secretes bile containing bilirubin, cholesterol, and degraded hormones; and skin's sweat and sebaceous glands eliminate NaCl, urea, lactic acid, sterols, and hydrocarbons. These organs work alongside kidneys to maintain internal homeostasis and eliminate diverse waste products.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Role of Other Organs in Excretion
summary: The lungs remove large amounts of carbon dioxide (approximately 200 mL/minute) produced by cellular respiration, and also eliminate significant quantities of water through respiration. This pulmonary excretion is a major pathway for CO2 elimination and contributes to water loss from the body.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Role of Other Organs in Excretion
summary: The liver, the largest gland in the body, secretes bile containing waste products including bilirubin and biliverdin (from haemoglobin breakdown), cholesterol, degraded steroid hormones, vitamins, and drugs. Most of these substances pass out with digestive wastes through the faeces, making the liver an important excretory organ.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Role of Other Organs in Excretion
summary: The sweat glands in skin secrete a watery fluid containing NaCl, small amounts of urea, and lactic acid. Although the primary function of sweat is thermoregulation (cooling the body), it also helps eliminate some wastes. Sebaceous glands eliminate sterols, hydrocarbons, and waxes through sebum, which provides a protective oily covering for the skin. Small amounts of nitrogenous wastes can also be eliminated through saliva.
subject: biology

summary: Kidney malfunction can lead to serious disorders including uremia (accumulation of urea in blood), chronic kidney failure, and other renal diseases. Treatment options include haemodialysis (artificial kidney filtration) and kidney transplantation, while renal calculi (kidney stones) and glomerulonephritis represent other common kidney pathologies.
subject: biology

summary: Uremia is a condition caused by malfunction of kidneys leading to accumulation of urea in the blood. This accumulation is highly harmful and can lead to acute kidney failure if not treated. The condition results from the kidneys' inability to filter nitrogenous wastes from the blood.
subject: biology

summary: Haemodialysis is a medical process for removing urea from the blood in patients with kidney failure. Blood from an artery is pumped into a dialysing unit containing a coiled cellophane tube surrounded by dialysing fluid with the same composition as plasma except for nitrogenous wastes. Urea and other wastes diffuse out based on the concentration gradient while useful substances are retained, and the cleaned blood is returned to the body through a vein.
subject: biology

summary: Kidney transplantation is the ultimate method for correcting acute renal failures (kidney failure). A functioning kidney from a donor, preferably a close relative to minimize immune rejection, is surgically grafted to the recipient. Modern clinical procedures have significantly increased the success rate of kidney transplantation.
subject: biology

summary: Renal calculi are stones or insoluble masses of crystallised salts (such as oxalates) that form within the kidney, potentially causing obstruction and pain. Glomerulonephritis is inflammation of the glomeruli of the kidney, affecting the filtration process. Both are common kidney disorders requiring clinical management.
subject: biology

summary: Human body cells exhibit three main types of movement: amoeboid (via pseudopodia in cells like macrophages and leucocytes), ciliary (coordinated beating in epithelial tissues), and muscular (contraction of skeletal, visceral, or cardiac muscles). Locomotion is a voluntary movement resulting in change of location and is distinct from other non-locomotory movements.
subject: biology

summary: Amoeboid movement is effected by pseudopodia formed by the streaming of protoplasm, similar to movement in Amoeba. Specialized cells like macrophages and leucocytes in blood exhibit this movement, which involves cytoskeletal elements such as microfilaments.
subject: biology

summary: Ciliary movement occurs in most internal tubular organs lined by ciliated epithelium and involves coordinated beating of cilia. The coordinated movements of cilia in the trachea help remove dust particles and foreign substances from inhaled air, and facilitate the passage of ova through the female reproductive tract.
subject: biology

summary: Muscular movement involves the contractile property of muscles and is essential for limb movement, jaw movement, and tongue control. Locomotion requires perfect coordinated activity of muscular, skeletal, and neural systems working together.
subject: biology

summary: Muscles are classified into three types based on location and appearance: skeletal (striated, voluntary), visceral (smooth, involuntary), and cardiac (striated, involuntary). Skeletal muscles comprise 40-50% of body weight in adults and possess properties of excitability, contractility, extensibility, and elasticity.
subject: biology

summary: Skeletal muscles are closely associated with skeletal components and appear striated under the microscope. They are under voluntary nervous system control and are primarily involved in locomotory actions and changes in body postures.
subject: biology

summary: Visceral muscles are located in the inner walls of hollow visceral organs like the alimentary canal and reproductive tract and lack striation. They are involuntary muscles not under nervous system control and assist in transporting food through the digestive tract and gametes through the genital tract.
subject: biology

summary: Cardiac muscles are the muscles of the heart, assembled in a branching pattern. They appear striated but are involuntary in nature, with activities not directly controlled by the nervous system.
subject: biology

summary: Each organized skeletal muscle is composed of muscle bundles (fascicles) held together by connective tissue (fascia), and each bundle contains muscle fibers. Muscle fibers contain parallelly arranged myofibrils, which are subdivided into functional units called sarcomeres that exhibit characteristic banding patterns.
diagram_description: Figure 17.1 shows a diagrammatic cross-sectional view of muscle with fascicles, muscle fibers, sarcolemma, and blood capillaries labeled.
subject: biology

summary: Each muscle bundle (fascicle) is held together by fascia, a collagenous connective tissue layer, and contains multiple muscle fibers. Each muscle fiber is lined by sarcolemma (plasma membrane) enclosing sarcoplasm, which is multinucleate (syncitium) and contains sarcoplasmic reticulum as a calcium ion storage house.
diagram_description: Figure 17.1 labels fascicles, muscle fibers, sarcolemma, and blood capillaries in cross-section.
subject: biology

summary: Myofibrils contain parallelly arranged myofilaments showing alternating dark (A) and light (I) bands due to distribution of actin and myosin proteins. The sarcomere, the functional unit of contraction between two Z lines, contains the A band with thick myosin filaments, I bands with thin actin filaments, and the H zone (central part of A band not overlapped by actin).
diagram_description: Figure 17.2 shows sarcomere anatomy with Z line, A band, I band, and H zone labeled in both microscopy image and diagram.
subject: biology

summary: Actin (thin) filaments are made of two F-actin helices (polymers of G-actin monomers) with tropomyosin and troponin proteins; in resting state, troponin masks myosin binding sites. Myosin (thick) filaments are polymerized meromyosins with globular heads (HMM—heavy meromyosin) containing ATPase activity and binding sites for ATP and actin, connected to tail (LMM—light meromyosin).
diagram_description: Figure 17.3 shows actin filament structure with F actin, tropomyosin, and troponin; and myosin monomer (meromyosin) with head, cross arm, ATP binding sites, and actin binding sites.
subject: biology

summary: Muscle contraction occurs via the sliding filament theory, whereby thin filaments slide over thick filaments. Contraction is initiated by a motor neuron signal that triggers calcium ion release from sarcoplasmic reticulum, activating actin and enabling myosin-actin cross-bridge formation and cycling powered by ATP hydrolysis.
diagram_description: Figure 17.4 shows stages of cross-bridge formation, rotation of myosin head, and breaking of cross-bridge with ATP and ADP involvement.
subject: biology

summary: The sliding filament theory states that muscle contraction occurs by the sliding of thin filaments over thick filaments. During contraction, I bands reduce in length while A bands retain their length, and Z lines move closer together due to thin filament movement.
diagram_description: Figure 17.5 illustrates sliding-filament theory showing relaxed, contracting, and maximally contracted states with movement of thin filaments and relative sizes of I band and H zones.
subject: biology

summary: Muscle contraction is initiated by a signal from the central nervous system via a motor neuron; a motor neuron and its connected muscle fibers form a motor unit. The neuromuscular junction (motor-end plate) is where neural signal releases acetylcholine, generating an action potential in sarcolemma that spreads through the muscle fiber.
subject: biology

summary: Increased Ca++ ions bind to troponin subunits on actin, removing the masking of myosin binding sites.
