Myosin heads, powered by ATP hydrolysis, bind to exposed actin sites forming cross-bridges, then rotate and pull actin filaments toward the A band center, pulling Z lines inward and shortening the sarcomere; new ATP binding breaks the cross-bridge and the cycle repeats.
diagram_description: Figure 17.4 shows the four stages: ATP hydrolysis, cross-bridge formation, sliding/rotation, and breaking of cross-bridge.
subject: biology

summary: Muscle relaxation occurs when Ca++ ions are pumped back to sarcoplasmic cisternae, masking actin binding sites and allowing Z lines to return to original position. Repeated muscle activation leads to anaerobic glycogen breakdown, accumulating lactic acid and causing muscle fatigue.
subject: biology

summary: Muscles are classified as red or white fibers based on myoglobin content. Red fibers contain high myoglobin (oxygen-storing pigment), abundant mitochondria for aerobic ATP production, and are used for sustained activities; white fibers have low myoglobin, few mitochondria, high sarcoplasmic reticulum, and rely on anaerobic processes for energy.
subject: biology

summary: Red fibers contain high myoglobin content giving them a reddish appearance and possess abundant mitochondria that utilize large amounts of stored oxygen for ATP production. These aerobic muscles are suited for sustained activities.
subject: biology

summary: White fibers contain very low myoglobin content, appearing pale or whitish, with few mitochondria but high sarcoplasmic reticulum. They depend on anaerobic processes for energy production.
subject: biology

summary: The skeletal system comprises 206 bones and cartilages forming a framework for movement and support. It is divided into two principal divisions: axial skeleton (skull, vertebral column, sternum, ribs) and appendicular skeleton (limb bones with their girdles).
subject: biology

summary: The axial skeleton comprises 80 bones distributed along the main axis of the body, including the skull (22 bones), vertebral column (26 vertebrae), sternum, and 12 pairs of ribs. It provides the central support framework and protects vital organs.
diagram_description: Figures 17.6, 17.7, and 17.8 show the skull, vertebral column, and rib cage components respectively.
subject: biology

summary: The appendicular skeleton comprises the bones of the limbs (30 bones per limb) along with their girdles. It includes the pectoral girdle (clavicle and scapula) for upper limb articulation and the pelvic girdle (coxal bones) for lower limb articulation with the axial skeleton.
diagram_description: Figures 17.9 and 17.10 show pectoral and pelvic girdles with upper and lower limb bones respectively.
subject: biology

summary: The skull is composed of 22 bones organized into cranial (8 bones forming the protective cranium) and facial (14 bones forming the front) regions. It includes the U-shaped hyoid bone at the base of the buccal cavity, three ear ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) in each middle ear, and articulates with the vertebral column via two occipital condyles (dicondylic skull).
diagram_description: Figure 17.6 shows a diagrammatic view of the human skull with all major bones labeled.
subject: biology

summary: The vertebral column consists of 26 serially arranged vertebrae extending from the skull base and forming the main trunk framework. It is differentiated into cervical (7), thoracic (12), lumbar (5), sacral (1-fused), and coccygeal (1-fused) regions; each vertebra has a neural canal protecting the spinal cord, and the first (atlas) articulates with occipital condyles.
diagram_description: Figure 17.7 shows the vertebral column in right lateral view with cervical, thoracic, lumbar vertebrae, sacrum, and coccyx labeled, plus intervertebral disc.
subject: biology

summary: There are 12 pairs of ribs: 7 pairs of true ribs (attached to sternum via hyaline cartilage), 3 pairs of vertebrochondral (false) ribs (joining the seventh rib), and 2 pairs of floating ribs (not ventrally connected). The rib cage is formed by thoracic vertebrae, ribs, and sternum together.
diagram_description: Figure 17.8 shows ribs and rib cage with true ribs, false ribs, floating ribs, sternum, and vertebral column labeled.
subject: biology

summary: Each upper limb (forelimb) contains 30 bones: humerus, radius, ulna, 8 carpals (wrist), 5 metacarpals (palm), and 14 phalanges (digits). The lower limb (hindlimb) also has 30 bones: femur (longest bone), tibia, fibula, 7 tarsals (ankle), 5 metatarsals, 14 phalanges, and a patella (knee cap) covering the knee ventrally.
diagram_description: Figures 17.9 and 17.10 show right pectoral and pelvic girdles with upper and lower limb bones in frontal view.
subject: biology

summary: The pectoral girdle (each half: clavicle and scapula) articulates upper limbs with the axial skeleton; the scapula has a glenoid cavity for humerus articulation. The pelvic girdle consists of two coxal bones (each fused from ilium, ischium, pubis) with an acetabulum cavity for femur articulation; the two halves meet ventrally at the pubic symphysis.
diagram_description: Figures 17.9 and 17.10 show pectoral girdle with clavicle and scapula, and pelvic girdle with ilium, ischium, pubis, and acetabulum.
subject: biology

summary: Joints are points of contact between bones or between bones and cartilages where muscles generate force to create movement. Joints are classified into three major structural types: fibrous (no movement), cartilaginous (limited movement), and synovial (considerable movement), with movability depending on structural characteristics.
subject: biology

summary: Fibrous joints do not allow any movement and are shown by flat skull bones which fuse end-to-end with dense fibrous connective tissue forming sutures that create the cranium.
subject: biology

summary: In cartilaginous joints, bones are joined together with cartilages, permitting limited movements. The joints between adjacent vertebrae in the vertebral column are examples of this joint type.
subject: biology

summary: Synovial joints are characterized by a fluid-filled synovial cavity between articulating bone surfaces, allowing considerable movement essential for locomotion. Types include ball and socket (humerus-pectoral girdle), hinge (knee), pivot (atlas-axis), gliding (carpals), and saddle (carpal-metacarpal of thumb) joints.
subject: biology

concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: The muscular and skeletal system can be affected by various disorders including autoimmune conditions (myasthenia gravis), genetic degeneration (muscular dystrophy), electrolyte imbalances (tetany), inflammatory conditions (arthritis, gout), and age-related disorders (osteoporosis).
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder affecting the neuromuscular junction, leading to fatigue, weakening, and paralysis of skeletal muscles.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Muscular dystrophy is a progressive degeneration of skeletal muscle, mostly due to genetic disorder.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Tetany is characterized by rapid spasms or wild contractions in muscle due to low calcium ion concentration in body fluid.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Arthritis is inflammation of joints.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Osteoporosis is an age-related disorder characterized by decreased bone mass and increased chances of fractures, with decreased estrogen levels being a common cause.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System
summary: Gout is inflammation of joints due to accumulation of uric acid crystals.
subject: biology

summary: The neural system is composed of specialized cells called neurons that detect, receive, and transmit stimuli. In humans, it is divided into the central neural system (CNS: brain and spinal cord) and peripheral neural system (PNS: all nerves associated with CNS). Afferent fibres transmit impulses from tissues to CNS, while efferent fibres carry regulatory impulses from CNS to peripheral tissues.
subject: biology

summary: The neural system varies in complexity across animal phyla. Lower invertebrates like Hydra have simple neural networks, insects have organized brains with ganglia and neural tissues, and vertebrates possess more developed neural systems. The increasing complexity reflects greater coordination and integration of functions.
subject: biology

summary: The human neural system is divided into two main parts: the central neural system (CNS), which includes the brain and spinal cord and serves as the site of information processing and control, and the peripheral neural system (PNS), which comprises all nerves of the body associated with the CNS. The PNS further divides into somatic neural system (controlling skeletal muscles) and autonomic neural system (controlling involuntary organs and smooth muscles).
subject: biology

summary: The PNS contains two types of nerve fibres: afferent fibres that transmit impulses from tissues and organs to the CNS, and efferent fibres that transmit regulatory impulses from the CNS to peripheral tissues and organs. This bidirectional communication allows sensory input and motor response.
subject: biology

aliases: Structural and Functional Unit of Neural System
summary: A neuron is a specialized cell composed of three major parts: the cell body containing Nissl's granules, dendrites that transmit impulses toward the cell body, and an axon that conducts impulses away from the cell body to synapses. The axon terminal contains synaptic vesicles with neurotransmitters for synaptic transmission.
diagram_description: Figure 18.1 shows neuron structure with labeled dendrites, cell body, nucleus, Nissl's granules, Schwann cell, myelin sheath, node of Ranvier, axon terminal, and synaptic knob.
subject: biology

summary: The neuron comprises a cell body containing cytoplasm and Nissl's granules, multiple dendrites that branch and receive impulses, and a long axon with branched distal endings. Each axon branch terminates in a synaptic knob containing synaptic vesicles filled with neurotransmitters for communication with other neurons.
diagram_description: Figure 18.1 illustrates complete neuron anatomy with all three major components labeled.
subject: biology

summary: Neurons are classified by the number of axons and dendrites: multipolar neurons (one axon, two or more dendrites; found in cerebral cortex), bipolar neurons (one axon, one dendrite; found in retina), and unipolar neurons (one axon only; found in embryonic stage). This classification reflects structural differences related to neural function and location.
subject: biology

summary: Myelinated axons are wrapped in myelin sheaths formed by Schwann cells, with gaps between sheaths called nodes of Ranvier; found in spinal and cranial nerves. Non-myelinated axons are enclosed by Schwann cells that do not form a myelin sheath; commonly found in autonomic and somatic neural systems. Myelin sheath provides insulation and allows faster impulse conduction.
diagram_description: Figure 18.1 shows myelin sheath, Schwann cell, and nodes of Ranvier in axon structure.
subject: biology

aliases: Generation and Conduction of Nerve Impulse
summary: Nerve impulse generation and conduction depend on the movement of ions across the axonal membrane. The resting potential is maintained by the sodium-potassium pump, and stimulation causes rapid Na+ influx leading to depolarization (action potential). This depolarization spreads sequentially along the axon, followed by K+ efflux restoring the resting potential.
diagram_description: Figure 18.2 shows diagrammatic representation of impulse conduction through axon at points A and B, with + and - charges indicating polarity changes.
subject: biology

summary: The resting membrane potential exists because ion channels are selectively permeable: the axonal membrane is highly permeable to K+ but nearly impermeable to Na+. The sodium-potassium pump actively transports 3 Na+ outward for 2 K+ inward, creating ionic gradients. This results in a negative charge inside the axon and positive charge outside, maintaining the polarized state.
subject: biology

summary: When a stimulus is applied to the polarized membrane, it becomes freely permeable to Na+, causing rapid Na+ influx that reverses polarity at that site (outer surface becomes negative, inner becomes positive). This reversed polarity is called depolarization and represents the action potential or nerve impulse. Repolarization occurs as K+ rapidly diffuses outward, restoring the resting potential.
diagram_description: Figure 18.2 illustrates depolarization at point A and the subsequent action potential spread to point B.
subject: biology

summary: When an action potential is generated at one site (point A), current flows on the inner surface to adjacent sites (point B), causing the outer surface to become depolarized. This sequential reversal of polarity propagates the action potential along the entire length of the axon. The impulse conduction is a continuous wave of depolarization and repolarization moving from the soma to the axon terminal.
diagram_description: Figure 18.2 shows current flow from point A to point B on inner and outer surfaces, demonstrating sequential depolarization.
subject: biology

summary: A synapse is the junction between a pre-synaptic neuron and post-synaptic neuron, either with or without a synaptic cleft. Two types exist: electrical synapses (rare, direct current flow) and chemical synapses (separated by synaptic cleft, involve neurotransmitter release). Neurotransmitters bind to receptors on the post-synaptic membrane, generating either excitatory or inhibitory potentials.
diagram_description: Figure 18.3 shows synapse structure with axon terminal, synaptic vesicles, synaptic cleft, pre-synaptic membrane, post-synaptic membrane, neurotransmitters, and receptors.
subject: biology

summary: A synapse is formed by the membranes of a pre-synaptic neuron and post-synaptic neuron. The membranes may or may not be separated by a gap called the synaptic cleft. At electrical synapses, membranes are in close proximity allowing direct current flow. At chemical synapses, a fluid-filled synaptic cleft separates the neurons, requiring neurotransmitter-mediated communication.
diagram_description: Figure 18.3 illustrates synapse anatomy including pre-synaptic and post-synaptic membranes separated by synaptic cleft.
subject: biology

summary: At chemical synapses, an action potential arriving at the axon terminal stimulates synaptic vesicles containing neurotransmitters to fuse with the pre-synaptic membrane and release their contents into the synaptic cleft. Neurotransmitters bind to specific receptors on the post-synaptic membrane, opening ion channels and generating either excitatory or inhibitory potentials in the post-synaptic neuron.
diagram_description: Figure 18.3 shows synaptic vesicles, neurotransmitter release into synaptic cleft, and receptors on post-synaptic membrane.
subject: biology

summary: At electrical synapses, pre- and post-synaptic membranes are in very close proximity, allowing electrical current to flow directly from one neuron into the other. Transmission is identical to impulse conduction along a single axon and is faster than chemical synaptic transmission. Electrical synapses are rare in the human nervous system.
subject: biology

summary: The brain is the central information processing organ that controls voluntary movements, vital organ functions, thermoregulation, circadian rhythms, and higher functions like vision, hearing, memory, and emotions. It is divided into forebrain (cerebrum, thalamus, hypothalamus), midbrain, and hindbrain (pons, cerebellum, medulla). The brain is protected by the skull and covered by three meningeal layers: dura mater, arachnoid, and pia mater.
diagram_description: Figure 18.4 shows sagittal section of human brain with major structures labeled including cerebral hemispheres, thalamus, hypothalamus, cerebral aqueduct, midbrain, pons, cerebellum, medulla, and spinal cord.
subject: biology

summary: The forebrain comprises the cerebrum (divided by corpus callosum into left and right hemispheres), thalamus, and hypothalamus. The cerebral cortex (grey matter) contains motor, sensory, and association areas; association areas handle memory and communication. The thalamus is the sensory-motor coordinating center. The hypothalamus controls body temperature, hunger, drinking, and secretes hypothalamic hormones. The limbic system (involving amygdala, hippocampus, and inner cerebral structures) regulates emotions, sexual behavior, and motivation.
diagram_description: Figure 18.4 shows cerebrum, corpus callosum, cerebral hemispheres, thalamus, and hypothalamus in sagittal brain section.
subject: biology

summary: The midbrain is located between the forebrain and hindbrain, with a canal called the cerebral aqueduct passing through it. Its dorsal portion contains four round swellings called corpora quadrigemina. The midbrain receives and integrates visual, tactile, and auditory inputs, serving as a relay and processing center.
diagram_description: Figure 18.4 shows midbrain location between thalamus and pons, with cerebral aqueduct labeled.
subject: biology

summary: The hindbrain comprises pons, cerebellum, and medulla (medulla oblongata). Pons contains fibre tracts interconnecting different brain regions. The cerebellum has a highly convoluted surface housing many neurons that integrate information from semicircular canals and auditory system for motor coordination. The medulla connects the brain to spinal cord and contains vital centers controlling respiration, cardiovascular reflexes, and gastric secretions.
diagram_description: Figure 18.4 labels pons, cerebellum, and medulla in the hindbrain region.
subject: biology

summary: The brain is protected by the skull and covered by three layers of cranial meninges: an outer layer called dura mater, a thin middle layer called arachnoid, and an inner layer called pia mater (in contact with brain tissue). These protective layers cushion the brain and allow for cerebrospinal fluid circulation.
subject: biology

summary: Coordination is the process through which two or more organs interact and complement each other's functions to maintain homeostasis. During physical exercise, coordination involves increased oxygen supply, respiration rate, heart beat, and blood flow; when exercise stops, activities gradually return to normal. The neural system and endocrine system jointly coordinate organ activities to function in synchronized fashion.
subject: biology

summary: Coordination is the process by which two or more organs interact and complement the functions of one another. It is essential for maintaining homeostasis and synchronized functioning of all body systems. Example: during physical exercise, muscles, lungs, heart, blood vessels, and kidneys coordinate to increase oxygen supply and meet energy demands.
subject: biology

summary: The neural system and endocrine system jointly coordinate and integrate all organ activities. The neural system provides an organized network of point-to-point connections for quick coordination through nerve impulses. The endocrine system provides chemical integration through hormones. Together, they ensure synchronized functioning of all body systems and maintain homeostasis.
subject: biology

summary: The endocrine system comprises ductless glands and diffused tissues that secrete hormones as intercellular messengers produced in trace amounts. Hormones regulate growth, metabolism, and development of body organs and functions. The system includes hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, pancreas, parathyroid, thymus, and gonads, coordinating with the neural system for body regulation.
diagram_description: Figure 19.1 shows the location of endocrine glands including hypothalamus, pituitary, pineal, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, pancreas, adrenal, and gonads (testis/ovary).
subject: biology

summary: Endocrine glands lack ducts and secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream. Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals that act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts. The modern definition encompasses molecules from organized endocrine glands and other hormone-producing cells.
subject: biology

summary: The hypothalamus, located in the diencephalon, contains neurosecretory cells that produce releasing and inhibiting hormones to regulate pituitary function. Hypothalamic hormones reach the anterior pituitary through a portal circulatory system and regulate synthesis and secretion of pituitary hormones. The posterior pituitary is under direct neural regulation of the hypothalamus.
diagram_description: Figure 19.2 shows the diagrammatic relationship between hypothalamus, anterior pituitary, posterior pituitary, hypothalamic neurons, and portal circulation.
subject: biology

summary: Major endocrine glands are distributed throughout the body: pituitary in the sella tursica, pineal on the dorsal forebrain, thyroid on either side of the trachea, parathyroid on the dorsal thyroid surface, thymus between the lungs, adrenals above the kidneys, pancreas in the abdomen, and gonads in the scrotum (testis) or abdomen (ovary).
diagram_description: Figure 19.1 illustrates the anatomical locations of all major endocrine glands in the human body.
subject: biology

summary: The pituitary gland, located in the sella tursica beneath the hypothalamus, is divided into adenohypophysis (anterior pituitary and pars intermedia) and neurohypophysis (posterior pituitary). The anterior pituitary produces six trophic hormones regulating growth and peripheral endocrine glands. The posterior pituitary stores and releases hormones synthesized by the hypothalamus.
diagram_description: Figure 19.2 shows the pituitary gland structure with anterior and posterior regions, connected to hypothalamus via portal circulation and neural connections.
subject: biology

summary: The anterior pituitary secretes six hormones: growth hormone (stimulates body growth), prolactin (regulates mammary gland development and milk production), TSH (stimulates thyroid hormone synthesis), ACTH (stimulates adrenal glucocorticoid secretion), and gonadotrophins LH and FSH (regulate gonadal activity and gamete production). Pars intermedia secretes melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) regulating skin pigmentation.
subject: biology

summary: The posterior pituitary stores and releases oxytocin and vasopressin, synthesized by the hypothalamus. Oxytocin stimulates smooth muscle contraction, uterine contractions during childbirth, and milk ejection. Vasopressin (ADH) acts on renal tubules to stimulate water and electrolyte resorption, reducing urine output.
subject: biology

summary: Over-secretion of growth hormone causes abnormal growth leading to gigantism in children or acromegaly (severe facial disfigurement) in adults with serious complications. Under-secretion results in pituitary dwarfism with stunted growth. Acromegaly is difficult to diagnose in early stages but can lead to premature death if unchecked.
subject: biology

summary: The thyroid gland, located on either side of the trachea, synthesizes T4 (thyroxine) and T3 (triiodothyronine) requiring iodine for normal synthesis. Thyroid hormones regulate basal metabolic rate, growth, development, and metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Thyrocalcitonin (TCT) from thyroid C-cells regulates blood calcium levels.
diagram_description: Figure 19.3 shows ventral view of thyroid lobes on either side of trachea connected by isthmus, and dorsal view showing parathyroid glands embedded in thyroid lobes.
subject: biology

summary: Thyroid hormones T4 and T3 regulate basal metabolic rate, support red blood cell formation, and control metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. They maintain water and electrolyte balance and are essential for normal physical and mental development. Iodine is essential for their synthesis; deficiency causes hypothyroidism and goitre.
subject: biology

summary: Iodine deficiency causes hypothyroidism and goitre (thyroid enlargement); during pregnancy it leads to cretinism (stunted growth and mental retardation). Hyperthyroidism from thyroid cancer or nodules causes excessive hormone synthesis with increased metabolism and weight loss. Exophthalmic goitre (Graves' disease) is a form of hyperthyroidism with eyeball protrusion and high basal metabolic rate.
subject: biology

summary: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases blood Ca2+ levels by stimulating bone resorption (demineralization), renal tubule reabsorption of Ca2+, and intestinal Ca2+ absorption. PTH is a hypercalcemic hormone regulating calcium homeostasis alongside thyrocalcitonin. Four parathyroid glands, one pair in each thyroid lobe, secrete PTH based on circulating calcium ion levels.
subject: biology

summary: The adrenal gland, located above each kidney, comprises centrally located medulla and outer cortex. The medulla secretes catecholamines (adrenaline and noradrenaline) during stress. The cortex has three zones secreting glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and androgenic steroids regulating metabolism, electrolytes, and sexual characteristics.
diagram_description: Figure 19.4 shows adrenal gland location above kidney and section view showing adrenal cortex and adrenal medulla.
subject: biology

summary: Adrenal medulla secretes adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine), called catecholamines or emergency hormones, rapidly released during stress. These hormones increase alertness, pupil dilation, heart beat, respiration rate, and stimulate glycogenolysis and lipolysis increasing blood glucose and lipid mobilization for fight-or-flight response.
subject: biology

summary: The adrenal cortex has three zones secreting corticoids: glucocorticoids (cortisol) stimulate gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, proteolysis, and suppress immune response; mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) regulate Na+ reabsorption and K+ excretion maintaining electrolyte balance; and small amounts of androgens. Underproduction causes Addison's disease with weakness and fatigue.
subject: biology

summary: The endocrine pancreas consists of Islets of Langerhans (1-2 million per pancreas) containing α-cells secreting glucagon and β-cells secreting insulin. These hormones jointly maintain blood glucose homeostasis through opposite actions on hepatocytes and adipocytes. Glucagon is hyperglycemic; insulin is hypoglycemic.
subject: biology

summary: Insulin, a peptide hormone from β-cells, enhances cellular glucose uptake and utilization in hepatocytes and adipocytes, decreasing blood glucose to hypoglycemic levels. Insulin stimulates glycogenesis (glucose-to-glycogen conversion) and promotes glucose storage. It is the major hormone for glucose homeostasis and metabolic regulation.
subject: biology

summary: Glucagon, a peptide hormone from α-cells, stimulates glycogenolysis (glycogen breakdown) and gluconeogenesis in liver, raising blood glucose to hyperglycemic levels. Glucagon reduces cellular glucose uptake and utilization. It acts antagonistically to insulin, maintaining glucose availability during fasting or stress.
subject: biology

summary: Prolonged hyperglycemia leads to diabetes mellitus, a complex disorder with glucose loss in urine and formation of harmful ketone bodies. Caused by insulin deficiency or resistance, it disrupts glucose homeostasis. Diabetic patients are successfully treated with insulin therapy to restore normal glucose regulation.
subject: biology

summary: The thymus gland, located between lungs behind the sternum, and the pineal gland, on the dorsal forebrain, are complementary endocrine tissues. Thymus secretes thymosins promoting T-lymphocyte differentiation for cell-mediated immunity and antibody production. Pineal secretes melatonin regulating 24-hour body rhythms.
subject: biology

summary: The thymus gland, a lobular structure between lungs, secretes thymosins playing a major role in T-lymphocyte differentiation providing cell-mediated immunity. Thymosins also promote antibody production for humoral immunity. Thymus degenerates in old age, reducing thymosins and weakening immune responses.
subject: biology

summary: The pineal gland, located on the dorsal forebrain, secretes melatonin regulating 24-hour (diurnal) body rhythms including sleep-wake cycles and body temperature. Melatonin also influences metabolism, skin pigmentation, menstrual cycle, and immune defense capability. It serves as a biological clock for the body.
subject: biology

summary: Gonads (testis and ovary) are primary sex organs with dual endocrine functions. Testis produces androgens (testosterone) from Leydig cells regulating male sexual development and reproduction. Ovary produces estrogen and progesterone controlling female sexual development, ovulation, and pregnancy maintenance.
subject: biology

summary: Testis, located in the scrotal sac, produces androgens (mainly testosterone) from Leydig cells in intertubular spaces. Androgens regulate development and maturation of male accessory organs, stimulate muscular growth, facial/axillary hair growth, and play a major stimulatory role in spermatogenesis. They also influence male sexual behaviour and produce anabolic effects on protein and carbohydrate metabolism.
subject: biology

summary: Ovary, located in the abdomen, produces estrogen from ovarian follicles and progesterone from corpus luteum after ovulation. Estrogen stimulates growth of female secondary sex organs, development of secondary sex characters, and regulates female sexual behaviour. Progesterone supports pregnancy and stimulates mammary gland alveoli development and milk secretion.
subject: biology

summary: Non-endocrine tissues also secrete hormones and growth factors. The heart's atrial wall secretes atrial natriuretic factor (ANF) decreasing blood pressure. Kidney juxtaglomerular cells produce erythropoietin stimulating red blood cell formation. Gastrointestinal tract endocrine cells secrete gastrin, secretin, cholecystokinin, and gastric inhibitory peptide regulating digestive function.
subject: biology

summary: Atrial natriuretic factor (ANF), secreted by the atrial wall of the heart, is a peptide hormone that decreases blood pressure by causing dilation of blood vessels when pressure rises. Erythropoietin, produced by kidney juxtaglomerular cells, is a peptide hormone stimulating erythropoiesis (formation of red blood cells). These hormones regulate critical homeostatic functions.
subject: biology

summary: Endocrine cells in the gastrointestinal tract secrete four major peptide hormones: gastrin stimulates gastric gland secretion of HCl and pepsinogen; secretin stimulates pancreatic water and bicarbonate ion secretion; cholecystokinin (CCK) stimulates pancreatic enzyme and bile juice secretion; and gastric inhibitory peptide (GIP) inhibits gastric secretion and motility. Growth factors from non-endocrine tissues are essential for tissue growth and repair.
subject: biology

summary: Hormones produce effects on target tissues by binding to specific hormone receptors present only in target tissues. Membrane-bound receptors generate second messengers (cyclic AMP, IP₃, Ca²⁺) regulating cellular metabolism. Intracellular receptors interact with the genome regulating gene expression. The hormone-receptor complex initiates biochemical changes producing physiological and developmental effects.
diagram_description: Figure 19.5 shows two mechanisms: (a) protein hormone binding to membrane receptor generating second messengers leading to biochemical and physiological responses; (b) steroid hormone binding to intracellular receptor entering nucleus to interact with genome producing mRNA, proteins, and tissue growth.

builds_upon.concept_name: Nervous vs Hormonal Communication in Multicellular Organisms

subject: biology

summary: Each hormone receptor is specific to one hormone only, ensuring hormonal specificity and precise target tissue response. Hormones produce effects exclusively on target tissues bearing specific receptors. Binding of hormone to receptor forms a hormone-receptor complex triggering biochemical changes that regulate target tissue metabolism and physiological functions.

builds_upon.concept_name: Nervous vs Hormonal Communication in Multicellular Organisms

subject: biology

summary: Hormones interacting with membrane-bound receptors do not enter target cells but generate second messengers (e.g., cyclic AMP, IP₃, Ca²⁺) within the cell. These second messengers regulate cellular metabolism, enzyme activity, and gene expression initiating physiological responses. This amplification cascade allows low hormone concentrations to produce large cellular effects.
diagram_description: Figure 19.5a shows FSH binding to ovarian cell membrane receptor, initiating response 1 generating second messengers (cyclic AMP or Ca²⁺) leading to biochemical and physiological responses (ovarian growth).
subject: biology

summary: Steroid hormones and iodothyronines bind intracellular receptors (mostly nuclear) and the hormone-receptor complex interacts with the genome regulating gene expression. This interaction increases mRNA synthesis and protein production leading to physiological and developmental effects like tissue growth and differentiation. This mechanism produces longer-lasting effects compared to second messenger systems.
diagram_description: Figure 19.5b shows estrogen entering uterine cell, binding intracellular receptor, hormone-receptor complex entering nucleus, interacting with genome to produce mRNA and proteins, resulting in tissue growth and differentiation.
subject: biology

subtopic_names: Iodothyronine and Amino Acid Derived Hormones
summary: Hormones are classified chemically into four groups: peptide, polypeptide, and protein hormones (insulin, glucagon, pituitary hormones); steroids (cortisol, testosterone, estradiol, progesterone); iodothyronines (thyroid hormones T3 and T4); and amino acid derivatives (epinephrine). Chemical classification determines receptor type and mechanism of action in target tissues.
subject: biology

summary: Peptide, polypeptide, and protein hormones include insulin, glucagon, growth hormone, TSH, ACTH, LH, FSH, prolactin, oxytocin, vasopressin, and hypothalamic hormones. These hormones interact with membrane-bound receptors generating second messengers. Their water-soluble nature allows transport in blood and rapid action on target tissues.
subject: biology

summary: Steroid hormones include cortisol, testosterone, estradiol, and progesterone, all derived from cholesterol. These lipophilic hormones interact with intracellular nuclear receptors regulating gene expression. Their lipid-soluble nature allows cell membrane penetration and longer-lasting effects on target tissues.
subject: biology

concept_name: Iodothyronine and Amino Acid Derived Hormones
summary: Iodothyronines are thyroid hormones T3 and T4 containing iodine, derived from tyrosine amino acid. Amino acid-derived hormones include epinephrine (adrenaline) and norepinephrine (noradrenaline) from the adrenal medulla, derived from tyrosine. Iodothyronines interact with intracellular receptors; catecholamines interact with membrane-bound receptors.
subject: biology

concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life
summary: A cell is the basic unit of life, defined as the smallest structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Unicellular organisms consist of a single cell capable of independent existence and performing all life functions. Multicellular organisms are composed of many cells with specialized functions.
subject: biology

summary: Unicellular organisms are single-celled organisms capable of independent existence and performing all essential life functions within one cell. Examples include bacteria, protists, and some algae.
subject: biology

summary: Multicellular organisms are composed of many cells that work together with division of labour and specialized functions. Different cells perform different functions contributing to the overall survival of the organism.
subject: biology

summary: The cell is recognized as the fundamental structural and functional unit of all living organisms, as established by cell theory. No structure smaller than a complete cell can independently sustain life.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life
summary: Matthias Schleiden observed in 1838 that all plants are composed of different kinds of cells that form the tissues of the plant. His work established that cellular organization is fundamental to plant structure.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life
summary: Theodore Schwann observed that all animal cells have a thin outer layer called the plasma membrane and concluded that animal bodies are composed of cells. He also noted that plant cells possess a cell wall, a unique character distinguishing them from animal cells.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life
summary: Rudolf Virchow in 1855 explained that cells divide and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula-e cellula). This contribution completed cell theory by explaining the origin of new cells.
subject: biology

parent_concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life
summary: Modern cell theory has two main postulates: (1) all living organisms are composed of cells and products of cells, and (2) all cells arise from pre-existing cells. These principles form the foundation of biology.
subject: biology

summary: Cells exhibit tremendous variation in size, shape, and structure but share common features including a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and in eukaryotes, a nucleus containing genetic material. The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activity where various chemical reactions occur.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.1 displays different cell shapes including red blood cells, white blood cells, columnar epithelial cells, nerve cells, mesophyll cells, and tracheids.
subject: biology

summary: Cells vary greatly in size, from Mycoplasmas (0.3 μm) to the ostrich egg (largest single cell), and in shape including disc-like, polygonal, columnar, cuboid, thread-like, and irregular forms. Cell shape often correlates with the specific functions they perform.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.1 shows various cell shapes and their functional forms.
subject: biology

summary: The nucleus is a dense membrane-bound structure within the cell that contains chromosomes composed of DNA, the genetic material. It serves as the control centre for cellular activities and heredity.
subject: biology

summary: Cytoplasm is a semi-fluid matrix that fills the cell and occupies the volume between the cell membrane and nucleus. It is the main arena of cellular activities where various chemical reactions occur to maintain the cell in the living state.
subject: biology

summary: Eukaryotic cells contain distinct membrane-bound organelles such as endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi complex, lysosomes, mitochondria, and vacuoles, while prokaryotic cells lack these structures. Non-membrane-bound organelles like ribosomes are found in both cell types.
subject: biology

summary: Prokaryotic cells are represented by bacteria, blue-green algae, mycoplasmas, and PPLO. They are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than eukaryotic cells, lacking a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles except ribosomes. Prokaryotes have a cell wall, cytoplasm, and genetic material that is circular and not enveloped by a nuclear membrane.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.2 shows a typical prokaryotic bacterial cell with flagella, pili, ribosomes, and comparison to PPLO, viruses, and eukaryotic cells.
subject: biology

summary: The prokaryotic cell wall is a chemically complex structure that determines cell shape and provides structural support. It may be surrounded by a glycocalyx, which can be a loose slime layer or a thick capsule, preventing the bacterium from bursting or collapsing.
subject: biology

summary: The prokaryotic plasma membrane is selectively permeable and interacts with the outside world. It is structurally similar to eukaryotic membranes and includes specialized extensions called mesosomes that aid in DNA replication, respiration, and enzymatic activity.
subject: biology

summary: Prokaryotic genetic material consists of a single circular chromosome (genomic DNA) that is naked and not enveloped by a nuclear membrane. Many bacteria also carry small circular DNA molecules called plasmids outside the genomic DNA, which confer unique phenotypic characters such as antibiotic resistance.
subject: biology

summary: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S in size, composed of 50S and 30S subunits, and are associated with the plasma membrane. Several ribosomes can attach to a single mRNA to form polyribosomes that translate mRNA into proteins.
subject: biology

summary: Reserve materials in prokaryotic cells are stored as inclusion bodies in the cytoplasm, not bound by any membrane. These include phosphate granules, cyanophycean granules, glycogen granules, and gas vacuoles found in photosynthetic bacteria.
subject: biology

summary: Mesosomes are specialized membranous structures formed by extensions of the plasma membrane into the cell, appearing as vesicles, tubules, and lamellae. They assist in cell wall formation, DNA replication, respiration, secretion processes, and increase the surface area for enzymatic activity.
subject: biology

summary: Bacterial flagella are thin filamentous extensions from the cell wall that provide motility. Each flagellum is composed of three parts: the filament (longest portion extending outside), hook, and basal body anchored in the cell wall.
subject: biology

summary: Pili are elongated tubular structures made of special proteins found on bacterial surfaces, while fimbriae are small bristle-like fibres. Unlike flagella, they do not play a role in motility but help bacteria attach to rocks in streams and to host tissues.
subject: biology

summary: Eukaryotic cells include those of protists, plants, animals, and fungi, characterized by a membrane-bound nucleus and extensive compartmentalization through membrane-bound organelles. They possess organized chromosomes, complex locomotory and cytoskeletal structures, and vary between plant and animal cells in their presence of cell walls, plastids, and vacuoles.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 shows detailed diagrams of (a) plant cell and (b) animal cell with labeled organelles including nucleus, mitochondria, ER, Golgi apparatus, vacuoles, chloroplasts, centrioles, and other structures.
subject: biology

summary: Eukaryotic cells possess a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, whereas prokaryotic cells lack both. Eukaryotic cells are generally larger and more complex, with extensive compartmentalization of the cytoplasm compared to prokaryotic cells.
subject: biology

summary: Plant cells are distinguished by the presence of a rigid cell wall outside the membrane, plastids (chloroplasts and other forms) for photosynthesis and storage, and a large central vacuole occupying up to 90% of cell volume. Plasmodesmata connect the cytoplasm of neighbouring plant cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3(a) shows plant cell with cell wall, large vacuole, chloroplasts, plasmodesmata, and middle lamella.
subject: biology

summary: Animal cells lack a cell wall, large vacuoles, and plastids present in plant cells. Instead, they contain centrioles organized within a centrosome, which play a crucial role in cell division by forming the spindle apparatus.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3(b) shows animal cell with centrioles, centrosome, microvilli, and absence of cell wall and large vacuoles.
subject: biology

summary: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier composed mainly of lipids and proteins, with the lipid bilayer having polar heads toward the outside and hydrophobic tails toward the inside. It controls the movement of molecules across the cell and is essential for cell growth, division, secretion, and intercellular interactions.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.4 shows the fluid mosaic model of plasma membrane with phospholipid bilayer, cholesterol, peripheral proteins, integral proteins, and sugar molecules.
subject: biology

summary: The plasma membrane is composed of phospholipids arranged in a bilayer with cholesterol interspersed throughout. Proteins, classified as integral (buried in the membrane) or peripheral (on the surface), and carbohydrates are also present, with the protein-to-lipid ratio varying among cell types.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.4 illustrates the composition showing phospholipid bilayer orientation, cholesterol placement, integral and peripheral proteins, and sugar attachments.
subject: biology

summary: The fluid mosaic model, proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972, describes the cell membrane as having a quasi-fluid nature that allows lateral movement of proteins within the lipid bilayer. This fluidity is essential for cellular functions like growth, division, and secretion.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.4 represents the fluid mosaic model showing dynamic movement of proteins within the fluid lipid bilayer.
subject: biology

summary: Membrane fluidity refers to the ability of components within the membrane to move laterally within the bilayer. This property is crucial for maintaining membrane function across varying environmental conditions and for cellular processes such as endocytosis and exocytosis.
subject: biology

summary: Transport across the cell membrane occurs through passive mechanisms (simple diffusion, osmosis) requiring no energy and active transport requiring ATP energy. Polar molecules require carrier proteins for facilitated diffusion, while ions and molecules can be transported against concentration gradients using active transport mechanisms like the Na+/K+ pump.
subject: biology

summary: The cell wall is a non-living rigid structure surrounding the plasma membrane in plants and fungi that provides shape, protection from mechanical damage and infection, and facilitates cell-to-cell interaction. In algae it is composed of cellulose and minerals; in plants it consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, pectins, and proteins.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3(a) shows the plant cell wall as the outermost layer surrounding the cell membrane.
subject: biology

summary: The primary cell wall of young plant cells is capable of growth, gradually diminishing as the cell matures. It provides flexibility while maintaining structural integrity.
subject: biology

summary: The secondary cell wall is formed on the inner side of the primary cell wall as the cell matures, providing additional rigidity and structural support.
subject: biology

summary: The middle lamella is a layer mainly composed of calcium pectate that glues neighbouring plant cells together, serving as an intercellular cement.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3(a) shows the middle lamella between adjacent plant cells.
subject: biology

summary: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that traverse the cell wall and middle lamella, connecting the cytoplasm of neighbouring plant cells and enabling cell-to-cell communication and transport.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3(a) shows plasmodesmata connecting adjacent plant cells through the cell wall.
subject: biology

summary: The endomembrane system comprises interconnected membrane-bound organelles (ER, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and vacuoles) whose functions are coordinated for cellular transport, synthesis, packaging, and digestion. Mitochondria, chloroplasts, and peroxisomes are not part of this system as their functions are not coordinated with these components.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.5 and related figures show the endomembrane system components and their spatial relationships.
subject: biology

summary: The endoplasmic reticulum is a network of membranous tubules that divides the intracellular space into luminal and extra-luminal compartments. Rough ER, studded with ribosomes, is involved in protein synthesis and secretion, while smooth ER is the major site for lipid synthesis including steroid hormones in animal cells.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.5 shows endoplasmic reticulum network with rough ER bearing ribosomes and smooth ER without ribosomes.
subject: biology

summary: The Golgi apparatus comprises stacked, disc-shaped sacs (cisternae) with distinct cis (forming) and trans (maturing) faces. It functions to package materials received from the ER in vesicles for delivery to intracellular targets or secretion, and is a major site for synthesis of glycoproteins and glycolipids.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.6 shows the Golgi apparatus structure with stacked cisternae and the direction of material movement.
subject: biology

summary: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles formed through Golgi apparatus packaging, containing diverse hydrolytic enzymes (lipases, proteases, carbohydrases) optimally active at acidic pH. These enzymes digest carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids within the cell.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 shows lysosomes as small vesicles in the cytoplasm.
subject: biology

summary: Vacuoles are membrane-bound spaces in the cytoplasm bounded by a tonoplast membrane, containing water, sap, excretory products, and other materials. In plant cells, vacuoles occupy up to 90% of cell volume and maintain turgor pressure; in Amoeba, contractile vacuoles regulate osmoregulation and excretion; in protists, food vacuoles engulf food particles.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 shows large central vacuoles in plant cells and their role in maintaining cell structure.
subject: biology

summary: Mitochondria are double membrane-bound organelles with outer smooth membrane and inner membrane forming cristae that increase surface area. They are the sites of aerobic respiration and produce cellular energy as ATP, earning them the title 'power houses' of the cell. They contain their own circular DNA, RNA, ribosomes, and divide by fission.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.7 shows a longitudinal section of mitochondrion with outer membrane, inner membrane, cristae, matrix, and inter-membrane space.
subject: biology

summary: Mitochondria are sausage-shaped or cylindrical structures (0.2-1.0 μm diameter, 1.0-4.1 μm length) with two distinct aqueous compartments. The outer membrane forms a continuous boundary, the inner membrane forms infoldings called cristae toward the matrix, and the inter-membrane space lies between the two membranes.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.7 illustrates mitochondrial structure with all membrane components and internal compartments.
subject: biology

summary: Mitochondria are the sites of aerobic respiration where cellular energy in the form of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is generated. The cristae and matrix contain specific enzymes that catalyze the oxidative phosphorylation process essential for energy production.
subject: biology

summary: The matrix of mitochondria contains a single circular DNA molecule, several RNA molecules, 70S ribosomes, and components required for protein synthesis. This semi-autonomous organization suggests mitochondrial origin from prokaryotic ancestors.
subject: biology

summary: Plastids are large membrane-bound organelles found in plant cells and euglenoides, containing specific pigments. They are classified by pigment type into chloroplasts (green), chromoplasts (yellow, orange, red), and leucoplasts (colourless storage forms).
diagram_description: Fig. 8.8 shows the sectional view of a chloroplast with thylakoids, grana, stroma lamellae, and stroma.
subject: biology

summary: Chloroplasts are double membrane-bound organelles (5-10 μm length, 2-4 μm width) containing chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments responsible for trapping light energy in photosynthesis. They contain stacked thylakoids forming grana, stroma lamellae, stroma, circular DNA, ribosomes, and enzymes for carbohydrate synthesis.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.8 shows chloroplast structure with outer and inner membranes, grana (stacked thylakoids), stroma lamellae, and stroma.
subject: biology

summary: Chromoplasts are plastids containing fat-soluble carotenoid pigments such as carotene and xanthophyll, imparting yellow, orange, or red coloration to plant parts like flowers and fruits.
subject: biology

summary: Leucoplasts are colourless plastids of varied shapes and sizes specialized for nutrient storage. Amyloplasts store carbohydrates (starch), elaioplasts store oils and fats, and aleuroplasts store proteins.
subject: biology

summary: Ribosomes are non-membrane-bound granular structures composed of RNA and proteins, observed as dense particles by electron microscopy. Eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S (60S and 40S subunits), prokaryotic are 70S (50S and 30S subunits), and they are sites of protein synthesis translating mRNA into proteins.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.9 shows ribosomal structure with large and small subunits.
subject: biology

summary: Each ribosome consists of two subunits (larger and smaller) that come together for protein synthesis. In 80S ribosomes, the subunits are 60S and 40S; in 70S ribosomes, they are 50S and 30S. The 'S' represents Svedberg units, indirectly measuring density and size.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.9 illustrates the relative sizes of large and small ribosomal subunits.
subject: biology

summary: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis where they translate mRNA codons into amino acid sequences. Multiple ribosomes attached to a single mRNA form polyribosomes or polysomes that simultaneously synthesize multiple copies of the same protein.
subject: biology

summary: The cytoskeleton is an elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures in the cytoplasm consisting of microtubules, microfilaments, and intermediate filaments. It provides mechanical support, maintains cell shape, and facilitates cell motility.
subject: biology

summary: The cytoskeleton comprises three types of filaments: microtubules (largest), microfilaments (smallest), and intermediate filaments (intermediate size). These structures work together to provide structural integrity, enable cell movement, and support organelle positioning.
subject: biology

summary: Cilia and flagella are hair-like outgrowths of the cell membrane used for locomotion. Cilia are small structures working like oars, while flagella are longer and propel the cell. Both contain an axoneme with nine doublets of peripheral microtubules and a central pair (9+2 array) and emerge from basal bodies.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.10(a) shows electron micrograph of cilium/flagellum cross-section; Fig. 8.10(b) is a diagrammatic representation showing the 9+2 microtubule arrangement with central sheath, radial spokes, and interdoublet bridges.
subject: biology

summary: Cilia are small hair-like structures that work like oars to cause movement of either the cell or surrounding fluid. They emerge from centriole-like structures called basal bodies.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.10 shows ciliary structure and cross-sectional organization.
subject: biology

summary: Flagella are comparatively longer hair-like structures than cilia and are responsible for cell movement. They also emerge from basal bodies and contain the same 9+2 microtubule arrangement, though they are structurally different from prokaryotic flagella.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.10 shows flagellar structure and arrangement.
subject: biology

summary: The axoneme core of cilia and flagella contains nine doublets of peripheral microtubules arranged radially around a central pair (9+2 array). The central tubules are connected by bridges and enclosed by a central sheath linked to peripheral doublets by nine radial spokes, while peripheral doublets are interconnected by linkers, enabling coordinated movement.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.10(b) illustrates the 9+2 array with central microtubules, central sheath, radial spokes, peripheral doublets, and interdoublet bridges.
subject: biology

summary: Centrosome is an organelle containing two cylindrical centrioles perpendicular to each other, surrounded by amorphous pericentriolar material. Each centriole has a cartwheel organization of nine evenly-spaced peripheral fibrils (triplets). Centrosomes form basal bodies for cilia and flagella, and in animal cells, generate spindle apparatus during cell division.
subject: biology

summary: Centrioles are cylindrical structures made of nine evenly-spaced peripheral fibrils of tubulin protein, with each fibril being a triplet. Adjacent triplets are linked, and the hub at the proximal region is connected to peripheral triplets by radial spokes made of protein.
subject: biology

summary: Centrioles form the basal body of cilia and flagella and in animal cells form spindle fibres that give rise to the spindle apparatus during cell division. The centrosome serves as the main microtubule organizing centre in animal cells.
subject: biology

summary: The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle that serves as the control centre of the cell, containing genetic material organized as chromatin and nucleoli. The nuclear envelope consists of two parallel membranes with nuclear pores allowing communication between nucleus and cytoplasm. The nucleoplasm contains the chromatin network and nucleoli.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.11 shows nucleus structure with nuclear envelope, nucleoplasm, nucleolus, nuclear pores, and chromatin.
subject: biology

summary: The nuclear envelope consists of two parallel membranes separated by a perinuclear space (10-50 nm), forming a barrier between nucleus and cytoplasm. The outer membrane is continuous with the endoplasmic reticulum and bears ribosomes. Nuclear pores are formed by fusion of the two membranes, allowing passage of RNA and protein molecules in both directions.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.11 shows the double membrane structure of the nuclear envelope and nuclear pores.
subject: biology

summary: The nucleolus is a spherical, non-membrane-bound structure within the nucleoplasm that is the site of active ribosomal RNA (rRNA) synthesis. Cells actively involved in protein synthesis have larger and more numerous nucleoli.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.11 shows the nucleolus as a distinct spherical structure within the nucleus.
subject: biology

summary: Chromatin in the interphase nucleus consists of loosely organized nucleoprotein fibres containing DNA, basic proteins called histones, non-histone proteins, and RNA. During cell division, chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes. A single human cell contains approximately two metres of DNA distributed among 46 chromosomes.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.11 shows chromatin as the nuclear material organized as a network in interphase.
subject: biology

summary: Chromosomes are condensed forms of chromatin visible only during cell division, each consisting of two chromatids held together by a centromere. The centromere has disc-shaped kinetochores on its sides. Chromosomes are classified into four types based on centromere position.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.12 shows a chromosome with kinetochore; Fig. 8.13 shows the four types of chromosomes (metacentric, sub-metacentric, acrocentric, telocentric) based on centromere position.
subject: biology

summary: Each visible chromosome during cell division consists of two identical sister chromatids joined at a primary constriction called the centromere. The centromere holds the chromatids together and serves as the attachment point for spindle fibres during cell division.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.12 shows chromosome structure with two chromatids, centromere, and kinetochores.
subject: biology

summary: The centromere is the primary constriction on each chromosome where two chromatids are held together. Disc-shaped kinetochore structures are present on the sides of the centromere, serving as attachment sites for spindle fibres.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.12 shows the centromere region with kinetochores.
subject: biology

summary: Chromosomes are classified into four types based on centromere position: metacentric (centromere at middle, equal arms), sub-metacentric (centromere slightly off-centre), acrocentric (centromere near end, very unequal arms), and telocentric (terminal centromere). Some chromosomes have secondary constrictions at constant locations forming satellites.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.13 displays all four chromosome types showing the position of centromere and resulting arm lengths.
subject: biology

summary: Microbodies are small membrane-bound vesicles present in both plant and animal cells, containing various enzymes. They perform specialized metabolic functions including fatty acid oxidation and hydrogen peroxide breakdown.
diagram_description: Fig. 8.3 shows peroxisomes (a type of microbody) in both plant and animal cells.
subject: biology

summary: G.N. Ramachandran (1922-2001) was an outstanding scientist who discovered the triple helical structure of collagen and developed the Ramachandran plot for analysing allowed protein conformations. His work on the alpha-helix and beta-sheet structures established the foundation for understanding protein structure.
subject: biology

summary: Chemical analysis of living tissues involves grinding tissue in trichloroacetic acid to separate acid-soluble compounds (small molecules) from acid-insoluble compounds (macromolecules). Elemental analysis reveals that living organisms contain the same elements as non-living matter, but with a higher relative abundance of carbon and hydrogen. The ash remaining after burning tissue contains inorganic elements and compounds essential for life.

builds_upon.concept_name: Cell as Basic Unit of Life

subject: biology

summary: Living organisms contain the same elements as earth's crust (hydrogen, carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur, sodium, calcium, magnesium, silicon), but the relative abundance of carbon and hydrogen is significantly higher in living tissues. This difference in elemental proportion is a key characteristic distinguishing living from non-living matter.
diagram_description: Table 9.1 compares the percentage weight of elements (H, C, O, N, S, Na, Ca, Mg, Si) in earth's crust versus the human body.
subject: biology

summary: When living tissue is ground in trichloroacetic acid and strained through cheesecloth, two fractions are obtained: the acid-soluble pool (filtrate) containing thousands of small organic compounds, and the acid-insoluble fraction (retentate) containing macromolecules like proteins, nucleic acids, and polysaccharides. The acid-soluble fraction represents the cytoplasmic composition, while the acid-insoluble fraction comprises macromolecules from cytoplasm and organelles.
subject: biology

summary: All carbon compounds extracted from living tissues are classified as biomolecules. Living organisms also contain inorganic elements and compounds (revealed by ash content after burning) such as sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, chloride, phosphate, and sulphate. The distinction between wet weight (fresh tissue with water) and dry weight (after water evaporation) helps determine the proportion of water and dissolved substances in living tissue.
diagram_description: Table 9.2 lists representative inorganic constituents including ions (Na+, K+, Ca++, Mg++) and compounds (H2O, NaCl, CaCO3, phosphates, sulphates).
subject: biology

summary: Metabolites are biomolecules with identifiable roles in cellular function and metabolism. They are classified into primary metabolites (essential for normal physiological processes, found in all organisms) and secondary metabolites (additional compounds in plants, fungi, and microorganisms with specialized functions or ecological importance).
subject: biology

summary: Primary metabolites include amino acids, monosaccharides, disaccharides, fatty acids, glycerol, nucleotides, and nucleosides. These compounds are found in all living organisms (including animal tissues) and serve known roles in nutrition, energy production, and structural functions.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows structures of primary metabolites including sugars (glucose, ribose), amino acids (glycine, alanine, serine), fatty acids, and nucleotides.
subject: biology

summary: Secondary metabolites are compounds found abundantly in plants, fungi, and microorganisms but not typically in animal tissues. Examples include alkaloids (morphine, codeine), terpenoids, essential oils, pigments (carotenoids, anthocyanins), toxins, lectins, drugs (vinblastin, curcumin), and polymeric substances (rubber, gums, cellulose). Many have ecological or commercial importance, though their exact roles in the host organism are not always understood.
diagram_description: Table 9.3 lists secondary metabolites by category: pigments, alkaloids, terpenoids, essential oils, toxins, lectins, drugs, and polymeric substances.
subject: biology

summary: Biomolecules are classified by molecular weight into two types: micromolecules (or simply biomolecules) with weights less than 1,000 daltons, found in the acid-soluble fraction, and macromolecules (biomacromolecules) with weights above 10,000 daltons, found in the acid-insoluble fraction. This classification reflects the structural and functional differences between small metabolic compounds and large polymeric molecules.
subject: biology

summary: Micromolecules are organic compounds with molecular weights ranging from 18 to approximately 800 daltons found in the acid-soluble fraction of living tissues. These include amino acids, monosaccharides, disaccharides, fatty acids, nucleotides, and nucleosides that represent the cytoplasmic composition of cells.
subject: biology

summary: Biomacromolecules are large polymeric organic compounds with molecular weights of 10,000 daltons or above found in the acid-insoluble fraction. The four main classes are proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides, and lipids (which, though smaller, associate with membranes and sediment with the macromolecular fraction). Together with micromolecules, they represent the complete chemical composition of living organisms.
subject: biology

summary: Proteins are linear polymers of amino acids linked by peptide bonds. As there are 20 types of amino acids, each protein is a heteropolymer with a unique amino acid sequence (primary structure) that determines its three-dimensional shape and function. Proteins carry out diverse functions including enzyme catalysis, transport, immunity, hormone signaling, and structural support.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 and Figure 9.3 show amino acid structures and peptide bond formation in proteins, including alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet secondary structures.
subject: biology

summary: Amino acids are organic compounds containing an amino group (-NH2) and an acidic carboxyl group (-COOH) bonded to the same carbon (the α-carbon), making them substituted methanes. Each amino acid has a variable R group that determines its chemical and physical properties. Only 20 types of amino acids occur in proteins, including glycine (R=H), alanine (R=CH3), and serine (R=CH2OH).
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the general structure of amino acids with H, carboxyl group, amino group, and R group bonded to the α-carbon, plus specific examples: glycine, alanine, and serine.
subject: biology

summary: Amino acids are classified as acidic (e.g., glutamic acid), basic (e.g., lysine), or neutral (e.g., valine) based on the number of amino and carboxyl groups. Some are aromatic (e.g., tyrosine, phenylalanine, tryptophan) containing aromatic ring structures. The ionizable -NH2 and -COOH groups cause amino acids to change structure at different pH values, forming a zwitterionic form (dipolar ion) at physiological pH where both groups are ionized.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the zwitterionic form (B) of amino acids with ionized amino and carboxyl groups at different pH conditions (forms A, B, C).
subject: biology

summary: Proteins are polypeptides—long chains of amino acids linked together by peptide bonds between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of the next. Because proteins contain 20 different amino acids, they are heteropolymers rather than homopolymers. Essential amino acids must be obtained through diet, while non-essential amino acids can be synthesized by the body.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3(a) shows the primary structure of proteins as a polypeptide chain with sequential amino acids linked by peptide bonds.
subject: biology

summary: Proteins exhibit four levels of hierarchical structure: primary (amino acid sequence), secondary (local folding into α-helices and β-pleated sheets), tertiary (three-dimensional folding with hydrogen and disulphide bonds), and quaternary (assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits). Each level determines the protein's shape and biological function, with tertiary and quaternary structures being essential for protein activity.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3 illustrates all four levels: (a) primary structure as a linear sequence, (b) secondary structure showing α-helix and β-pleated sheet, (c) tertiary structure with hydrogen and disulphide bonds forming a compact 3D shape, and (d) quaternary structure with multiple subunits assembled together.
subject: biology

summary: Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids in a protein, read from the N-terminal amino acid (the first amino acid with a free -NH2 group) to the C-terminal amino acid (the last with a free -COOH group). This sequence encodes the positional information that determines all higher levels of protein structure and ultimately the protein's function.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3(a) shows a polypeptide chain with multiple amino acids linked sequentially, illustrating the primary structure.
subject: biology

summary: Secondary structure refers to local folding patterns within the protein chain, primarily α-helices (spiral structures similar to a revolving staircase) and β-pleated sheets (extended, zigzag arrangements). In proteins, only right-handed helices are observed. These structures are stabilized by hydrogen bonds between the backbone amide groups, and other regions of the protein adopt different secondary structures.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3(b) depicts secondary structures as an α-helix (coiled spring-like) and β-pleated sheet (extended zigzag pattern) formed by regular hydrogen bonding.
subject: biology

summary: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a protein, resulting from the folding of the polypeptide chain upon itself like a hollow woolen ball. This folding is stabilized by hydrogen bonds and disulphide bonds between amino acid R groups. The tertiary structure creates crevices and pockets including the active site, which is absolutely necessary for the protein's biological activities.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3(c) shows tertiary structure as a compact folded protein with hydrogen and disulphide bonds holding the structure together, creating a three-dimensional shape.
subject: biology

summary: Quaternary structure describes how multiple folded polypeptide subunits (or chains) are arranged relative to each other in proteins composed of more than one polypeptide. Examples include the arrangement of subunits in linear, cubic, or plate-like configurations. Adult human hemoglobin exemplifies this, consisting of 4 subunits: two identical α-type and two identical β-type subunits.
diagram_description: Figure 9.3(d) shows quaternary structure as multiple polypeptide subunits assembled together in a specific 3D arrangement, illustrated with hemoglobin-like structure.
subject: biology

summary: Lipids are generally water-insoluble organic compounds composed of glycerol and fatty acids. They include simple fatty acids, glycerides (fats and oils based on melting point), phospholipids (found in cell membranes), and cholesterol. Lipids are considered part of the macromolecular fraction because they associate with membranes as vesicles rather than dissolving in water.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 and a comprehensive diagram show structures of fatty acids (palmitic acid), glycerol, triglycerides, phospholipids (lecithin), and cholesterol.
subject: biology

summary: Lipids include simple fatty acids (a carboxyl group attached to an R group of variable length), glycerol (trihydroxy propane), phospholipids (fatty acids and phosphorylated organic compounds esterified to glycerol, found in cell membranes; lecithin is an example), and cholesterol (a complex lipid in neural tissues). Complex polysaccharide-containing lipids are also found, especially in neural tissue.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows chemical structures of palmitic acid, glycerol, triglycerides, phospholipids (lecithin with phosphate group), and cholesterol.
subject: biology

summary: Fatty acids are classified as saturated (without C=C double bonds) or unsaturated (with one or more C=C double bonds). Palmitic acid with 16 carbons and arachidonic acid with 20 carbons are examples. Unsaturated fatty acids like those in gingelly oil have lower melting points and remain liquid in winter, whereas saturated fats have higher melting points.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the structural difference between saturated fatty acids (straight-chain structure) and unsaturated fatty acids (with C=C double bonds).
subject: biology

summary: Glycerides are lipids in which fatty acids are esterified (bonded) to glycerol. They are classified as monoglycerides (one fatty acid), diglycerides (two fatty acids), or triglycerides (three fatty acids). Glycerides are called fats if they have high melting points (solid at room temperature) or oils if they have low melting points (liquid at room temperature).
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the structure of glycerol with three hydroxyl groups and a triglyceride formed by esterification of three fatty acids (R1, R2, R3) to glycerol.
subject: biology

summary: Nucleic acids are polynucleotides that serve as genetic material in living organisms. They consist of nucleotides as building blocks, each containing three chemically distinct components: a nitrogenous base, a monosaccharide sugar, and a phosphate group. DNA contains deoxyribose sugar while RNA contains ribose sugar, and both function to carry and transmit hereditary information.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows nucleotide components including nitrogen bases (adenine purine, uracil pyrimidine), nucleosides (adenosine, uridine), and nucleotides (adenylic acid).
subject: biology

summary: A nucleotide is the basic building block of nucleic acids, consisting of three chemically distinct components: a heterocyclic nitrogenous base, a monosaccharide sugar (either ribose or 2'-deoxyribose), and a phosphoric acid or phosphate group. The phosphate group is esterified to the sugar, forming the nucleotide.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the structure of a nucleotide (adenylic acid) with adenine base, sugar ring, and phosphate group (PO4) bonded together.
subject: biology

summary: Nitrogenous bases in nucleic acids are heterocyclic compounds classified as purines (adenine and guanine, which are substituted purines with a double-ring structure) or pyrimidines (cytosine, uracil, and thymine, which have a single-ring structure). Adenine and guanine occur in both DNA and RNA, cytosine occurs in both, thymine occurs only in DNA, and uracil occurs only in RNA.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows the chemical structures of purine bases (adenine) and pyrimidine bases (uracil) with their characteristic ring structures.
subject: biology

summary: A nucleoside is formed when a nitrogenous base is attached to a monosaccharide sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) without a phosphate group. Examples include adenosine, guanosine, thymidine, uridine, and cytidine. Nucleosides are intermediates in nucleotide synthesis and can be found free in cells.
diagram_description: Figure 9.1 shows nucleoside structures (adenosine and uridine) with nitrogen base attached directly to the sugar ring, without phosphate groups.
subject: biology

summary: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a nucleic acid containing nucleotides with deoxyribose sugar. RNA (ribonucleic acid) contains nucleotides with ribose sugar. Both DNA and RNA consist exclusively of nucleotides linked together and serve as genetic material, carrying hereditary information that is passed from parental generation to progeny.
subject: biology

summary: Polysaccharides are long chains of monosaccharide building blocks linked together, forming thread-like polymers. They serve as structural components (cellulose in plant cell walls, chitin in arthropod exoskeletons) and energy storage molecules (starch in plants, glycogen in animals). Polysaccharides can be homopolymers (single type of monosaccharide) or contain multiple types and chemically modified sugars.
diagram_description: Figure 9.2 shows a glycogen structure with branched chains of monosaccharides, illustrating the reducing end, non-reducing end, and branch points.
subject: biology

summary: Polysaccharide chains have a reducing end (right end) and a non-reducing end (left end) and may contain branches. Many polysaccharides are homopolymers consisting of only one type of monosaccharide. The structure and branching pattern determine their physical properties and biological functions.
diagram_description: Figure 9.2 illustrates polysaccharide chain structure with labeled reducing end, non-reducing end, and branch point (CH2 group) in a glycogen molecule.
subject: biology

summary: Polysaccharides are classified primarily by their monosaccharide composition: cellulose consists of glucose only (homopolymer), starch is a glucose polymer serving as an energy store in plants, glycogen is the animal equivalent energy storage, and inulin is a fructose polymer. Many are homopolymers, while complex polysaccharides contain amino-sugars and chemically modified sugars.
subject: biology

summary: Starch is a plant polysaccharide serving as an energy storage molecule that forms helical secondary structures capable of holding iodine molecules in the helical portion, producing a blue color (starch-I2 complex). Glycogen is the animal equivalent, also forming branched structures. Both store glucose for energy mobilization during cellular respiration.
diagram_description: Figure 9.2 shows glycogen structure with branched glucose chains, helical organization, and branch points. Starch also forms helices distinguishing it from cellulose.
subject: biology

summary: Cellulose is a homopolymer consisting exclusively of glucose units, forming the structural component of plant cell walls. Unlike starch, cellulose does not form complex helical structures and therefore cannot hold iodine. Paper made from plant pulp and cotton fiber consists primarily of cellulose.
subject: biology

summary: Complex polysaccharides contain amino-sugars (like glucosamine) and chemically modified sugars (like N-acetyl galactosamine) as building blocks in addition to standard monosaccharides. Chitin, found in arthropod exoskeletons, is an example of a complex homopolymer. These modifications provide structural rigidity and specialized functions.
subject: biology
